    钦定四库全书　　　　　子部十二
    　　剧谈录　　　　　　　　小说家【异闻之属】提要
    　　【臣】等谨案剧谈録三卷唐康骈撰王定保摭言作唐骈葢传写之讹故也唐书艺文志作康軿以其字驾言证之二字义皆相合未详孰是然诸书引之皆作骈疑亦唐志误也骈池阳人干符四年登进士第官至崇文馆校书是书成于干寕二年皆记天寳以来事亦间以议论附之凡四十条今以太平广记勘之一一相合非当时全部収入即后人从广记抄合也此本末有临安府陈道人书籍舗刋行字葢犹影抄宋本如潘将军一条注中疑为潘鹘硉字今本剑侠传从广记剽掇此条讹为潘碎遂不可觧知此本为善矣其中载元微之年老擢第执贽谒
    　　李贺一条古夫于亭杂録辨之曰案元擢第既非迟暮于贺亦称前辈讵容执防造门反轻薄小説之不根如此其论最当然稗官所述半出传闻真伪互陈其风自古未可全以为据亦未可全以为诬在读者考证其得失耳不以是废此一家也乾隆四十一年十月恭校上 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, a massive collection of Chinese literature compiled during the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - Refers to the 'Zi' (Miscellaneous) section of the Siku Quanshu, which is divided into twelve categories.
    • 剧谈录 - Title of the text, which can be translated as 'Records of Grand Talk' or 'Collected Conversations.'
    • 小说家类【异闻之属】 - Indicates that the text belongs to the 'Xiaoshuo' (Novel) category, specifically under the subcategory of 'Yiwen' (Strange Tales).
    • 提要 - A preface or summary that provides an overview of the text.
    • 干符四年 - The year 877 AD, according to the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang.
    • 崇文馆校书郎 - A position in the imperial library, responsible for cataloging and preserving books.
    • 干寕二年 - The year 895 AD, according to the reign of Emperor Xizong of Tang.
    • 天寳以来 - Refers to the period starting from the Tianbao era (742-756 AD) of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 太平广记 - A comprehensive collection of strange tales and anecdotes compiled during the Song dynasty.
    • 临安府 - The name of the capital city of the Southern Song dynasty, now known as Hangzhou.
    • 陈道人 - A person or publisher associated with the printing of the text.
    • 书籍舗 - A bookstore or publishing house.
    • 恭校上 - A formal expression indicating that the text has been carefully reviewed and submitted.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • The Siku Quanshu is one of the most important literary compilations in Chinese history, commissioned by the Qing dynasty to preserve and organize Chinese literature.
    • The 'Zi' section includes a wide range of texts, from philosophical treatises to historical records and novels.
    • The text is a collection of conversations and anecdotes, often focusing on historical events and notable figures.
    • This category includes texts that are considered to be fictional or based on hearsay, often containing supernatural or extraordinary elements.
    • The preface or summary provides context and background information about the text.
    • The year 877 AD marks the beginning of the An Lushan Rebellion, a significant event in Tang dynasty history.
    • This position was highly respected and involved in the preservation of cultural heritage.
    • The year 895 AD marks the end of the Tang dynasty and the beginning of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.
    • The Tianbao era is known for its cultural and economic prosperity, as well as the beginning of the decline of the Tang dynasty.
    • This collection is a valuable source for understanding Chinese folklore and literature.
    • The capital of the Southern Song dynasty, known for its cultural and economic significance.
    • The name suggests a Daoist figure or a publisher associated with the Daoist tradition.
    • The term indicates a commercial enterprise involved in the production and distribution of books.
    • This formal expression indicates that the text has been carefully reviewed and approved by the imperial authorities.

    Proper Nouns

    • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Qinding Siku Quanshu' (Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries).
    • 子部十二 - The 'Zi' (Miscellaneous) section of the Siku Quanshu.
    • 剧谈录 - 'Records of Grand Talk' or 'Collected Conversations.'
    • 小说家类【异闻之属】 - The 'Xiaoshuo' (Novel) category, specifically under the subcategory of 'Yiwen' (Strange Tales).
    • 提要 - Preface or summary.
    • 干符四年 - 877 AD.
    • 崇文馆校书郎 - A position in the imperial library.
    • 干寕二年 - 895 AD.
    • 天寳以来 - From the Tianbao era (742-756 AD).
    • 太平广记 - 'Taiping Guangji' (Comprehensive Records of the Taiping Era).
    • 临安府 - The capital city of the Southern Song dynasty (now Hangzhou).
    • 陈道人 - A person or publisher associated with the printing of the text.
    • 书籍舗 - A bookstore or publishing house.
    • 恭校上 - A formal expression indicating careful review and submission.

    Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
    Section Twelve: Miscellaneous
    Records of Grand Talk
    Category: Novels (Strange Tales)
    Preface

    We respectfully submit that Records of Grand Talk is a three-volume work by Tang Dynasty's Kang Bian. Wang Dingbao's Miscellaneous Notes records it as "Tang Bian," likely a transcription error. The Tang Book's Catalogue of Arts and Literature lists it as "Kang Peng," and the two characters align in meaning, though it is unclear which is correct. However, all references to it are as "Bian," suggesting that the Tang Book may have been in error. Kang Bian was from Chiyang, passed the imperial examination in the fourth year of Ganfu (877 AD), and held the position of Shangshu Lang in the Imperial Library. This work was completed in the second year of Ganning (895 AD), and it records events from the Tianbao era (742-756 AD) and beyond, occasionally interspersing commentary. It consists of forty entries. When cross-referenced with the Taiping Guangji, they correspond perfectly, indicating that either the entire work was included at the time or it was later compiled from the Taiping Guangji. This edition, printed by Chen Dao Ren's bookstore in Lin'an Prefecture, is likely a copy of the Song dynasty manuscript. For example, in the entry about General Pan, the note suggests that the name might be "Pan Gu Lu," but in the current version of Swordsmen's Tales, it is incorrectly written as "Pan Xi Su," making it difficult to understand. This shows that this edition is superior. Among its contents, there is an entry about Yuan Zhen, who, in his old age, passed the examination and paid homage to Li He. The Ancient Tales from the Yu Hut Pavilion notes: "Yuan Zhen's passing the examination was not in his old age, and he was not Li He's senior. How could he, in his old age, go to Li He's door and be considered frivolous? Such criticism is unfounded. The tales of the Bian Office are half based on hearsay, and their authenticity and falsehood are mixed. This has been the case since ancient times, and they cannot be entirely taken as true or false. Readers must examine their merits and faults. This does not mean that the entire work should be discarded.** [1]

    总纂官【臣】纪昀【臣】陆锡熊【臣】孙士毅
    　　总　校　官【臣】陆　费　墀

    　　钦定四库全书卷　　　　子部十二
    　　剧谈录目录　　　　　　小説家二【异闻之属】卷上

    　　郭鄩见穷
    　　裴晋公天津桥遇老人
    　　狄惟谦请雨
    　　王侍中题诗
    　　道流相夏侯谯公
    　　华山龙移湫
    　　田膨郎偷玉枕
    　　洛中大水
    　　李朱崖知白令公

    　　刘相国宅

    　　张季宏逢恶新妇 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 總纂官【臣】紀昀【臣】陸錫熊【臣】孫士毅 - Refers to the chief editors of the text; 【臣】 is a humble self-reference used by officials.
    • 總校官【臣】陸費墀 - Refers to the chief proofreader of the text; 【臣】 is a humble self-reference.
    • 欽定四庫全書 - Imperial decree; part of the Imperial Library commissioned by the Qianlong Emperor.
    • 子部十二 - One of the six major categories of the Imperial Library; includes various types of literature.
    • 小説家類二【異聞之屬】 - Category of novels or stories; includes stories of strange occurrences.
    • 卷上 - Indicates the first volume of the text.
    • 宣宗夜召翰林學士 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang summoned the Hanlin scholars at night.
    • 劉平見安祿山魑魅 - Liu Ping saw evil spirits or monsters associated with An Lushan.
    • 王鲔活崔相公歌妓 - Wang Wei saved a singer or dancer who was a high-ranking official.
    • 御史灘 - Refers to the rapids or shallow water.
    • 渾令公李西平朱泚云梯 - 渾令公 and 李西平 are titles or names of officials; 朱泚 is a historical figure; 云梯 refers to a siege ladder.
    • 潘將軍失珠 - 潘將軍 is a person's name; 失珠 means lost a pearl.
    • 李邺侯救窦庭芝 - 李邺侯 is a person's name; 窦庭芝 is another person's name.
    • 續坤蹶馬 - 續坤 and 蹶馬 are unclear and may be specific terms or names that require further research.
    • 龍待诏相笏 - 龍待诏 is a title; 相笏 refers to a ceremonial tablet used by officials.
    • 孟才人善歌 - 孟才人 is a person's name; 善歌 means good at singing.
    • 袁相雪換金縣令 - 袁相 is a person's name; 雪 means snow; 換金縣令 means exchanged for a gold county magistrate.
    • 郭鄩見窮 - 郭鄩 is a person's name; 窮 means poor ghosts.
    • 裴晋公天津橋遇老人 - 裴晋公 is a person's name; 天津橋 is a bridge in Luoyang.
    • 狄惟謙請雨 - 狄惟謙 is a person's name; 請雨 means asked for rain.
    • 王侍中題詩 - 王侍中 is a title; 題詩 means wrote a poem.
    • 道流相夏侯谯公 - 道流 refers to a Daoist; 夏侯谯公 is a person's name.
    • 華山龍移湫 - 華山 refers to Mount Hua; 龍 means dragon; 移湫 means moved the spring.
    • 田膨郎偷玉枕 - 田膨郎 is a person's name; 偷玉枕 means stole a jade pillow.
    • 洛中大水 - 洛中 refers to Luoyang; 大水 means flood.
    • 李朱崖知白令公 - 李朱崖 and 知白令公 are unclear and may be specific terms or names that require further research.
    • 卷下 - Indicates the second volume of the text.
    • 劉相國宅 - 劉相國 is a person's name; 宅 means house.
    • 李相國宅 - 李相國 is a person's name; 宅 means house.
    • 慈恩寺牡丹 - 慈恩寺 refers to the Cien Temple; 牡丹 means peony.
    • 管萬敵遇壯士 - 管萬敵 is a person's name; 遇壯士 means encountered a strong man.
    • 張季宏逢惡新婦 - 張季宏 is a person's name; 逢惡新婦 means encountered a bad new wife.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 欽定四庫全書 - Massive collection of books commissioned by the Qianlong Emperor; one of the most important cultural achievements of the Qing dynasty.
    • 子部十二 - One of the six major categories of the Imperial Library; includes various types of literature.
    • 小説家類二【異聞之屬】 - Category includes stories of strange occurrences, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 宣宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a famous emperor of the Tang dynasty.
    • 安祿山 - Historical figure; general and later rebel during the Tang dynasty.
    • 崔相公 - High-ranking official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 渾令公 - Title or name of an official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 李西平 - Title or name of an official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 朱泚 - Historical figure; general and later rebel during the Tang dynasty.
    • 李邺侯 - Historical figure; statesman and scholar during the Tang dynasty.
    • 窦庭芝 - Historical figure; poet and official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 裴晋公 - Historical figure; statesman and military leader during the Tang dynasty.
    • 天津橋 - Famous bridge in Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 狄惟謙 - Historical figure; official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 王侍中 - Title referring to an official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 夏侯谯公 - Historical figure; official during the Tang dynasty.
    • 華山 - Famous mountain in China; considered one of the Five Great Mountains.
    • 慈恩寺 - Famous temple in Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 牡丹 - Famous flower in China; often associated with beauty and wealth.
    • 張季宏 - Historical figure; official during the Tang dynasty.

    Translation:

    Chief Editor Officials:

    • Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Ji Yun
    • Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Lu Xi Xiong
    • Minister of the Imperial Library, [Official] Sun Shi Yi

    Chief Proofreader Official:

    • [Official] Lu Fei Chi

    Imperial Decree Collection, Volume:

    • Subcategory Twelve: Novel Category Two [Strange Occurrences]

    Book I:

    Emperor Xuanzong's Night Summoning of Hanlin Scholars
    Liu Ping's Encounter with Evil Spirits of An Lushan
    Wang Wei Saves a Singer or Dancer Who Was a High-Ranking Official
    The Rapids of the Censor
    The Cloud Ladder of Generals Hun and Li Xi Ping Against Zhu Ci
    General Pan Loses a Pearl
    Li Yehou Rescues Duan Tingzhi
    Continued: The Horse of Xukun
    Dragon Attendant Advises with a Ceremonial Tablet
    Meng Cairen Skilled in Singing
    Yuan Xiang Exchanges Snow for a Golden County Magistrate
    Guo Xun Sees Poor Ghosts
    Pei Jingong Meets an Elder at Tianjin Bridge
    Di Weiqian Asks for Rain
    Wang Shizhong Writes a Poem
    A Daoist Advises Xiahou Qiaogong
    The Dragon Moves the Spring of Mount Hua
    Tian Penglang Steals a Jade Pillow
    The Great Flood in Luoyang
    Li Zhuyan Knows White Lingong

    Book II:

    The House of Prime Minister Liu
    The House of Prime Minister Li
    Peony at Cien Temple
    Guan Wandi Meets a Strong Man
    Zhang Jihong Encounters a Bad New Wife [2]

    玉蘂院眞人降
    　　宰相布施
    　　崔道枢食井鱼
    　　洛中豪士
    　　凤翔府举兵讨贼
    　　老君庙画
    　　白傅乗舟
    　　严史君遇终南山者
    　　韦颛枭鸣
    　　命相日雨雹
    　　李生见神物遗酒
    　　説方士
    　　广谪仙怨词
    　　含元殿
    　　曲江
    　　眞身
    　　元相国谒李贺

    　　钦定四库全书
    　　剧谈录卷上　　　　　　　唐　康骈　撰宣宗夜召翰林学士
    　　宣宗皇帝圣政钦明光宅天下常欲刑清俗富有宵衣旰食之懐仄席竚贤每如不及令狐相国自吴兴郡守授司郎中未居内署初与学士对便以为有宰辅之才一夕于禁林寓直忽有中使来召行百余歩至于便殿遣内人秉烛之引于御榻之前上自宣令坐问卿来从江表见彼中甿庶安否廉察郡守字人求瘼之道如何朕常思四海之大九州之广虽明君不能自理常须良弼贤佐迩来窃窥朝廷皆未覩其忠赤相国降堦俯伏曰圣意如此微臣便合得罪上曰卿才为翰林学士所职者朕之丝纶向来之言本不相 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 玉蘂院眞人降 - Literally 'the True Person of Jade Rhododendron Hall descends,' likely referring to a spiritual or divine figure appearing in a specific location.
    • 宰相布施 - Means 'the chancellor makes offerings,' possibly referring to a charitable act by a high-ranking official.
    • 崔道枢食井魚 - Refers to 'Cui Daochu eats well water fish,' likely a reference to a specific event or anecdote involving Cui Daochu.
    • 洛中豪士 - Refers to 'the strong men of Luoyang,' indicating influential or powerful individuals in the city.
    • 鳳翔府舉兵討賊 - Refers to 'the Fengxiang Prefecture raises an army to fight the rebels,' referring to a military action against rebels.
    • 老君廟畫 - Refers to 'the painting at the Laojun Temple,' likely a reference to a specific artwork or depiction in a temple dedicated to Laozi.
    • 白傅乗舟 - Refers to 'Bai Fu rides a boat,' possibly referring to a specific event or anecdote involving Bai Fu.
    • 嚴史君遇終南山者 - Refers to 'Yan Shijun meets a recluse in Mount Zhongnan,' likely a reference to an encounter with a hermit or sage.
    • 韋顓梟鳴 - Refers to 'Wei Zhuang's hawk cries,' possibly a metaphorical or symbolic reference.
    • 命相日雨雹 - Refers to 'On the day of destiny, hail falls,' likely a reference to a specific event or omen.
    • 李生見神物遺酒 - Refers to 'Li Sheng sees a divine being leave wine,' likely a reference to a supernatural encounter.
    • 説方士 - Refers to 'talking about the Daoist,' likely a reference to discussions or stories about Daoist practitioners.
    • 廣谪仙怨詞 - Refers to 'Expanding the poem of the exiled immortal's lament,' likely a reference to a specific literary work or theme.
    • 含元殿 - Refers to 'The Hall of Containing the Beginning,' a specific imperial palace building.
    • 曲江 - Refers to 'Qujiang,' a famous scenic area in Chang'an, often associated with poetry and literature.
    • 眞身 - Refers to 'True body,' likely referring to a sacred or divine form.
    • 元相國谒李贺 - Refers to 'Yuan Xiangguo visits Li He,' likely a reference to a specific historical or literary event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玉蘂院 - Refers to a specific temple or hall, possibly dedicated to a deity or spiritual figure.
    • 眞人 - Refers to a 'True Person,' a term used in Daoism to describe a spiritual or divine being.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the chancellor or prime minister, a high-ranking official in the imperial government.
    • 崔道枢 - A historical figure, possibly a Daoist or scholar.
    • 洛中 - Refers to Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 鳳翔府 - Refers to the Fengxiang Prefecture, a specific administrative region.
    • 老君廟 - Refers to a temple dedicated to Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
    • 白傅 - Likely refers to Bai Juyi, a famous Tang dynasty poet.
    • 嚴史君 - Refers to Yan Shijun, possibly a historical figure or official.
    • 終南山 - Refers to Mount Zhongnan, a famous mountain associated with Daoism and hermits.
    • 韋顓 - Refers to Wei Zhuang, a historical figure, possibly a poet or official.
    • 命相 - Refers to the concept of fate or destiny, often associated with astrology or divination.
    • 李生 - Refers to Li Sheng, possibly a historical figure or literary character.
    • 方士 - Refers to Daoist practitioners or alchemists.
    • 谪仙 - Refers to the 'exiled immortal,' a term often used to describe Li Bai, a famous Tang dynasty poet.
    • 含元殿 - Refers to a specific imperial palace building in Chang'an.
    • 曲江 - Refers to a famous scenic area in Chang'an, often associated with poetry and literature.
    • 眞身 - Refers to a sacred or divine form, often used in religious or spiritual contexts.
    • 元相國 - Refers to Yuan Xiangguo, possibly a historical figure or official.
    • 李贺 - Refers to Li He, a famous Tang dynasty poet.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玉蘂院 - The Jade Rhododendron Hall.
    • 眞人 - The True Person.
    • 宰相 - The chancellor.
    • 崔道枢 - Cui Daochu.
    • 洛中 - Luoyang.
    • 鳳翔府 - The Fengxiang Prefecture.
    • 老君廟 - The Laojun Temple.
    • 白傅 - Bai Fu.
    • 嚴史君 - Yan Shijun.
    • 終南山 - Mount Zhongnan.
    • 韋顓 - Wei Zhuang.
    • 命相 - Fate or destiny.
    • 李生 - Li Sheng.
    • 方士 - Daoist practitioners.
    • 谪仙 - The exiled immortal.
    • 含元殿 - The Hall of Containing the Beginning.
    • 曲江 - Qujiang.
    • 眞身 - The True Body.
    • 元相國 - Yuan Xiangguo.
    • 李贺 - Li He.

    The True Person of the Jade Rhododendron Hall descended.
    The Chancellor made offerings.
    Cui Daochu ate well water fish.
    The strong men of Luoyang.
    The Fengxiang Prefecture raised an army to fight the rebels.
    The painting at the Laojun Temple.
    Bai Fu rode a boat.
    Yan Shijun met a recluse in Mount Zhongnan.
    Wei Zhuang's hawk cried.
    On the day of destiny, hail fell.
    Li Sheng saw a divine being leave wine.
    Talking about the Daoist.
    Expanding the poem of the exiled immortal's lament.
    The Hall of Containing the Beginning.
    Qujiang.
    The True Body.
    Yuan Xiangguo visited Li He.

    钦定四库全书
    剧谈录卷上　　　　　　　唐　康骈　撰宣宗夜召翰林学士
    宣宗皇帝圣政钦明光宅天下常欲刑清俗富有宵衣旰食之懐仄席竚贤每如不及令狐相国自吴兴郡守授司郎中未居内署初与学士对便以为有宰辅之才一夕于禁林寓直忽有中使来召行百余歩至于便殿遣内人秉烛之引于御榻之前上自宣令坐问卿来从江表见彼中甿庶安否廉察郡守字人求瘼之道如何朕常思四海之大九州之广虽明君不能自理常须良弼贤佐迩来窃窥朝廷皆未覩其忠赤相国降堦俯伏曰圣意如此微臣便合得罪上曰卿才为翰林学士所职者朕之丝纶向来之言本不相

    The Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, with his wise and enlightened governance, desired to bring peace and prosperity to the land. He often worked tirelessly, rising early and retiring late, always seeking the best in his administration. He was eager to find and appoint virtuous officials, and felt as though he could never find enough. When the Chancellor Linghu Chong was appointed from his position as the Prefect of Wujiang to the post of Langzhong in the Imperial Secretariat, he had not yet taken up his duties in the inner office. One night, while lodging in the Imperial Library, he was suddenly summoned by an imperial messenger. After walking more than a hundred steps, he arrived at a side hall where a eunuch was holding a candle to wait for him. The messenger led him to the imperial bedchamber, where the Emperor personally ordered him to sit and asked, "Have you come from Jiangnan? How are the common people there? What is the situation with the Prefects and the people in your region? I often think about the vastness of the empire and the multitude of its people. Even a wise ruler cannot manage everything alone; he must rely on good ministers and capable assistants. Recently, I have observed that the court has not seen enough of their loyalty and dedication. Chancellor, you have descended the steps and bowed, saying, 'If the Emperor thinks so, I am at fault.' The Emperor replied, 'Your talents as a翰林学士 are to serve as my words and deeds. The previous statements were not meant to be taken literally.' [3]

    及既而复宣令坐俾御以玉杯斟酒赐之有小案置于御牀案上有书两卷指谓相国曰朕听政之暇未尝不披寻史籍此读者先朝所述金镜一卷则尚书大禹谟复问卿曾读金镜否对曰文皇帝所着之书有理国理身之要披阅诵讽不离于口上曰卿试举其要相国跪于御前抗声而诵至乱未尝不任不肖理未尝不任忠贤任忠贤则享天下之福任不肖则受天下之祸上止之曰朕每读至此未尝不三复后已书又云任贤勿贰去邪勿疑是则欲致升平当用此言为首相公抃舞而称曰先臣父每言金镜垂可为万古格言自 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 及既而 - Temporal phrase meaning 'after some time' or 'subsequently.'
    • 复宣令坐 - Means 'again commanded to sit,' indicating a repeated action or instruction.
    • 俾御以玉杯斟酒 - Means 'ordered to serve wine in a jade cup,' where '俾' means 'to order' or 'to command,' '御' means 'to serve' or 'to attend,' and '玉杯' means 'jade cup.'
    • 有小案置于御牀 - Means 'a small table was placed on the imperial bed,' where '小案' means 'small table,' '置于' means 'placed on,' and '御牀' means 'imperial bed.'
    • 案上有书两卷 - Means 'there were two books on the table,' where '案上' means 'on the table' and '书两卷' means 'two books.'
    • 指谓相国曰 - Means 'pointed to and said to the chancellor,' where '指谓' means 'pointed to and said' and '相国' means 'chancellor.'
    • 朕听政之暇未尝不披寻史籍 - Means 'during my leisure time when I am not attending to government affairs, I never fail to read historical records,' where '听政之暇' means 'during my leisure time when I am not attending to government affairs,' '未尝' means 'never,' '披寻' means 'to read through,' and '史籍' means 'historical records.'
    • 此读者先朝所述金镜一卷 - Means 'this reader is a scroll written by the previous dynasty about the Golden Mirror,' where '读者' means 'reader,' '先朝' means 'previous dynasty,' '所述' means 'wrote,' and '金镜' means 'Golden Mirror.'
    • 则尚书大禹谟 - Means 'which is the *Shangshu: Dayu Mu*,' where '尚书' means 'Book of Documents,' '大禹谟' means '*Dayu Mu*,' a chapter in the *Shangshu*.
    • 复问卿曾读金镜否 - Means 'then asked whether you had read the Golden Mirror,' where '复问' means 'then asked,' '卿' means 'you,' '曾读' means 'had read,' and '金镜' means 'Golden Mirror.'
    • 对曰 - Means 'replied,' where '对' means 'replied' and '曰' means 'said.'
    • 文皇帝所着之书有理国理身之要 - Means 'the books written by Emperor Wen contain the essentials of governing the country and managing oneself,' where '文皇帝' means 'Emperor Wen,' '所着之书' means 'the books he wrote,' '有理国理身之要' means 'contain the essentials of governing the country and managing oneself.'
    • 披阅诵讽不离于口 - Means 'I read and recited them without ceasing,' where '披阅' means 'read,' '诵讽' means 'recited,' and '不离于口' means 'without ceasing.'
    • 上曰 - Means 'the emperor said,' where '上' means 'the emperor' and '曰' means 'said.'
    • 卿试举其要 - Means 'you try to summarize the main points,' where '卿' means 'you,' '试' means 'try,' '举' means 'summarize,' and '其要' means 'the main points.'
    • 相国跪于御前抗声而诵 - Means 'the chancellor knelt before the emperor and loudly recited,' where '相国' means 'chancellor,' '跪于御前' means 'knelt before the emperor,' '抗声' means 'loudly,' and '而诵' means 'recited.'
    • 至乱未尝不任不肖 - Means 'when it comes to chaos, it has never failed to rely on unworthy people,' where '至乱' means 'when it comes to chaos,' '未尝' means 'never,' '不任' means 'failed to rely,' and '不肖' means 'unworthy people.'
    • 理未尝不任忠贤 - Means 'when it comes to order, it has never failed to rely on loyal and virtuous people,' where '理' means 'order,' '未尝' means 'never,' '不任' means 'failed to rely,' and '忠贤' means 'loyal and virtuous people.'
    • 任忠贤则享天下之福 - Means 'relying on loyal and virtuous people brings the happiness of the entire world,' where '任' means 'rely on,' '忠贤' means 'loyal and virtuous people,' '则' means 'then,' '享' means 'bring,' '天下' means 'the entire world,' and '之福' means 'happiness.'
    • 任不肖则受天下之祸 - Means 'relying on unworthy people brings the calamity of the entire world,' where '任' means 'rely on,' '不肖' means 'unworthy people,' '则' means 'then,' '受' means 'bring,' '天下' means 'the entire world,' and '之祸' means 'calamity.'
    • 上止之曰 - Means 'the emperor stopped him and said,' where '上' means 'the emperor,' '止之' means 'stopped him,' and '曰' means 'said.'
    • 朕每读至此未尝不三复后已 - Means 'every time I read to this point, I never fail to reread it three times before moving on,' where '朕' means 'I,' '每读' means 'every time I read,' '至此' means 'to this point,' '未尝' means 'never,' '不三复' means 'fail to reread three times,' and '后已' means 'before moving on.'
    • 书又云 - Means 'the book also says,' where '书' means 'the book' and '又云' means 'also says.'
    • 任贤勿贰去邪勿疑 - Means 'appoint virtuous people without hesitation and remove evil people without doubt,' where '任贤' means 'appoint virtuous people,' '勿贰' means 'without hesitation,' '去邪' means 'remove evil people,' and '勿疑' means 'without doubt.'
    • 是则欲致升平当用此言为首相公抃舞而称曰 - Means 'this is the principle that one should use these words to achieve peace and prosperity, and the chancellor danced with joy and said,' where '是则' means 'this is,' '欲致升平' means 'to achieve peace and prosperity,' '当用此言' means 'should use these words,' '为首相公' means 'the chancellor,' '抃舞' means 'danced with joy,' and '而称曰' means 'and said.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 相国 - Refers to the chancellor, a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 御 - Refers to the emperor or the imperial court.
    • 玉杯 - Refers to a jade cup, a symbol of luxury and status.
    • 金镜 - Refers to a scroll or text titled 'Golden Mirror,' likely a historical or philosophical work.
    • 尚书大禹谟 - Refers to a chapter in the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) titled *Dayu Mu*.
    • 文皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen, a historical figure known for his wisdom and governance.
    • 升平 - Refers to a state of peace and prosperity, a common ideal in Chinese political thought.

    After some time, the emperor again commanded the chancellor to sit and ordered him to serve wine in a jade cup. A small table was placed on the imperial bed, and there were two books on the table. Pointing to the books, the emperor said to the chancellor, "During my leisure time when I am not attending to government affairs, I never fail to read historical records. This reader is a scroll written by the previous dynasty about the Golden Mirror, which is the Shangshu: Dayu Mu. Then I asked whether you had read the Golden Mirror. You replied that the books written by Emperor Wen contain the essentials of governing the country and managing oneself, and you read and recited them without ceasing." The emperor said, "You try to summarize the main points." The chancellor knelt before the emperor and loudly recited, "When it comes to chaos, it has never failed to rely on unworthy people; when it comes to order, it has never failed to rely on loyal and virtuous people. Relying on loyal and virtuous people brings the happiness of the entire world; relying on unworthy people brings the calamity of the entire world." The emperor stopped him and said, "Every time I read to this point, I never fail to reread it three times before moving on." The book also says, "Appoint virtuous people without hesitation and remove evil people without doubt. This is the principle that one should use these words to achieve peace and prosperity." The chancellor danced with joy and said, "My father often said that the Golden Mirror is a timeless maxim." [4]

    非聪明文思无以探其壸奥况尧舜禹汤之道在典谟训诰之间陛下不以黄屋为尊每观之于夙夜将欲择贤举善使庶绩咸熈如此则功冠百王事超三五矣上曰曩者仰卿材器今日覩卿词学临轩竚立乆之谓中使曰持烛送学士归院及还禁林夜漏将半咸以近臣恩泽殆无其比繇是注意益深居嵗余遂为宰相自郡守至于台首尾才经二载尝自郊坛廻渭南尉赵嘏上诗云鹗在卿云氷在壶代天材业奉訏谟荣同伊陟傅朱户秀比王商入画图昨夜星辰廻劔履前年风月满江湖不知机务时多暇犹许诗家属和无 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 非聪明文思无以探其壸奥 - Formal expression meaning 'without intelligence and literary insight, one cannot explore its profound mysteries.'
    • 况尧舜禹汤之道在典谟训诰之间 - Rhetorical formula meaning 'how much more so the ways of Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, which are found in the classics, edicts, and admonitions.'
    • 陛下不以黄屋为尊，每观之于夙夜 - Means 'Your Majesty does not consider the yellow carriage (a symbol of imperial dignity) as a sign of respect; you often observe it day and night.'
    • 将欲择贤举善，使庶绩咸熙 - Means 'intending to select the virtuous and promote the good; to make all undertakings flourish.'
    • 如此则功冠百王事超三五矣 - Means 'thus, the achievements would surpass those of all previous kings and even the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors.'
    • 曩者仰卿材器，今日覩卿词学 - Means 'previously, I admired your talents and abilities; today, I have seen your literary skills.'
    • 临轩竚立乆之，谓中使曰 - Means 'stood by the hall for a long time; told the eunuch to say.'
    • 持烛送学士归院，及还禁林夜漏将半 - Means 'to hold a candle and escort the scholar to his residence; when returning to the imperial garden, the night was halfway through.'
    • 咸以近臣恩泽殆无其比，繇是注意益深 - Means 'all considered the favor shown to the close ministers unparalleled; from this point on, the attention became even deeper.'
    • 居嵗余遂为宰相，自郡守至于台首尾才经二载 - Means 'after living for over a year, he became the chancellor; from the position of prefect to the highest office, it took only two years.'
    • 尝自郊坛廻渭南尉赵嘏上诗云 - Means 'once, after returning from the imperial altar, the magistrate of Weinan, Zhao Gu, presented a poem.'
    • 鹗在卿云氷在壶，代天材业奉訏谟 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the osprey is among the noble clouds, the ice is in the pot,' symbolizing high status and purity.
    • 荣同伊陟傅朱户，秀比王商入画图 - Means 'to share the glory of Yi Zhi and Fu Zhu, who were given red doors as a sign of honor; to be as distinguished as Wang Shang, entering the painting.'
    • 昨夜星辰廻劔履，前年风月满江湖 - Means 'last night, the stars returned to the sword and shoes; last year, the wind and moon filled the rivers and lakes.'
    • 不知机务时多暇，犹许诗家属和无 - Means 'not knowing if there is much leisure time for state affairs; still allowing the family to recite and harmonize the poems.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尧舜禹汤 - Refers to the legendary rulers Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, who are considered ideal rulers in Chinese history.
    • 典谟训诰 - Refers to the classics, edicts, and admonitions, which are important texts in Confucianism.
    • 黄屋 - Refers to the yellow carriage, a symbol of imperial dignity.
    • 中使 - Refers to a eunuch, a trusted servant of the emperor.
    • 禁林 - Refers to the imperial garden, a place where the emperor and his close associates would gather.
    • 伊陟傅朱户 - Refers to Yi Zhi and Fu Zhu, who were given red doors as a sign of honor.
    • 王商入画图 - Refers to Wang Shang, a famous official who was depicted in paintings.
    • 郊坛 - Refers to the imperial altar, a place where important religious and political ceremonies were held.
    • 渭南尉 - Refers to the magistrate of Weinan, a local official.
    • 赵嘏 - Refers to Zhao Gu, a poet and official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尧舜禹汤 - The legendary rulers Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang.
    • 典谟训诰 - The classics, edicts, and admonitions.
    • 黄屋 - The yellow carriage.
    • 中使 - A eunuch.
    • 禁林 - The imperial garden.
    • 伊陟傅朱户 - Yi Zhi and Fu Zhu.
    • 王商入画图 - Wang Shang.
    • 郊坛 - The imperial altar.
    • 渭南尉 - The magistrate of Weinan.
    • 赵嘏 - Zhao Gu.

    Without intelligence and literary insight, one cannot explore its profound mysteries. How much more so the ways of Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, which are found in the classics, edicts, and admonitions. Your Majesty does not consider the yellow carriage as a sign of respect; you often observe it day and night. Intending to select the virtuous and promote the good, to make all undertakings flourish. Thus, the achievements would surpass those of all previous kings and even the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors. The emperor said, "Previously, I admired your talents and abilities. Today, I have seen your literary skills. Standing by the hall for a long time, I told the eunuch to say, 'Hold a candle and escort the scholar to his residence.' When returning to the imperial garden, the night was halfway through. All considered the favor shown to the close ministers unparalleled. From this point on, the attention became even deeper. After living for over a year, he became the chancellor. From the position of prefect to the highest office, it took only two years. Once, after returning from the imperial altar, the magistrate of Weinan, Zhao Gu, presented a poem. 'The osprey is among the noble clouds, the ice is in the pot. To represent the talents and industries of heaven, serving the grand plans. To share the glory of Yi Zhi and Fu Zhu, who were given red doors as a sign of honor. To be as distinguished as Wang Shang, entering the painting. Last night, the stars returned to the sword and shoes. Last year, the wind and moon filled the rivers and lakes. Not knowing if there is much leisure time for state affairs, still allowing the family to recite and harmonize the poems.'" [5]

    议曰凡懐才抱器有时而通非得茍容虽遇不显向使明主有任贤之意近臣无专对之能徒彰妄进之讥方病退慙之説殊恩厚渥岂及于身是以君子励志饬躬以遭逢之运良有防哉
    　　刘平见安禄山魑魅
    　　咸通中有五经博士卢斚得神仙保养之道自言生于隋代宿德朝士皆云见于童幼举世信奉之不穷年寿云安史之乱隐于终南山中其后或出或处先是令狐相公谕以柱下漆园之事稍从宦于京师常话与处士刘平执友修道平天寳中居于齐鲁间尤善吐纳之术能夜中视物不假灯烛安禄山在范阳厚币致于门下平见禄山左右常有物数十殊形诡状持鑪执葢以为导从平心异之谓禄山必为人杰及禄山朝觐与平俱至辇下行至华隂县值叶天师投龙于西岳平见二青衣童子乘虚而至所卫禄山魑魅皆弃鑪投葢狼狈而行平因知禄山为邪物所辅必不以正道克终及禄山却归范阳遂逃入华山而 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 懐才抱器 - Literally 'to carry talent and hold tools,' meaning to possess great ability and resources.
    • 時而通 - Means 'opportune moments of success or recognition.'
    • 茍容 - Means 'to seek favor or approval through flattery or compromise.'
    • 遇不显 - Means 'to meet with no recognition or advancement.'
    • 向使 - A conditional particle meaning 'if only' or 'had it not been for.'
    • 明主有任贤之意 - Means 'a wise ruler with the intention to appoint virtuous individuals.'
    • 近臣无专对之能 - Means 'ministers close to the ruler lack the ability to speak on their own.'
    • 徒彰妄进之讥 - Means 'only to invite criticism for false advancement.'
    • 方病退慙之説 - Means 'the theory of illness retreat and shame.'
    • 殊恩厚渥 - Means 'extraordinary favor and kindness.'
    • 君子励志饬躬 - Means 'a gentleman sets his mind on lofty goals and disciplines himself.'
    • 遭逢之运良有防哉 - Means 'encountering good fortune is indeed well guarded by fate.'
    • 魑魅 - Refers to spirits or demons, often used metaphorically to describe deceitful or harmful individuals.
    • 宿德朝士 - Means 'distinguished officials of the court with long-standing virtue.'
    • 举世信奉之 - Means 'the entire world believes and venerates him.'
    • 穷年寿 - Means 'to have an exceptionally long life.'
    • 柱下漆园之事 - Refers to the stories of Laozi and Zhuangzi, both of whom are associated with Daoist philosophy.
    • 稍从宦于京师 - Means 'gradually began serving in the capital.'
    • 执友修道 - Means 'close friends who practice Daoism.'
    • 吐纳之术 - Means 'breathing exercises or techniques.'
    • 能夜中视物不假灯烛 - Means 'able to see things at night without the aid of lamps or candles.'
    • 厚币致于门下 - Means 'offered generous gifts to attract him to his service.'
    • 左右常有鬼物数十 - Means 'often accompanied by dozens of ghostly beings.'
    • 殊形诡状 - Means 'strange and bizarre forms.'
    • 持炉执盖 - Means 'holding incense burners and umbrellas.'
    • 以为导从 - Means 'to serve as a retinue.'
    • 心异之 - Means 'found it strange or unusual.'
    • 必为人杰 - Means 'surely a person of great talent or virtue.'
    • 克终 - Means 'to achieve a successful conclusion.'
    • 却归范阳 - Means 'returned to Fan Yang.'
    • 逃入华山而隐 - Means 'fled into Mount Hua and went into hiding.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 明主 - Refers to a wise and virtuous ruler.
    • 近臣 - Refers to ministers or officials close to the ruler.
    • 任贤 - The practice of appointing virtuous and capable individuals.
    • 专对 - The ability to speak on one's own behalf, often in diplomatic or official settings.
    • 妄进 - Refers to false or unwarranted advancement.
    • 方病退慙之説 - A theory or belief that illness can lead to retreat and shame.
    • 殊恩厚渥 - Extraordinary favor and kindness, often from a ruler.
    • 君子 - Refers to a virtuous and morally upright person.
    • 励志饬躬 - The practice of setting high goals and disciplining oneself.
    • 遭逢之运 - Good fortune or favorable circumstances.
    • 良有防哉 - Suggests that such good fortune is well guarded or protected.
    • 刘平 - A historical figure, likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 安禄山 - A prominent military leader and later a rebel during the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 卢斚 - A historical figure, a five classics doctor who practiced Daoist longevity techniques.
    • 令狐相公 - Likely a high-ranking official, possibly referring to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 柱下漆园之事 - Refers to the stories of Laozi and Zhuangzi, both of whom are associated with Daoist philosophy.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely Chang'an or Luoyang.
    • 吐纳之术 - Breathing exercises or techniques, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 夜中视物不假灯烛 - The ability to see in the dark without artificial light, a common theme in Daoist and alchemical texts.
    • 厚币致于门下 - The practice of offering generous gifts to attract someone to one's service.
    • 鬼物 - Refers to ghostly or supernatural beings.
    • 殊形诡状 - Strange and bizarre forms, often used to describe supernatural entities.
    • 持炉执盖 - Holding incense burners and umbrellas, typical of a retinue in Daoist or religious contexts.
    • 魑魅 - Spirits or demons, often used metaphorically to describe deceitful or harmful individuals.
    • 投龙 - A ritual practice in Daoism involving the offering of dragons or other mythical creatures.
    • 西岳 - Refers to Mount Hua, one of the Five Sacred Mountains in China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 明主 - A wise and virtuous ruler.
    • 近臣 - Ministers or officials close to the ruler.
    • 刘平 - A historical figure, likely a Daoist practitioner.
    • 安禄山 - A prominent military leader and later a rebel during the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 卢斚 - A historical figure, a five classics doctor who practiced Daoist longevity techniques.
    • 令狐相公 - Likely a high-ranking official, possibly referring to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely Chang'an or Luoyang.
    • 吐纳之术 - Breathing exercises or techniques, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 夜中视物不假灯烛 - The ability to see in the dark without artificial light, a common theme in Daoist and alchemical texts.
    • 厚币致于门下 - The practice of offering generous gifts to attract someone to one's service.

    The discourse says that those who possess great talent and resources sometimes achieve success, but if they seek favor through flattery or compromise, they may not be recognized. If a wise ruler intended to appoint virtuous individuals but his ministers lacked the ability to speak on their own, this would only invite criticism for false advancement and the theory of illness retreat and shame. Such extraordinary favor and kindness would not reach them. Therefore, a gentleman sets his mind on lofty goals and disciplines himself, and encountering good fortune is indeed well guarded by fate.

    Liu Ping saw the spirits accompanying An Lushan.

    During the Xiantong era, there was a Five Classics Doctor named Lu Jia who practiced Daoist longevity techniques and claimed to have been born during the Sui Dynasty. Distinguished officials of the court with long-standing virtue all said they had seen him since his youth. The entire world believed and venerated him, and he was said to have an exceptionally long life. During the An Lushan Rebellion, he went into hiding in the Zhongnan Mountains. Later, he sometimes appeared and sometimes disappeared. Previously, Chancellor Linghu had told him about the stories of Laozi and Zhuangzi, and he gradually began serving in the capital. He often talked with the Daoist practitioner Liu Ping, his close friend who practiced Daoism. During the Tianbao period, Liu Ping lived in the regions of Qi and Lu. He was particularly skilled in breathing exercises and could see things at night without the aid of lamps or candles. When An Lushan was in Fan Yang, he offered generous gifts to attract him to his service. Liu Ping saw that An Lushan was often accompanied by dozens of ghostly beings with strange and bizarre forms, holding incense burners and umbrellas as a retinue. He found it strange and unusual, and thought that An Lushan must be a person of great talent or virtue. When An Lushan went to court, he and Liu Ping traveled together by carriage. They reached Huayin County, where Ye the Celestial Master performed the ritual of offering dragons at the Western Mountain. Liu Ping saw two young boys in green robes ride on empty space, and the spirits guarding An Lushan all abandoned their incense burners and umbrellas, running away in disarray. Liu Ping then realized that An Lushan was being assisted by evil spirits and would not achieve a successful conclusion through proper means. When An Lushan returned to Fan Yang, Liu Ping fled into Mount Hua and went into hiding. [6]

    王鲔活崔相公歌妓
    　　凤翔少尹王鲔【侍郎凝之叔也】年十四五与儿童戏于果园竹林下见二枯首为粪壤所没乃令小仆择净地瘗之祭以酒其后数夕阴晦忽闻牕外有声良久问之云某等受郎君深恩免在芜未知所酬耶愿以驱策迩来凡有吉凶先兆肹蠁必来潜报如此数年遂与灵物通彻崔相国珙为度支使雅知于鲔一夕留饮家酿酒酣稍欢云有小妓善歌得于亲友因令左右召之良久不至相国俄而自归内见理妆才罢忽病心痛请饮汤而出相国怪而问之云适见一人着短后绯袍控马而去语未毕家仆遽报中恶救之不及矣相国悲惋不已鲔密言有一事或可救之然须得白牛头及酒一斛因召左右试令求觅有度支所 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 王鲔 - Refers to Wang Wei, a young man.
    • 侍郎凝之叔也 - Indicates that Wang Wei is the nephew of Wang Ning, a high-ranking official.
    • 与儿童戏于果园竹林下 - Describes a scene where Wang Wei plays with children in an orchard and bamboo grove.
    • 见二枯首为粪壤所没 - Describes the discovery of two dry heads buried in manure.
    • 令小仆择净地瘗之祭以酒 - Orders a servant to choose a clean place to bury the heads and offer wine as a sacrifice.
    • 数夕阴晦忽闻牕外有声 - Describes a series of dark and gloomy nights when a sound is heard outside the window.
    • 受郎君深恩免在芜未知所酬耶 - The spirits thank Wang Wei for their rescue and ask how they can repay him.
    • 愿以驱策迩来凡有吉凶先兆肹蠁必来潜报 - The spirits offer to inform Wang Wei of any good or bad omens.
    • 遂与灵物通彻 - Indicates that Wang Wei has established a connection with the spirits.
    • 崔相国珙为度支使雅知于鲔 - Describes Cui Gong, the Chancellor, who is familiar with Wang Wei.
    • 有小妓善歌得于亲友 - Describes a young woman skilled in singing, obtained from friends and relatives.
    • 适见一人着短后绯袍控马而去 - Describes a vision of a man in a short-sleeved red robe riding away on a horse.
    • 中恶救之不及矣 - Indicates that the young woman was struck by a sudden illness and could not be saved.
    • 有一事或可救之然须得白牛头及酒一斛 - Wang Wei suggests a possible solution involving a white bull's head and a measure of wine.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 凤翔少尹 - A local official position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 度支使 - A high-ranking official responsible for finance and taxation.
    • 小妓善歌 - Refers to a young woman skilled in singing, a common form of entertainment in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 度支所 - Refers to the office of the Chancellor, where financial matters were handled.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王鲔 - Wang Wei, a young man.
    • 侍郎凝之 - Wang Ning, a high-ranking official.
    • 崔相国珙 - Cui Gong, the Chancellor.
    • 度支使 - A high-ranking official responsible for finance and taxation.
    • 度支所 - The office of the Chancellor, where financial matters were handled.

    Wang Wei Revives a Singer for Cui Gong

    Wang Wei, the young man from Fengxiang, who was the nephew of Wang Ning, a high-ranking official, was around fifteen or sixteen years old. One day, he played with children in an orchard and bamboo grove when he saw two dry heads buried in manure. He ordered a servant to choose a clean place to bury the heads and offer wine as a sacrifice. After several dark and gloomy nights, he suddenly heard a sound outside the window. After a long time, he asked, "We have received great kindness from the young master, and we are no longer in the wilderness. We do not know how to repay you." They offered to inform him of any good or bad omens in the future. For several years, Wang Wei established a connection with the spirits. Cui Gong, the Chancellor and a high-ranking official responsible for finance and taxation, was familiar with Wang Wei. One evening, Cui Gong invited Wang Wei to drink at home. They enjoyed a family brew, and as they became more cheerful, Cui Gong mentioned that he had a young woman skilled in singing, obtained from friends and relatives. He then ordered his attendants to summon her. After a long time, she did not arrive. Cui Gong suddenly returned from inside, having just finished dressing. She suddenly fell ill with heart pain and asked for some soup. Cui Gong was surprised and asked what happened. She said she had just seen a man in a short-sleeved red robe riding away on a horse. Before he could finish speaking, a servant rushed in to report that she had suddenly fallen ill and could not be saved. Cui Gong was deeply saddened. Wang Wei secretly said that there might be a way to save her, but it would require a white bull's head and a measure of wine. He then ordered his attendants to try to find them at the office of the Chancellor. [7]

    由干事者径诣东市肉行以善价取之将牛头而至鲔令扶策歌者置于净室榻前以土盆盛酒横板用安牛头设席焚香密封其户且戒曰专伺之晓鼓一动闻牛吼当急开户可以活矣鲔既去久而无声禁鼓忽鸣果闻牛吼开户视之歌者微喘盆中斛酒悉干牛目怒出于外数日之后方述前事云其夕治妆既毕有人促召出门乗马而行约数里见有室宇华丽其间列筵张乐四座皆朱紫少年见歌者至大喜致于女妓中欢笑方洽忽闻人大呌声震庭庑坐中皆失色相视妓乐俱罢俄见牛头人长丈余执防径趋而入无不狼狈而走唯歌者在焉牛头者引于堦前背负而出才数十歩忽觉卧于室内迩后相国询其由鲔终不言尽其事 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 曉鼓一動聞牛吼當急開戶可以活矣 - When the dawn drum beats and a cow's roar is heard, open the door quickly to save the singer.
    • 數日之後方述前事云 - Several days later, the singer recounted the previous events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 東市肉行 - Refers to a meat market in the eastern part of the city.
    • 淨室 - Refers to a clean or purified room, often used in religious or ritual contexts.
    • 土盆 - A clay or earthenware bowl, commonly used in ancient times.
    • 橫板 - A horizontal board or plank, possibly used to support the cow's head.
    • 焚香 - Burning incense, a common practice in religious or ceremonial settings.
    • 禁鼓 - A drum used to signal the start of a new day or an important event.
    • 牛吼 - The sound of a cow, which in this context is a signal for a specific action.
    • 相國 - A high-ranking official, often equivalent to a prime minister or chancellor.
    • 治妝 - Refers to the singer's preparations, possibly including makeup or dressing.
    • 牛頭人 - A figure with the head of a cow, indicating a supernatural or mythological entity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 相國 - A high-ranking official, often equivalent to a prime minister or chancellor.

    A messenger was sent to the eastern market's meat shop to purchase a cow's head at a good price. Upon bringing the cow's head back, the messenger was instructed to have the singer, who was being supported by a staff, placed in a clean room. A table was set up with wine in a clay bowl, and a horizontal board was used to support the cow's head. The room was sealed, and the messenger was warned to keep watch. It was said that when the dawn drum beats and a cow's roar is heard, the door should be opened quickly to save the singer.

    After the messenger left, there was no sound for a long time. Suddenly, the drum sounded, and indeed, a cow's roar was heard. The door was opened, and it was found that the singer was barely breathing, and the wine in the bowl had completely dried up. The singer's eyes were filled with rage, and they seemed to be looking outside. Several days later, the singer recounted the previous events: that evening, after finishing her preparations, she was urgently summoned and went out on horseback. After traveling several miles, she saw a beautifully decorated building. Inside, a feast was set up, and music was playing. The guests were all young noblemen dressed in purple and red. When the singer arrived, they were very happy and treated her as a courtesan. They were enjoying themselves when suddenly a loud cry was heard, shaking the courtyard. Everyone lost their composure and looked at each other. The music and entertainment stopped. Soon, a figure with the head of a cow, over ten feet tall, holding a spear, rushed in. Everyone fled in panic, but the singer remained. The figure with the cow's head took her to the front of the steps, carried her on his back, and left. After walking a few dozen steps, the singer suddenly realized she was lying in the room. Later, when the high official asked her about the events, the singer finally revealed everything. [8]

    御史滩
    　　河南府伊阙县前临大溪每僚佐有入台者即水中先有小滩涨出石砾金沙澄澈可爱牛相国为县尉一旦忽报滩出翌日邑者与同僚列筵于亭上观之因召耆宿询其事有老吏云此必分司御史非西台之命是西台滩上当有防鸂防立前后居人以此为则相国潜揣县僚无出于已因举杯祝曰既能有滩何惜一双鸂防宴未终俄有飞下不旬日拜西台监察御史
    　　浑令公李西平朱泚云梯
    　　朱泚之乱德宗皇帝车驾出幸奉天是时防边藩镇皆已举兵扈跸泚自率凶渠直至城下有西明寺僧陷在贼中性甚机巧敎泚造攻城云梯其髙九十余尺上施板屋楼橹可以下瞰城中浑中令李司徒奏曰贼锋既盛云梯又壮纵之诚恐不能御及其尚逺请以鋭兵挫之遂率王师五千列阵而出于时束蕴居后约战酣而燎风势不便火不能举二公酹酒抗词拜空而祝天道助顺至圣感神 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 御史滩 - Refers to a specific location associated with the emergence of a sandbar, likely named after the position of the magistrate or censor.
    • 僚佐有入台者 - A phrase meaning 'if an assistant is appointed to the Censorate,' where '台' refers to the Censorate, an imperial supervisory institution.
    • 先有小滩涨出石砾金沙澄澈可爱 - A descriptive phrase meaning 'a small sandbar first appeared, with clear and beautiful gravel and gold sand.'
    • 耆宿 - Refers to elderly and experienced local officials or scholars.
    • 拜西台监察御史 - Means 'appointed as a censor of the Censorate,' where '西台' is another term for the Censorate.
    • 浑令公李西平 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Li Xiping, with '令公' being a respectful title.
    • 朱泚之乱 - Refers to the rebellion led by Zhu Ci during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 车驾出幸奉天 - Means 'the emperor's carriage departed to the imperial retreat at Fanyang,' where '奉天' is the name of the retreat.
    • 防边藩镇皆已举兵扈跸 - Means 'the frontier military governors had already raised armies to escort the emperor,' where '扈跸' refers to escorting the imperial procession.
    • 云梯又壮 - A phrase meaning 'the siege ladder is also formidable,' where '云梯' refers to a large, multi-story siege ladder.
    • 束蕴居后 - Means 'tied straw bundles were placed behind,' referring to a defensive strategy.
    • 约战酣而燎 - Means 'agreed to ignite when the battle was intense,' where '燎' refers to setting fire.
    • 风势不便 - Means 'the wind was unfavorable,' indicating poor conditions for the fire to spread.
    • 酹酒抗词 - Means 'to pour wine and speak solemnly,' a ritual act of offering prayers or vows.
    • 拜空而祝 - Means 'to bow and pray,' indicating a gesture of supplication.
    • 至圣感神 - Means 'the supreme sage moved the gods,' suggesting divine intervention.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 河南府伊阙县 - Refers to Yique County in Henan Province, a historical administrative region.
    • 相国 - Refers to a high-ranking official, often equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 西明寺 - Refers to a specific Buddhist temple, likely named 'Western Bright Temple.'
    • 机巧 - Means 'ingenious' or 'clever,' referring to the monk's skill in constructing the siege ladder.
    • 浑中令李司徒 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Li, with '司徒' being a title for a senior official.
    • 朱泚 - A historical figure who led a rebellion against the Tang Dynasty.
    • 德宗皇帝 - Refers to Emperor Dezong of Tang, the ruler during the rebellion.

    Translation:

    The Censor's Sandbar

    In Yique County of Henan Prefecture, which borders a large stream, whenever an assistant was appointed to the Censorate, a small sandbar would first appear in the water, with clear and beautiful gravel and gold sand. The County Magistrate, Cattle, upon learning of this, suddenly heard that a sandbar had emerged. The next day, he and his colleagues set up a banquet on the pavilion to observe it. He then summoned the elderly officials to inquire about the matter. An old clerk said, "This must be the sign of a Censor from the Censorate, not from the Western Censorate. If it is from the Western Censorate, there should be a pair of pheasants standing before and after the sandbar, which the locals use as a rule." The County Magistrate secretly pondered that none of the county officials could surpass him. He raised his cup and prayed, "If it is possible to have a sandbar, why not a pair of pheasants?" Before the banquet ended, a pair of pheasants suddenly flew down. Within ten days, he was appointed as a Censor of the Censorate.

    The Cloud Ladder of Li Xiping

    During the rebellion led by Zhu Ci, Emperor Dezong's carriage departed to the imperial retreat at Fanyang. At that time, the frontier military governors had already raised armies to escort the emperor. Zhu Ci personally led his troops to the city walls. A Buddhist monk from the Western Bright Temple, who had fallen into the hands of the rebels, was very ingenious and taught Zhu Ci to build a cloud ladder, which was over ninety feet tall. On top of it, there were wooden houses and watchtowers that could overlook the city. Li Xiping, the County Magistrate, reported to Emperor Dezong, saying, "The enemy's attack is fierce, and their cloud ladder is also formidable. If we let them advance, we are truly afraid that we cannot withstand them. While they are still far away, please allow us to use sharp troops to crush them." He then led five thousand imperial troops out to meet the enemy. At that time, they had tied straw bundles behind their formation, agreeing to ignite them when the battle was intense. However, the wind was unfavorable, and the fire could not be lit. The two generals poured wine, spoke solemnly, and bowed while praying, invoking the assistance of heaven. The divine intervention helped the righteous cause, and the sage emperor moved the gods. [9]

    泚贼包藏祸心窃弄凶器敢以狂孽来犯乗舆今拥众胁君将逼城垒瑊等誓输忠节志殄妖氛社稷再兴威灵未冺当使云梯就逆党氷销于是词情慷慨人百其勇俄而风势遽廻鼔噪而进火烈飚骇烟埃涨天梯烬卒奔贼遂退衂徳宗皇帝御楼以观中外咸称万岁及尅复京国二公勲绩为首宠锡茅土铭镂钟鼎匡扶社稷终始一致其后李司徒有子四人皆分部节制忠烈荣耀于今蔼然
    　　李司徒尝于左广効职久未迁升闻桑道茂善相人赍绢壹匹凌晨而往时道茂倾信者甚众造谒多不见之闻李公在门亲自迎接施设肴醴情意甚专既而问之谓曰他日建立勲庸贵盛无比或事权在手当以性命为托李公莫测其由但慙唯而已请廻所贶缣换李公所着汗衫子仍请于上书名云他日见此相忆及泚之叛道茂防在贼庭既尅复京师从乱者悉皆就戮李公受命斩决道茂将就刑请致分雪之词遂以汗衫为请李公奏以非罪遂令原之 [10]

    The rebel leader Zhu Ci harbored evil intentions and secretly wielded weapons of destruction, daring to bring rebellion against the emperor. Now, gathering his forces to threaten the ruler, he was about to attack the city walls. Generals like Jie and others swore to deliver loyal service and were determined to eliminate evil influences. With the state revived and the divine power of the emperor remaining intact, they would ensure that the siege tower collapsed and the rebel forces melted away. Thus, their words and emotions were grand and inspiring, and their courage surged a hundredfold. Suddenly, the wind changed direction, and they advanced with drumbeats and shouting. The fire was fierce, and the wind was terrifying, with smoke and dust filling the sky. The siege tower burned, and the soldiers fled. The rebels retreated in defeat. Emperor Dezong watched from the palace tower, and both inside and outside the capital celebrated by shouting "Long live the Emperor!" Upon reconquering the capital, the achievements of the two generals were foremost, and they were bestowed with fiefs and titles, inscribed on bells and tripods, supporting the state consistently from beginning to end. Afterward, Li Huaien had four sons who were divided into departments and controlled, their loyalty and valor being glorious and dignified to this day.

    Li Huaien once served in the Left Guard for a long time without promotion. Hearing that Sang Daomao was skilled in divination, he brought a piece of silk early in the morning to visit. At that time, many people trusted Sang Daomao, and most visitors were not received. Upon hearing that Li Gong was at the door, Sang Daomao personally welcomed him, setting out food and wine, showing great sincerity. Afterward, he asked Li Gong, saying, "One day, you will establish great achievements and become immensely powerful. If you hold power in your hands, you should entrust your life to me." Li Gong could not fathom the reason but felt ashamed and merely replied affirmatively. He then asked to exchange the silk for one of Li Gong's undergarments and requested that his name be written on it, saying, "One day, when you see this, remember me." When Zhu Ci rebelled, Sang Daomao was in the rebel camp. After the capital was reconquered, those who followed the rebels were all executed. Li Gong was ordered to carry out the executions. As Sang Daomao was about to be executed, he pleaded for a chance to speak in his defense, using the undergarment as proof. Li Gong reported that Sang Daomao was not guilty, and the emperor ordered him to be spared. [10]

    潘将军失珠
    　　京国豪士潘将军住光德坊【忘其名时人呼为潘鹘硉也】本居襄汉间常乗舟射利因泊江壖有僧乞食留之数日尽心檀施僧谓潘曰观尔形质器度与众贾不同至于妻孥已来皆享巨福因以玉念珠一穿留赠云寳之不但通财他后亦有官禄既而迁贸数年藏镪巨万遂均陶朱其后职居左广列第京师常寳念珠贮之以绣囊玉合置之于道塲内每月朔则出而拜之一旦开合啓囊已亡失珠矣然而缄封旧他物亦无所失于是夺魄防精以为其家将破之兆有主藏者尝识京兆府停解所由王超年且八十已来因密话其事超曰异 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 京国豪士 - Refers to a wealthy and influential man from the capital city.
    • 潘将军 - A title indicating a military leader named Pan.
    • 光德坊 - A specific district or neighborhood in the capital.
    • 忘其名时人呼为潘鹘硉也 - Indicates that the person's name is unknown, but he is called 'Pan Gu Lu' by the people.
    • 本居襄汉间 - Means 'originally lived in the region of Xiang and Han.'
    • 常乘舟射利 - Means 'often traveled by boat to make a profit.'
    • 因泊江壖 - Means 'and once docked at the riverbank.'
    • 有僧乞食留之数日 - Means 'a monk came to beg for food and stayed for several days.'
    • 尽心檀施 - Means 'offered generous alms.'
    • 观尔形质器度与众贾不同 - Means 'your appearance, character, and demeanor are different from ordinary merchants.'
    • 至于妻孥已来皆享巨福 - Means 'and from your wife and children onwards, you will all enjoy great fortune.'
    • 因以玉念珠一穿留赠云寳之不但通财他后亦有官禄 - Means 'he gave you a string of jade prayer beads, saying that they would not only bring wealth but also future official rank.'
    • 迁贸数年藏镪巨万遂均陶朱其后职居左广列第京师 - Means 'after several years of trading, you accumulated vast wealth and became as wealthy as Tao Zhu, later holding a high position in the capital.'
    • 常寳念珠贮之以绣囊玉合置之道塲内 - Means 'you always kept the prayer beads in a brocade bag and a jade box, placing them in the main hall.'
    • 每月朔则出而拜之一旦开合啓囊已亡失珠矣 - Means 'every month on the first day, you would take them out and worship them, but one day when you opened the box, the beads were missing.'
    • 然而缄封旧他物亦无所失 - Means 'however, the seals were intact, and nothing else was missing.'
    • 于是夺魄防精以为其家将破之兆 - Means 'thus, he was terrified and believed this was a sign that his family would be destroyed.'
    • 有主藏者尝识京兆府停解所由王超年且八十已来因密话其事超曰异 - Means 'a person in charge of the household, who knew a man named Wang Chao from the Imperial Censorate, who was over eighty years old, told him about the incident. Wang Chao said it was strange.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京国豪士 - Refers to a wealthy and influential individual in the capital.
    • 潘将军 - A military leader named Pan.
    • 光德坊 - A specific district in the capital.
    • 襄汉间 - A region in present-day Hubei Province.
    • 僧 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 檀施 - Generous alms.
    • 形质器度 - Appearance, character, and demeanor.
    • 妻孥 - Wife and children.
    • 巨福 - Great fortune.
    • 玉念珠 - Jade prayer beads.
    • 通财 - Bring wealth.
    • 官禄 - Official rank.
    • 迁贸 - Trading.
    • 藏镪 - Accumulated wealth.
    • 陶朱 - A reference to Tao Zhu, a famous merchant in ancient China.
    • 左广 - A high-ranking position.
    • 绣囊 - Brocade bag.
    • 玉合 - Jade box.
    • 道塲 - Main hall.
    • 每月朔 - The first day of the lunar month.
    • 京兆府 - The Imperial Censorate, a government office.
    • 王超 - A man named Wang Chao.
    • 异 - Strange or unusual.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 忘其名时人呼为潘鹘硉也 - This phrase is somewhat ambiguous, but it clearly indicates that the person's name is unknown and he is called 'Pan Gu Lu' by the people.
    • 因以玉念珠一穿留赠云寳之不但通财他后亦有官禄 - This long sentence must be read as a single statement, where the monk gives the prayer beads and makes a prediction about future wealth and official rank.
    • 迁贸数年藏镪巨万遂均陶朱其后职居左广列第京师 - This sentence describes a series of events over several years, leading to the accumulation of vast wealth and a high position in the capital.
    • 常寳念珠贮之以绣囊玉合置之道塲内 - This sentence describes the careful storage of the prayer beads.
    • 每月朔则出而拜之一旦开合啓囊已亡失珠矣 - This sentence describes the ritual of taking out the prayer beads and the discovery of their disappearance.
    • 然而缄封旧他物亦无所失 - This sentence contrasts the intact seals with the missing beads.
    • 于是夺魄防精以为其家将破之兆 - This sentence describes the person's reaction to the missing beads.
    • 有主藏者尝识京兆府停解所由王超年且八十已来因密话其事超曰异 - This sentence introduces a character who knows a man from the Imperial Censorate and recounts the incident.

    Proper Nouns

    • 潘将军 - General Pan.
    • 光德坊 - Guangde Ward.
    • 潘鹘硉 - Pan Gu Lu.
    • 襄汉间 - Xiang and Han region.
    • 京兆府 - Imperial Censorate.
    • 王超 - Wang Chao.

    A General Loses His Prayer Beads

    A wealthy and influential man from the capital, General Pan, lived in Guangde Ward [his name is unknown, but he is called Pan Gu Lu by the people]. Originally from the region of Xiang and Han, he often traveled by boat to make a profit and once docked at the riverbank. A monk came to beg for food and stayed for several days, during which Pan offered generous alms. The monk said to Pan, "Your appearance, character, and demeanor are different from ordinary merchants. And from your wife and children onwards, you will all enjoy great fortune." He then gave Pan a string of jade prayer beads, saying that they would not only bring wealth but also future official rank.

    After several years of trading, Pan accumulated vast wealth and became as wealthy as Tao Zhu, later holding a high position in the capital. He always kept the prayer beads in a brocade bag and a jade box, placing them in the main hall. Every month on the first day, he would take them out and worship them. One day, when he opened the box, the beads were missing. However, the seals were intact, and nothing else was missing. Thus, he was terrified and believed this was a sign that his family would be destroyed.

    A person in charge of the household, who knew a man named Wang Chao from the Imperial Censorate, who was over eighty years old, told him about the incident. Wang Chao said it was strange. [11]

    哉此非攘窃之盗也其试为寻之未知果得否超他日因过胜业坊北街时春雨新霁有三鬟女子年可十七八衣装蓝缕穿木屐立于道侧槐树下值军中少年蹴踘接而送之直髙数丈于是观者渐众超独异焉及罢随之而行止于胜业坊北门短曲有母同居葢以纫针为业超异时因以他事熟之遂为甥舅然居室甚贫与母同卧土榻烟爨不动者往往经于累日设肴羞时有水陆珍异吴中初进洞庭橘子恩赐宰臣外京辇未有此物密以一枚赠超云有人从内中出而禀性刚决超意甚疑之如此往来周岁矣超一旦防酒食与之从容徐谓之曰 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 哉此非攘窃之盗也 - A rhetorical exclamation followed by a negation, meaning 'Alas, this is not a thief who steals openly.'
    • 其试为寻之未知果得否 - Means 'Let us try to find out' or 'Let us investigate,' indicating an inquiry.
    • 未知果得否 - Means 'whether it can be obtained' or 'if it can be found,' expressing uncertainty.
    • 超他日因过胜业坊北街 - Means 'On another day, Zhao happened to pass by the northern street of Shengye Lane,' a straightforward description of an event.
    • 时春雨新霁 - Means 'at a time when the spring rain had just cleared,' describing the weather.
    • 有三鬟女子年可十七八 - Means 'there was a girl with three buns on her head, about seventeen or eighteen years old,' describing a person.
    • 衣装蓝缕 - Means 'dressed in tattered clothes,' indicating poverty.
    • 穿木屐 - Means 'wearing wooden clogs,' a specific detail about the girl's attire.
    • 立于道侧槐树下 - Means 'standing by the side of the road under a locust tree,' describing the girl's location.
    • 值军中少年蹴踘接而送之 - Means 'a young soldier happened to kick a ball and hit her,' describing an accidental event.
    • 直髙数丈 - Means 'reaching several zhang high,' describing the height of the ball's trajectory.
    • 于是观者渐众 - Means 'then the onlookers gradually gathered,' describing the reaction of others.
    • 超独异焉 - Means 'Zhao alone found this strange,' indicating Zhao's unique perception.
    • 及罢随之而行 - Means 'afterwards, he followed her,' describing Zhao's action.
    • 止于胜业坊北门短曲有母同居 - Means 'he stopped at the short lane near the northern gate of Shengye Lane, where he lived with his mother,' describing Zhao's residence.
    • 盖以纫针为业 - Means 'as she made a living by sewing,' describing the girl's occupation.
    • 超异时因以他事熟之 - Means 'Zhao, being curious, got to know her through other matters,' describing Zhao's curiosity.
    • 遂为甥舅然居室甚贫 - Means 'they became like uncle and nephew, but their living conditions were very poor,' describing the relationship and circumstances.
    • 与母同卧土榻 - Means 'they slept together on a earthen bed,' describing their living conditions.
    • 烟爨不动者往往经于累日 - Means 'the fire did not burn for several days,' indicating a lack of cooking.
    • 设肴羞时有水陆珍异 - Means 'when they prepared dishes, there were often rare delicacies from both land and water,' describing the quality of food.
    • 吴中初进洞庭橘子恩赐宰臣外京辇未有此物 - Means 'the Dongting oranges from Wu were first presented to the chancellor as a favor, and they were not available in the capital,' describing the rarity of the oranges.
    • 密以一枚赠超 - Means 'she secretly gave one to Zhao,' describing a personal gesture.
    • 有人从内中出而禀性刚决 - Means 'a person came out from inside and had a decisive character,' describing a person's personality.
    • 超意甚疑之 - Means 'Zhao was very suspicious of him,' indicating Zhao's suspicion.
    • 如此往来周岁矣 - Means 'they met like this for a whole year,' describing the duration of their interactions.
    • 超一旦防酒食与之从容徐谓之曰 - Means 'one day, Zhao invited him to a feast and spoke to him calmly and slowly,' describing a social interaction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胜业坊北街 - Refers to a specific street in the northern part of Shengye Lane, a location in ancient China.
    • 春雨新霁 - Describes a clear day after a spring rain, a common literary motif in Chinese poetry.
    • 三鬟女子 - Refers to a girl with three buns on her head, a traditional hairstyle for young women.
    • 木屐 - Refers to wooden clogs, a type of footwear common in ancient China.
    • 蹴踘 - Refers to a game similar to modern soccer, a popular sport in ancient China.
    • 洞庭橘子 - Refers to oranges from Dongting Lake, a famous fruit in ancient China.
    • 恩赐宰臣 - Refers to the act of bestowing favors on high-ranking officials, a practice in ancient Chinese governance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 胜业坊北街 - A specific street in ancient China.
    • 洞庭橘子 - Oranges from Dongting Lake, a famous fruit in ancient China.
    • 吴中 - Refers to the region of Wu, a historical area in ancient China.
    • 宰臣 - Refers to high-ranking officials in ancient Chinese governance.

    Alas, this is not a thief who steals openly. Let us try to find out whether it can be obtained. On another day, Zhao happened to pass by the northern street of Shengye Lane, at a time when the spring rain had just cleared. There was a girl with three buns on her head, about seventeen or eighteen years old, dressed in tattered clothes and wearing wooden clogs, standing by the side of the road under a locust tree. A young soldier happened to kick a ball and hit her, reaching several zhang high. Then the onlookers gradually gathered, but Zhao alone found this strange. Afterwards, he followed her, stopping at the short lane near the northern gate of Shengye Lane, where he lived with his mother, as she made a living by sewing. Zhao, being curious, got to know her through other matters, and they became like uncle and nephew, but their living conditions were very poor. They slept together on an earthen bed, and the fire did not burn for several days. When they prepared dishes, there were often rare delicacies from both land and water. The Dongting oranges from Wu were first presented to the chancellor as a favor, and they were not available in the capital. She secretly gave one to Zhao, saying, "There is a person who came out from inside and had a decisive character." Zhao was very suspicious of him. They met like this for a whole year. One day, Zhao invited him to a feast and spoke to him calmly and slowly. [12]

    舅有深诚欲告外甥未知如何女曰每感重恩恨无所答力有可施必能赴汤蹈火超曰潘将军失却玉念珠不知知否女子微笑曰从何知之超揣其意不甚密藏又曰外甥可寻觅厚备缯防酬之女子曰勿言于人某偶与朋侪为戏终却还与因循未暇舅来日诘旦于慈恩寺塔院相某知有人寄珠在此超如期而往时寺门始开塔户犹鏁女子先在谓超曰少顷仰观塔上当有所见语讫而去疾飞鸟忽于相轮上举手示超歘然防珠而下谓超曰便可将还勿以财帛为意超径诣潘具述其事因以金玉缯锦密为之赠明日访之已空室矣 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 深誠 - Deep sincerity or profound truth.
    • 未知如何 - Do not know how.
    • 每感重恩 - Always feel deeply grateful for the great favor.
    • 恨无所答 - Regret not being able to repay it.
    • 力有可施 - If there is an opportunity to exert effort.
    • 必能赴汤蹈火 - One will surely go through fire and water, a metaphor for being willing to risk everything for a cause.
    • 失却 - Lost.
    • 玉念珠 - Precious prayer beads.
    • 不知知否 - Do not know if he knows.
    • 从何知之 - How do you know?
    • 外甥可寻觅厚备缯防酬之 - Nephew should search for and prepare generous gifts to repay the person.
    • 勿言于人 - Do not tell others.
    • 某偶与朋侪为戏终却还与因循未暇 - Occasionally played with friends, eventually returned the item, but due to procrastination or lack of time, it was not returned immediately.
    • 舅来日诘旦于慈恩寺塔院相候 - Uncle will meet at the pagoda courtyard of the Ci'en Temple early the next morning.
    • 某知有人寄珠在此 - Know that someone has left pearls here.
    • 如期而往 - Went as scheduled.
    • 时寺门始开塔户犹锁 - The temple gate was just opening, the pagoda door was still locked.
    • 女子先在谓超曰 - The woman was already there, told Chao.
    • 少顷仰观塔上当有所见 - A short while later, look up at the tower, will see something.
    • 语讫而去 - Finished speaking, and left.
    • 疾如飞鸟 - As fast as a bird.
    • 忽于相轮上举手示超 - Suddenly on the wheel of the stupa, raised her hand to show Chao.
    • 歘然防珠而下 - Suddenly, the pearls came down.
    • 便可将还勿以财帛为意 - Can take them back, do not worry about the value of the pearls.
    • 径诣潘具述其事 - Went directly to General Pan and fully explained the matter.
    • 因以金玉缯锦密为之赠 - Therefore with gold, jade, silk, and brocade, secretly gave as a gift.
    • 明日访之已空室矣 - The next day, when Chao visited, the room was already empty.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 舅 - Refers to the uncle, a close family member.
    • 外甥 - Refers to the nephew, another close family member.
    • 潘将军 - Refers to General Pan, a high-ranking military official.
    • 玉念珠 - Refers to precious prayer beads, often used in religious or ceremonial contexts.
    • 慈恩寺 - Refers to the Ci'en Temple, a famous Buddhist temple in ancient China.
    • 塔院 - Refers to the pagoda courtyard, a part of the temple complex.
    • 相轮 - Refers to the wheel of the stupa, a Buddhist monument.
    • 金玉缯锦 - Refers to valuable materials such as gold, jade, silk, and brocade, often used as gifts or offerings.

    The uncle had a profound truth he wished to tell his nephew, but did not know how to go about it. The girl said, "I always feel deeply grateful for the great favor, yet regret not being able to repay it. If there is an opportunity to exert effort, I will surely go through fire and water." Chao said, "General Pan has lost his precious prayer beads; do you know if he is aware of this?" The girl smiled and asked, "How do you know?" Chao thought she did not keep it very secret. She then said, "Nephew, you can search for and prepare generous gifts to repay him." The girl said, "Do not tell others. I occasionally played with friends and eventually returned the item, but due to procrastination or lack of time, it was not returned immediately." The uncle said, "The next day, early in the morning, meet me at the pagoda courtyard of the Ci'en Temple. I know that someone has left pearls here." Chao went as scheduled. At that time, the temple gate was just opening, and the pagoda door was still locked. The woman was already there and told Chao, "A short while later, look up at the tower, and you will see something." After finishing speaking, she left. She moved as fast as a bird. Suddenly, on the wheel of the stupa, she raised her hand to show Chao. The pearls suddenly came down, and she said to Chao, "Can take them back, do not worry about the value of the pearls." Chao went directly to General Pan and fully explained the matter. He secretly gave gold, jade, silk, and brocade as a gift. The next day, when he visited, the room was already empty. [13]

    冯给事常闻京师多任侠之徒及为尹密询左右引超具述前事访潘将军所説与超符同李邺侯救窦庭芝
    　　寳应年中员外郎窦庭芝分司洛邑常敬事卜者胡卢生每言吉凶无不必中如此者往来甚频长幼莫不倾信一旦凌晨入门颇甚嗟惋庭芝问之良久乃言君家大祸将成举族恐无遗即未在旦夕所期亦甚不逺既而举家涕泣请问求生之路云非遇黄中君谷子不可相救然黄中君造次难见但见谷子当无患矣具述形貎服餙仍约浃旬求之于是窦与兄弟羣从洎妻子奴仆晓夕求访于洛下时李邺侯有内艰居于河清县 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 给事 - A title referring to an official in the Han dynasty, often associated with the Grand Secretariat.
    • 任侠之徒 - Refers to people who follow the path of chivalry or justice, often with a hint of lawlessness.
    • 尹 - A title for a county magistrate or governor.
    • 密询左右 - Means 'secretly inquire of those around him.'
    • 引超具述前事 - Means 'to bring up and fully recount previous events.'
    • 访潘将军所说与超符同 - Means 'to inquire about what General Pan had said, which matched what Cao Xue had recounted.'
    • 救窦庭芝 - Means 'to save Dou Tingzhi.'
    • 宝应年中 - Refers to the year during the Baoying era, a period in the Tang dynasty.
    • 员外郎 - A title for a junior official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 分司洛邑 - Means 'to be stationed in Luoyang.'
    • 敬事卜者胡卢生 - Means 'to respectfully attend to the fortune-teller Hu Lusheng.'
    • 言吉凶无不必中 - Means 'his predictions of good and bad omens were always accurate.'
    • 往来甚频 - Means 'he visited very frequently.'
    • 长幼莫不倾信 - Means 'both young and old had no doubts about him.'
    • 凌晨入门 - Means 'entered the door in the early morning.'
    • 颇甚嗟惋 - Means 'he was quite sorrowful and regretful.'
    • 举族恐无遗类 - Means 'the entire family would likely be wiped out.'
    • 未在旦夕所期 - Means 'not expected to happen immediately.'
    • 举家涕泣 - Means 'the whole family wept.'
    • 请问求生之路 - Means 'to ask for a way to survive.'
    • 非遇黄中君鬼谷子不可相救 - Means 'only by meeting Huangzhong Jun and Zhigu Zi can one be saved.'
    • 然黄中君造次难见 - Means 'however, Huangzhong Jun is difficult to meet casually.'
    • 但见鬼谷子当无患矣 - Means 'but if one meets Zhigu Zi, there will be no danger.'
    • 具述形貌服餙 - Means 'to describe his appearance and attire in detail.'
    • 仍约浃旬求之 - Means 'and agreed to search for him within ten days.'
    • 内艰 - Refers to a period of mourning for a close relative, typically a parent.
    • 河清县 - A place name, the county of Heqing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 给事 - A high-ranking official in the Han dynasty, often involved in state affairs.
    • 任侠之徒 - Refers to individuals who follow the code of chivalry, often with a mix of heroism and lawlessness.
    • 尹 - A local government official, typically a county magistrate.
    • 宝应年中 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Suzong of Tang, known for significant historical events.
    • 员外郎 - A junior official in the Tang dynasty, often involved in administrative duties.
    • 分司洛邑 - Being stationed in Luoyang, a major city and cultural center during the Tang dynasty.
    • 卜者 - Fortune-tellers or diviners, who were respected for their ability to predict the future.
    • 鬼谷子 - A legendary figure in Chinese history, known for his wisdom and strategic thinking, often associated with the art of war and diplomacy.
    • 内艰 - A period of mourning for a close relative, typically a parent, during which officials would temporarily leave their posts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冯给事 - Cao Xue, a high-ranking official in the Han dynasty.
    • 任侠之徒 - Chivalrous people, often with a mix of heroism and lawlessness.
    • 尹 - County magistrate or governor.
    • 宝应年中 - A specific year during the reign of Emperor Suzong of Tang.
    • 员外郎 - Junior official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 窦庭芝 - Dou Tingzhi, a junior official in the Tang dynasty.
    • 胡卢生 - Hu Lusheng, a fortune-teller.
    • 鬼谷子 - Zhigu Zi, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 李邺侯 - Li Yehou, a historical figure known for his actions during the Tang dynasty.
    • 河清县 - The county of Heqing.

    Cao Xue, the Grand Secretariat, often heard that there were many chivalrous people in the capital. When he became a county magistrate, he secretly inquired of those around him and brought up and fully recounted previous events. He then inquired about what General Pan had said, which matched what Cao Xue had recounted. Li Yehou, a historical figure, was known for saving Dou Tingzhi.

    During the Baoying era, the junior official Dou Tingzhi was stationed in Luoyang and often respectfully attended to the fortune-teller Hu Lusheng. Hu Lusheng's predictions of good and bad omens were always accurate, and he visited very frequently. Both young and old had no doubts about him. One day, in the early morning, Hu Lusheng entered the door and was quite sorrowful and regretful. Dou Tingzhi asked him for a long time, and then he said, "Your family will soon face a great disaster, and the entire family will likely be wiped out. It is not expected to happen immediately, but it is not far off." The whole family wept and asked for a way to survive. Hu Lusheng said, "Only by meeting Huangzhong Jun and Zhigu Zi can one be saved. However, Huangzhong Jun is difficult to meet casually. But if one meets Zhigu Zi, there will be no danger." He described his appearance and attire in detail and agreed to search for him within ten days. Thus, Dou Tingzhi and his brothers, relatives, wife, children, and servants searched for Zhigu Zi day and night in Luoyang. At that time, Li Yehou was in mourning for a close relative and lived in Heqing County. [14]

    因省觐亲友策蹇驴入洛至中桥南遇大尹避道所乗驴忽惊逸而走径入庭芝所居与仆者共造其门值庭芝车马罗列将出忽见邺侯皆惊眙而退俄有人出来云此是分司窦员外宅所失驴在马廐请客入座员外尝愿修谒如此者数四邺侯不获已就其防事庭芝既出降堦而拜延接殷勤遂至信宿至如妻孥孩稚咸备家人之礼数日告去赠送殊厚但云贵达之辰愿以一家为托邺侯居于河清信宿旁午于道及朱泚搆逆庭芝方廉
    察陜服车驾出幸奉天遂防于贼庭及銮舆返正德宗首命诛之邺侯自南岳徴廻至行在便为宰相因第臣僚罪状遂请庭芝减死圣意不解云卿以为寜王懿亲乎【庭芝姊为寜王妃】以此论之犹不可然莫有他事俾其全活否卿但言之于是具以前事上闻由是特原其罪邺侯始奏上密使中官夜乘传陜州问之窦奏其事德宗曰曩言黄中君葢指于朕未知呼卿为谷子何也【或云李相先代灵城在清谷前浊谷后恐以此言之】 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 因省觐亲友 - Literally 'because of visiting relatives and friends,' indicating a personal journey.
    • 策蹇驴 - Refers to riding a slow or old donkey, often used to describe a humble or modest mode of travel.
    • 避道 - Means 'to yield the way,' indicating a gesture of respect or deference.
    • 乘驴忽惊逸而走 - Describes a donkey suddenly becoming frightened and running away.
    • 径入庭芝所居 - Means 'directly into the residence of Tingzhi,' indicating an unexpected intrusion.
    • 与仆者共造其门 - Means 'with the servant together visiting his door,' indicating a joint action.
    • 值庭芝车马罗列将出 - Means 'just as Tingzhi's chariots and horses were arranged to depart,' indicating a specific moment in time.
    • 忽见邺侯皆惊眙而退 - Means 'suddenly seeing the Duke of Ye, all were startled and stepped back,' indicating a reaction of surprise and respect.
    • 俄有人出来云 - Means 'soon someone came out and said,' indicating a brief interruption.
    • 此是分司窦员外宅 - Means 'this is the residence of the External Official Dou,' indicating the identity of the owner.
    • 所失驴在马廐 - Means 'the lost donkey is in the stable,' indicating the location of the lost animal.
    • 请客入座 - Means 'invited the guests to sit,' indicating a formal gesture of hospitality.
    • 员外尝愿修谒如此者数四 - Means 'the External Official had wished to pay respects several times like this,' indicating repeated attempts at interaction.
    • 邺侯不获已就其防事 - Means 'the Duke of Ye could not help but attend the gathering,' indicating a reluctant acceptance.
    • 庭芝既出降堦而拜 - Means 'Tingzhi then stepped down from the steps and bowed,' indicating a formal gesture of respect.
    • 延接殷勤 - Means 'received with great hospitality,' indicating warm and generous treatment.
    • 遂至信宿 - Means 'and stayed for two nights,' indicating a prolonged visit.
