    • 至如妻孥孩稚咸备家人之礼数 - Means 'even the wife, children, and grandchildren were treated with the same family courtesy,' indicating comprehensive hospitality.
    • 数日告去 - Means 'after several days, he bid farewell,' indicating the end of the visit.
    • 赠送殊厚 - Means 'gave gifts of great value,' indicating generous parting gifts.
    • 但云贵达之辰愿以一家为托 - Means 'but said that on the day of your promotion, he wishes to entrust his entire family to you,' indicating a request for future assistance.
    • 贵达之辰 - Means 'on the day of your promotion,' indicating a significant event in the future.
    • 愿以一家为托 - Means 'wishes to entrust his entire family to you,' indicating a request for future assistance.
    • 德宗首命诛之 - Means 'Emperor Dezong ordered his execution first,' indicating a severe punishment.
    • 自南岳徵廻至行在 - Means 'after being summoned from the Southern Mountain to the imperial presence,' indicating a return to the emperor.
    • 便为宰相 - Means 'became Chancellor,' indicating a high-ranking position.
    • 因第臣僚罪状 - Means 'because of listing the crimes of officials,' indicating a formal accusation.
    • 遂请庭芝减死 - Means 'then requested that Tingzhi's sentence be commuted to death,' indicating a plea for leniency.
    • 圣意不解 - Means 'the emperor did not understand,' indicating confusion.
    • 卿以为宁王懿亲乎 - Means 'do you think he is a close relative of Prince Ning?' indicating a question about a relationship.
    • 以此论之犹不可然莫有他事俾其全活否 - Means 'even if this is not the case, is there no other reason to allow him to live?' indicating a further inquiry.
    • 卿但言之 - Means 'just tell me,' indicating an invitation to explain.
    • 于是具以前事上闻 - Means 'then he detailed the previous events to the emperor,' indicating a report.
    • 由是特原其罪 - Means 'thus, he was specially pardoned,' indicating a final decision.
    • 密使中官夜乘传陕州问之 - Means 'secretly sent an eunuch by night on a mission to Shaanxi to inquire,' indicating a covert investigation.
    • 曩言黄中君盖指于朕未知呼卿为谷子何也 - Means 'the previous statement about Yellow Middle Lord refers to me, I do not know why you call you Green Valley Son,' indicating a reference to a nickname.
    • 或云李相先代灵城在清谷前浊谷后恐以此言之 - Means 'some say that the former residence of Li Xiang was in Lingcheng, which is before Qing Valley and after Zhuo Valley, perhaps this is what is meant,' indicating a possible explanation for the nickname.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 省觐亲友 - Refers to the act of visiting relatives and friends, a common social practice in ancient China.
    • 蹇驴 - A slow or old donkey, often used to describe a humble mode of travel.
    • 避道 - A gesture of respect or deference, often seen in feudal societies.
    • 庭芝 - Refers to Dou Tingzhi, a historical figure.
    • 邺侯 - Refers to the Duke of Ye, a high-ranking official.
    • 分司窦员外 - Refers to an external official named Dou, who had a specific role in the government.
    • 德宗 - Refers to Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南岳 - Refers to the Southern Mountain, a significant geographical and cultural landmark.
    • 行在 - Refers to the imperial presence or the place where the emperor resides.
    • 宁王 - Refers to Prince Ning, a member of the royal family.
    • 黄中君 - A term that may refer to the emperor himself, as indicated by the context.
    • 谷子 - A nickname for the Duke of Ye, possibly derived from his place of residence.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sequence describes a series of actions: visiting relatives, riding a donkey, encountering a high official, and the donkey running away.
    • This sequence describes the reaction of the servants and the donkey's owner when they see the Duke of Ye.
    • This sequence describes the appearance of someone who identifies the donkey's owner and invites the guests to sit.
    • This sequence describes the repeated attempts of the external official to pay respects and the Duke of Ye's reluctant acceptance.
    • This sequence describes the formal greeting and the extended stay, including the treatment of the external official's family.
    • This sequence describes the parting gifts and the request for future assistance.
    • This sequence describes the political context, the Duke of Ye's intervention, and the emperor's eventual decision to pardon the external official.
    • This sequence describes the covert investigation and the possible explanation for the nickname 'Green Valley Son.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 省觐亲友 - Visiting relatives and friends.
    • 蹇驴 - Slow or old donkey.
    • 避道 - Yielding the way.
    • 庭芝 - Dou Tingzhi.
    • 邺侯 - Duke of Ye.
    • 分司窦员外 - External Official Dou.
    • 德宗 - Emperor Dezong.
    • 南岳 - Southern Mountain.
    • 行在 - Imperial presence.
    • 宁王 - Prince Ning.
    • 黄中君 - Yellow Middle Lord (likely referring to the emperor).
    • 谷子 - Green Valley Son (nickname for the Duke of Ye).

    Because of visiting relatives and friends, he rode a slow donkey into Luoyang and arrived at the south side of Zhong Bridge, where he encountered a high official who yielded the way. The donkey he was riding suddenly became frightened and ran away, directly entering the residence of Dou Tingzhi. With the servant together, they visited his door just as Dou Tingzhi's chariots and horses were arranged to depart. Suddenly seeing the Duke of Ye, all were startled and stepped back. Soon, someone came out and said, "This is the residence of the External Official Dou, and the lost donkey is in the stable." They invited the guests to sit. The External Official had wished to pay respects several times like this, but the Duke of Ye could not help but attend the gathering. Dou Tingzhi then stepped down from the steps and bowed, received with great hospitality, and stayed for two nights. Even the wife, children, and grandchildren were treated with the same family courtesy. After several days, he bid farewell, giving gifts of great value, but said, "On the day of your promotion, he wishes to entrust his entire family to you." The Duke of Ye stayed at Heqing for two nights, traveling back and forth on the road. When Zhu Ci rebelled, Dou Tingzhi was just inspecting Shaanxi. The emperor's carriage went out to Fengtian, and Dou Tingzhi was captured by the rebels. When the imperial carriage returned to the capital, Emperor Dezong first ordered his execution. The Duke of Ye was summoned from the Southern Mountain to the imperial presence and became Chancellor. Because of listing the crimes of officials, he then requested that Dou Tingzhi's sentence be commuted to death. The emperor did not understand and asked, "Do you think he is a close relative of Prince Ning?" [Dou Tingzhi's sister was the wife of Prince Ning] Even if this is not the case, is there no other reason to allow him to live? Just tell me." Then he detailed the previous events to the emperor, and thus, he was specially pardoned. The Duke of Ye then reported to the emperor, who secretly sent an eunuch by night on a mission to Shaanxi to inquire. Dou reported the matter, and Emperor Dezong said, "The previous statement about Yellow Middle Lord refers to me, I do not know why you call you Green Valley Son." [Some say that the former residence of Li Xiang was in Lingcheng, which is before Qing Valley and after Zhuo Valley, perhaps this is what is meant.] [15]

    续坤蹶马
    　　咸通干符中京师医者续坤【坤官为都水使者】颇得秦和之术评脉知吉凶休咎至于得失时日皆可预言【古者善医道多矣迹其前亊不过视彻膏肓心觧分剂未闻乎评诊脉候见于蓍龟之能也】适有燕中奏事大将暴得风众医无不疗之不瘳舁疾请坤投药数服而愈所酬金帛甚多仍以邉马一疋留赠马之骨相甚竒然歩骤多蹶虽制以衔勒加之鞭策而欵段之性竟莫能改坤以浪费刍粟托人以贱价卖之求骏者才试遂复如初累月不售隣伍间有王生贸易于中贵之门颇甚贫窭忽诣坤云有青州监军将发须鞍马以备行李亦知驰骋非骏但欲致于牵控之间坤直以无用之畜付焉亦不约鬻马之价王 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 咸通干符中 - Time period during the Tang Dynasty, specifically the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Yizong (干符).
    • 颇得秦和之术 - He had acquired the skills of Qin He, a renowned physician named Qin He.
    • 评脉知吉凶休咎 - To diagnose by pulse and predict auspicious or inauspicious outcomes.
    • 至于得失时日皆可预言 - Even the gains and losses, and the times and dates, can be foretold.
    • 骨相甚竒 - The horse has a very unique skeletal structure.
    • 歩骤多蹶 - It often stumbles or trips.
    • 欵段之性 - A slow, steady nature, often used to describe a horse that moves at a leisurely pace.
    • 浪费刍粟 - Wasting feed and grain.
    • 托人以贱价卖之 - Entrusting someone to sell it at a low price.
    • 求骏者才试遂复如初 - Those seeking a good horse would try it and find it just as it was before.
    • 累月不售 - It remained unsold for months.
    • 诣坤云 - Came to see Kun and said.
    • 有青州监军将发 - There was a military officer from Qingzhou about to depart.
    • 须鞍马以备行李 - Who needed a horse and saddle for his journey.
    • 亦知驰骋非骏 - He also knew that the horse was not suitable for racing.
    • 但欲致于牵控之间 - But wanted it to be used for pulling and controlling.
    • 直以无用之畜付焉 - He simply gave it away as a useless animal.
    • 亦不约鬻马之价 - He did not set a price for selling the horse.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸通干符中 - The time period during the Tang Dynasty, known for its cultural and literary achievements.
    • 秦和之术 - Refers to the medical skills of Qin He, a famous physician in Chinese history.
    • 评脉知吉凶休咎 - A traditional Chinese medical practice where pulse diagnosis is used to predict health outcomes.
    • 骨相甚竒 - Refers to the belief in the importance of bone structure in determining the characteristics of a horse.
    • 欵段之性 - A term used to describe a horse's temperament and gait.
    • 浪费刍粟 - Reflects the economic considerations of feeding and maintaining a horse.
    • 托人以贱价卖之 - A common practice in ancient China where intermediaries were used to sell goods at lower prices.
    • 求骏者才试遂复如初 - Describes the disappointment of those seeking a good horse when they find the horse unsuitable.
    • 累月不售 - Indicates the difficulty in selling the horse due to its poor performance.
    • 邻伍间有王生 - Refers to a local resident named Wang.
    • 贸易于中贵之门 - Indicates the social status and connections of Wang, who traded at the door of a powerful official.
    • 颇甚贫窭 - Describes Wang's financial situation, emphasizing his poverty.
    • 诣坤云 - Refers to Wang visiting Kun to discuss the horse.
    • 有青州监军将发 - Refers to a military officer from Qingzhou, indicating a specific geographical and social context.
    • 须鞍马以备行李 - Describes the practical needs of a traveler.
    • 亦知驰骋非骏 - Reflects the realistic assessment of the horse's capabilities.
    • 但欲致于牵控之间 - Indicates the intended use of the horse for pulling and controlling, rather than racing.
    • 直以无用之畜付焉 - Describes the horse as being considered useless.
    • 亦不约鬻马之价 - Indicates the lack of a formal agreement or price for the horse.

    Proper Nouns

    • 咸通 - The reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 干符 - The reign title of Emperor Yizong of Tang.
    • 续坤 - The name of the main character, a physician.
    • 秦和 - A renowned physician in Chinese history.
    • 燕中 - A region in northern China.
    • 青州 - A historical region in China.
    • 监军 - A military officer.
    • 王生 - The name of a local resident.

    During the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (Suantong) and Emperor Yizong (Ganfu), there was a physician in the capital named Xun Kun, who held the title of Director of Waterways. He had acquired the skills of Qin He, a renowned physician, and could diagnose by pulse to predict auspicious or inauspicious outcomes, even the gains and losses, and the times and dates, could be foretold. One day, a high-ranking official from Yanzhong, who was about to present matters to the court, suddenly fell ill with a severe wind disease. Many doctors treated him without success. The official was carried to Kun, who prescribed a few doses of medicine, and the official recovered. The official rewarded Kun with a large amount of gold and silk, and also gave him a horse as a gift. The horse had a very unique skeletal structure but often stumbled and tripped, despite being controlled with a bit and bridle and spurred with a whip, its steady and slow nature could not be changed. Kun, considering the horse wasted feed and grain, entrusted someone to sell it at a low price. Those seeking a good horse would try it and find it just as it was before, and it remained unsold for months. A man named Wang, who lived nearby and traded at the door of a powerful official, was quite poor. He came to see Kun and said, "There is a military officer from Qingzhou about to depart and needs a horse and saddle for his journey. He also knows that the horse is not suitable for racing but wants it to be used for pulling and controlling." Kun simply gave it away as a useless animal and did not set a price for selling the horse. Wang took the horse and was satisfied. [16]

    生经旬不至谓其脱畧亡逸一旦复来且输十万坤既获善价因以十千遗之俄见王生易衣装置仆马至于奴婢妻子服餙皆鲜防或曰王生卖马金帛缣资几三四百万坤甚惊试询其事王生初不备説坤曰某以无用之畜获价颇多但惊驽劣之材何以至此乃云初致马于青社监军举足如有羁绊及将还途遇小马坊中使因遣留试信宿而往不复见焉密询左右云数日前魏博曽进一马毛骨大小与此正同圣人常乘打毬骏异未有其偶将到日方遣调习歩骤萦转如风今则进御数朝所赐之物甚厚王生因大索其价遂以四十万酬之是以物之逢时亦有防数不遇其主则驽骥莫分乃知耨莘野筑傅岩未遇良途奚异于此 [17]

    Translation: For a long time, the person did not come, and it was thought that the horse had been neglected or lost. Suddenly, he returned and paid ten thousand. After obtaining a good price, he gave ten thousand to him. Soon, he saw Wang Sheng change clothes and prepare his servant and horse. Even the servants, wives, and children were well-dressed. Someone said that Wang Sheng sold the horse for gold, silk, and cloth worth several million, and the speaker was very surprised. He asked about the matter, but Wang Sheng did not fully explain. The speaker said, "I got a lot of money for an animal that was not very useful, but I am surprised that such a poor horse could be worth so much." Wang Sheng said that when he first brought the horse to the military governor of Qing She, the horse seemed to be restrained. When he was returning, he met a minor official from the horse stable who asked him to stay for two nights and then did not see him again. He secretly asked his attendants, who said that several days ago Wei Bo had presented a horse with the same size and appearance. The emperor often rides a swift horse for polo, and there is no match for it. It was only sent to be trained when it arrived, and it moved like the wind. Now it has been presented to the emperor, and the gifts from the emperor are very generous. Wang Sheng then demanded a large sum and paid forty thousand. Thus, things that are fortunate in their time have their own fate; if they do not meet their master, even good and bad horses cannot be distinguished. Thus, we know that even the fields of Nuo and the rock of Fu, if they do not meet the right path, are no different. [17]

    龙待诏相笏【丁重相于驸马附】
    　　开成中有龙复本者无目善听声揣骨每言休咎无不必中凡有象简竹笏以手捻之必知官禄年寿宋祁阙有盛名于世搢绅之士靡不倾属屈指翘足期于贵达时永乐萧相亦居谏署同日诣之授以所持竹笏复本执萧相笏良乆置于案上曰宰相笏次至宋阙笏曰长官笏宋闻之不乐相国曰无慿之言安足介意经月余同列于中书见宰相时李朱崖方秉钧轴威震朝野未见间伫立闲谈互有谐谑顷之丞相遽出宋以手板障面笑犹未已朱崖目之谓左右曰宋阙笑某何事闻之者莫不寒心股栗未旬日出为河清县令岁余遂终所任其后萧相杨歴清途自浙西观察使入判户部未乆乃居廊庙俱如复本之言 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 開成 - Reign title of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the events.
    • 善聽聲揣骨 - Literally 'good at hearing sounds and divining bones,' meaning skilled in fortune-telling through divination methods.
    • 休咎 - Refers to auspicious and inauspicious signs, indicating predictions of good or bad fortune.
    • 無不必中 - Means 'without fail, all are accurate,' emphasizing the accuracy of the fortune-teller's predictions.
    • 象簡竹笏 - Refers to symbols of official rank, such as ivory tablets and bamboo tablets, which were used by officials.
    • 捻之 - Means 'to pinch or squeeze,' referring to the action of handling the tablets.
    • 官禄年寿 - Refers to one's official position, salary, and lifespan.
    • 搢绅之士 - Refers to scholars or officials, indicating those in high social or official positions.
    • 靡不倾属 - Means 'all are eager and attentive,' describing the widespread interest in the fortune-teller's predictions.
    • 屈指翘足期于贵达 - Means 'counting on their fingers and toes, hoping for high rank and success,' indicating the high hopes people had for their fortunes.
    • 永乐萧相 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Xiao, indicating a prominent figure in the court.
    • 谏署 - Refers to the office of censors, indicating a position in the imperial court responsible for advising and criticizing the emperor.
    • 置于案上 - Means 'placed on the table,' describing the action of putting down the tablet.
    • 宰相笏 - Refers to the tablet of a chancellor, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 长官笏 - Refers to the tablet of a high-ranking official, indicating a similar but slightly lower rank.
    • 无凭之言安足介意 - Means 'empty words are not worth worrying about,' indicating that the fortune-teller's prediction should not be taken too seriously.
    • 经月余 - Means 'after several months,' indicating a significant passage of time.
    • 同列于中书 - Refers to being in the same office in the central government, indicating a high-ranking position.
    • 李朱崖 - Refers to Li Deyu, a powerful chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 威震朝野 - Means 'awe-inspiring in the court and the countryside,' indicating Li Deyu's significant influence.
    • 伫立闲谈互有谐谑 - Means 'standing idly and chatting, with some joking and teasing,' describing a relaxed and informal atmosphere.
    • 遽出 - Means 'suddenly left,' indicating a sudden departure.
    • 以手板障面 - Means 'covering his face with his hand,' indicating embarrassment or discomfort.
    • 杨歴清途 - Means 'ascending the clear path,' indicating a successful career.
    • 自浙西观察使入判户部 - Means 'from the position of governor of Zhe West to the head of the Ministry of Revenue,' indicating a promotion.
    • 乃居廊庙 - Means 'then became a high-ranking official,' indicating a further advancement in his career.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 龙复本 - A renowned fortune-teller during the Tang Dynasty, known for his accuracy in predictions.
    • 宋祁 - A prominent scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary works.
    • 萧相 - A high-ranking official named Xiao, indicating a prominent figure in the court.
    • 李朱崖 - Refers to Li Deyu, a powerful chancellor during the Tang Dynasty, known for his influence and authority.
    • 永乐 - A reign title of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the events.
    • 开成 - A reign title of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the events.
    • 象简竹笏 - Symbols of official rank, such as ivory tablets and bamboo tablets, which were used by officials.
    • 搢绅之士 - Scholars or officials, indicating those in high social or official positions.
    • 永乐萧相 - A high-ranking official named Xiao, indicating a prominent figure in the court.
    • 谏署 - The office of censors, indicating a position in the imperial court responsible for advising and criticizing the emperor.
    • 宰相 - Chancellor, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 长官 - High-ranking official, indicating a similar but slightly lower rank.
    • 李朱崖 - Li Deyu, a powerful chancellor during the Tang Dynasty, known for his influence and authority.
    • 中书 - The central government, indicating a high-ranking position.
    • 浙西观察使 - Governor of Zhe West, indicating a regional administrative position.
    • 户部 - Ministry of Revenue, indicating a central government department.
    • 廊庙 - High-ranking officials, indicating a position of significant influence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 龙复本 - A renowned fortune-teller during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宋祁 - A prominent scholar and official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 萧相 - A high-ranking official named Xiao.
    • 李朱崖 - Refers to Li Deyu, a powerful chancellor during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 永乐 - A reign title of Emperor Wenzong of Tang.
    • 开成 - A reign title of Emperor Wenzong of Tang.

    During the Kai Cheng era, there was a fortune-teller named Long Fuben who was blind but skilled in hearing sounds and divining bones. He made predictions about auspicious and inauspicious signs without fail. Whenever he handled symbols of official rank, such as ivory tablets and bamboo tablets, he could predict one's official position, salary, and lifespan. Cui Qi was renowned in the world for his literary achievements, and scholars and officials were all eager and attentive. They counted on their fingers and toes, hoping for high rank and success. At that time, a high-ranking official named Xiao, also in the office of censors, visited him on the same day and presented him with the bamboo tablet he was holding. Dragon Fuben held the high-ranking official Xiao's tablet for a long time and placed it on the table, saying it was a chancellor's tablet. Next, he took Cui Qi's tablet and said it was a high-ranking official's tablet. Cui Qi heard this and was not happy. The chancellor said, "Empty words are not worth worrying about." After several months, when they were waiting to see the chancellor, Li Deyu, who was wielding power and awe-inspiring in the court and the countryside, suddenly left. Cui Qi covered his face with his hand, still laughing, and Li Deyu looked at him. Those who heard about it were all filled with fear and trembling. Within ten days, he was appointed as the magistrate of Heqing County and died shortly after. Later, the high-ranking official Xiao ascended the clear path, from the position of governor of Zhe West to the head of the Ministry of Revenue, and then became a high-ranking official, all as predicted by Dragon Fuben. [18]

    自咸通干符已来京国察相者殊多言事适中者甚少愚之所识处士丁重善于相人吉凶屡有奇验于都尉方判盐铁频有宰弼之耗时路相国秉钧持权与之不叶一旦重在新昌私第值于公适至路曰某与之賔朋处士垂箔细看此人终作宰相否备陈饮馔留连数刻既去问之曰所见何如重曰入相必矣兼在旬月之内路公笑曰见是帝王密亲复作盐铁使尔重曰不然请问于之恩泽何如宣宗 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自咸通干符已来 - Time phrase meaning 'since the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (805-820) and Emperor Wenzong (826-840), referring to the late Tang Dynasty.
    • 京国察相者 - Refers to fortune-tellers or face readers in the capital.
    • 殊多言事适中者甚少 - Means 'there are many who make predictions, but few are accurate.'
    • 愚之所识 - A modest self-reference meaning 'what I have heard or known.'
    • 处士丁重 - Refers to a scholar named Ding Zhong.
    • 善于相人吉凶 - Means 'skilled in predicting auspicious and inauspicious events.'
    • 屡有奇验 - Means 'frequently has remarkable accuracy.'
    • 于都尉方判盐铁 - Refers to a high-ranking official who was in charge of salt and iron administration.
    • 频有宰弼之耗 - Means 'frequently had news of becoming a prime minister.'
    • 路相国秉钧持权 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Lu, who held significant power.
    • 与之不叶 - Means 'was not in harmony with him.'
    • 垂箔细看 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'carefully observing through a curtain.'
    • 入相必矣 - Means 'he will certainly become a chancellor.'
    • 兼在旬月之内 - Means 'within a few months.'
    • 帝王密亲 - Refers to close relatives of the emperor.
    • 盐铁使尔 - Refers to the position of Salt and Iron Commissioner.
    • 宣宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a previous emperor.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸通干符 - Refers to the reigns of Emperor Xianzong and Emperor Wenzong, indicating the late Tang Dynasty.
    • 京国 - Refers to the capital city, Chang'an.
    • 察相 - Refers to the practice of fortune-telling or face reading.
    • 处士 - Refers to a scholar who has not entered officialdom.
    • 宰弼 - Refers to high-ranking officials, particularly the prime minister.
    • 盐铁使 - Refers to a high-ranking official in charge of salt and iron administration.
    • 宣宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for his cultural and military achievements.

    Proper Nouns

    • 咸通干符 - The reigns of Emperor Xianzong and Emperor Wenzong.
    • 京国 - The capital city, Chang'an.
    • 处士丁重 - A scholar named Ding Zhong.
    • 于都尉 - A high-ranking official in charge of salt and iron administration.
    • 路相国 - A high-ranking official named Lu.
    • 宣宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.

    Since the reigns of Emperor Xianzong and Emperor Wenzong, there have been many fortune-tellers in the capital, but very few of their predictions have been accurate. What I have heard is that the scholar Ding Zhong is skilled in predicting auspicious and inauspicious events, and he has frequently had remarkable accuracy. At the time, a high-ranking official in charge of salt and iron administration frequently had news of becoming a prime minister. The high-ranking official Lu held significant power and was not in harmony with him. One day, Ding Zhong was at his private residence in Xinchang, and the official happened to visit. Lu said, "I am a friend of his. Could you please observe him carefully through the curtain and tell me if he will eventually become a chancellor?" Ding Zhong prepared a feast and stayed for several hours. After he left, Lu asked, "What did you see?" Ding Zhong replied, "He will certainly become a chancellor, and it will happen within a few months." Lu laughed and said, "He is a close relative of the emperor and will become a Salt and Iron Commissioner." Ding Zhong disagreed and asked, "How does he compare to Emperor Xuanzong's favor?" [19]

    朝郑都尉相国曰又安可比乎重曰郑为宣宗注意久之而竟不为相岂将人事可以斟酌某熟识于侍郎今日见之观其骨状真为贵者其次风仪秀整礼貌谦揖如百斛巨器所贮尚空其半安使不受益于禄位哉茍逾月不居廊庙某无复敢至门下路曰处士可谓逺矣其后浃旬果登台路相国每见朝士大为称赏由兹声动京邑车马造门者甚众凡有所説其言皆验后居终南山好事者亦至其所
    　　孟才人善歌
    　　孟才人善歌有宠于武宗皇帝嫔御之中莫与为比一旦龙体不豫召而问曰我不讳汝将何之对曰以微眇之身受君王之宠陛下万岁之后无复生焉是日俾于御榻前歌河满子一曲声调凄切闻者莫不涕零及宫车晏驾哀恸数日而殒禁掖近臣以小棺殡于殿侧山陵之际梓宫重莫能举识者曰得非候才人乎于是舆榇以殉遂于端陵之侧是岁攻文之士或为赋题或为诗目以为冯媛班姬无以过也所知者张祜有诗云偶因清唱咏歌频奏入宫中二十春却为一声河满子下泉须吊孟才人 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 又安可比乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how can it be compared?' or 'how can it be equated?'
    • 重曰 - Means 'again said' or 'replied,' indicating a continuation of the dialogue.
    • 而竟不為相 - Emphasizes the contrast between expectation and outcome, meaning 'but ultimately did not become a chancellor.'
    • 豈將人事可以斟酌 - A rhetorical question expressing skepticism, meaning 'how can it be that human affairs can be measured or judged?'
    • 某熟識於侍郎今日見之 - Indicates a personal connection, meaning 'today I saw him, well acquainted with the Censorate.'
    • 觀其骨狀真為貴者 - A physical and moral assessment, meaning 'to observe his bone structure, truly of noble character.'
    • 其次風儀秀整 - Describes his appearance and demeanor, meaning 'next, his bearing is elegant and upright.'
    • 禮貌謙揖 - Describes his behavior, meaning 'courteous and humble.'
    • 如百斛巨器所貯尚空其半 - A metaphor for potential, meaning 'like a large vessel of a hundred measures, still half empty.'
    • 安使不受益於祿位哉 - A rhetorical question expressing the inevitability of success, meaning 'how can it be that not benefit from rank and position?'
    • 苟逾月不居廊廟 - A conditional statement, meaning 'if more than a month without holding office in the imperial court.'
    • 某無復敢至門下 - Expresses a sense of humility and respect, meaning 'no longer dare to come to your door.'
    • 處士可谓弘逺矣 - A compliment, meaning 'a recluse scholar can be called broad and far-reaching.'
    • 其后浃旬果登台閣 - Describes a rapid rise in status, meaning 'afterwards, indeed ascended to high office within ten days.'
    • 路相国每见朝士大为称赏 - Describes the reaction of officials, meaning 'the Censorate always highly praised the officials.'
    • 由兹声动京邑 - Describes the impact, meaning 'from this, echoes through the capital city.'
    • 车马造门者甚众 - Describes the influx of visitors, meaning 'carriages and horses came to the door, very many.'
    • 凡有所説其言皆验 - Describes the accuracy of his predictions, meaning 'whatever is said, his words all come true.'
    • 后居终南山好事者亦至其所 - Describes the continued interest in him, meaning 'later lived in the end of the mountain, people who are interested also came to his place.'
    • 召而问曰我疾不讳汝将何之 - Describes the emperor's question, meaning 'called and asked, my illness is serious, where will you go?'
    • 对曰以微眇之身受君王之宠陛下万岁之后无复生焉 - Describes her response, meaning 'replied, with my humble body enjoying the emperor's favor, after the emperor's death, will not live again.'
    • 是日俾于御榻前歌河满子一曲 - Describes the event, meaning 'that day ordered to sing the song 'He Manzi' in front of the emperor's bed.'
    • 声调凄切闻者莫不涕零 - Describes the emotional impact, meaning 'the tone was sad and poignant, those who heard all shed tears.'
    • 及宫车晏驾哀恸数日而殒禁掖 - Describes the aftermath, meaning 'when the emperor's death, mourned for several days and died in the palace.'
    • 近臣以小棺殡于殿侧 - Describes the burial, meaning 'close attendants buried with a small coffin beside the palace.'
    • 山陵之际梓宫重莫能举 - Describes the difficulty, meaning 'during the burial, the emperor's coffin was heavy and could not be lifted.'
    • 识者曰得非候才人乎 - Describes the speculation, meaning 'those who knew said, is it not the concubine?'
    • 于是舆榇以殉遂窆于端陵之侧 - Describes the final burial, meaning 'then carried the coffin to accompany the emperor, buried beside the emperor's tomb.'
    • 是岁攻文之士或为赋题或为诗目以为冯媛班姬无以过也 - Describes the literary response, meaning 'that year, literary scholars some wrote poems, some wrote titles, considered none could surpass her.'
    • 所知者张祜有诗云偶因清唱咏歌频奏入宫中二十春却为一声河满子下泉须吊孟才人 - Describes the poem, meaning 'those who knew, Zhang Hu wrote a poem saying, occasionally because of clear singing, often played in the palace for twenty years, then for a song 'He Manzi', the spring needs to mourn Sheng Cairen.'

    The Chancellor of the Court, Viceroy of Zheng, said, "How can it be compared?" The Viceroy replied, "Zheng was highly regarded by Emperor Xuan for a long time, yet ultimately did not become a chancellor. How could it be that human affairs can be measured? I am well acquainted with the Censorate; today I saw him. Observing his bone structure, he is truly of noble character. Next, his bearing is elegant and upright, his manners courteous and respectful. He is like a large vessel of a hundred measures, still half empty. How can it be that he does not benefit from rank and position? If he does not hold office for more than a month, I dare not come to your door." The recluse scholar said, "You can be called broad and far-reaching." Afterwards, within ten days, he indeed ascended to high office. The Viceroy of the Road always highly praised officials when he saw them, and from this, his fame echoed through the capital city. Many carriages and horses came to his door, and whatever he said, his words all came true. Later, he lived on Zhongnan Mountain, and people interested in him also came to his place.

    The Shangcai Woman was good at singing. She was favored among the concubines of Emperor Wu of the Tang Dynasty, and no one could compare to her. One day, when the emperor's health was not well, he called her and asked, "If my illness is serious, where will you go?" She replied, "With my humble body, enjoying the emperor's favor, after the emperor's death, I will not live again." That day, she was ordered to sing the song "He Manzi" in front of the emperor's bed. Her tone was sad and poignant, and all who heard her shed tears. When the emperor died, she mourned for several days and died in the palace. Close attendants buried her with a small coffin beside the palace. During the burial, the emperor's coffin was too heavy to be lifted. Those who knew said, "Is it not the concubine?" Then, they carried her coffin to accompany the emperor and buried her beside the emperor's tomb. That year, literary scholars wrote poems or titles, considering her none could surpass the historical figures Feng Yuan and Ban Ji. Those who knew, Zhang Hu, wrote a poem saying, "Occasionally, due to clear singing and frequent performances in the palace for twenty springs, she sang 'He Manzi' once, and in the netherworld, one must mourn the Shangcai Woman." [20]

    袁相雪换金县令
    　　李汧公鎭凤翔有属邑编甿因耨田得马蹄金一瓮【注汉书武帝诏云往者东岳见金又有白麟神马之瑞宜以黄金铸麟状以叶瑞应葢铸金象马蹄之状其后民间效之】里民送于县署防牒将至府庭宰邑者喜于获寳欲自以为殊绩虑公藏主守不严因使置于私室信宿与官吏重开视之则皆为土块矣瓮金出土之际乡社悉来观騐遽为变更靡不惊骇以状闻于府主议者佥云奸计换之遂遣理曹椽与军吏数人就鞫案其事获金之社咸共证焉宰邑者为众所挤摧沮莫能自白既而诎辱滋甚遂以为易金伏罪词欵具存未穷 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 袁相雪换金县令 - Title meaning 'Commander Yuan Xue, the gold-changing magistrate of Jinyang County' or 'Commander Yuan Xue, the magistrate of Jinyang County who changed gold.'
    • 因耨田得马蹄金一瓮 - Literal phrase meaning 'because of plowing the fields, a jar of horse-shoe-shaped gold was found.'
    • 宰邑者喜于获寳 - Formal phrase meaning 'the county magistrate was delighted to have found a treasure.'
    • 欲自以为殊绩 - Formal phrase meaning 'wished to claim this as a special achievement.'
    • 虑公藏主守不严 - Formal phrase meaning 'worried that the public treasury was not strictly guarded.'
    • 因使置于私室 - Formal phrase meaning 'therefore, he placed it in his private room.'
    • 信宿与官吏重开视之 - Formal phrase meaning 'after a few days, he opened it again with the officials.'
    • 则皆为土块矣 - Formal phrase meaning 'but it was all turned into clay blocks.'
    • 瓮金出土之际 - Formal phrase meaning 'when the gold from the jar was unearthed.'
    • 乡社悉来观騐 - Formal phrase meaning 'all the villagers came to inspect it.'
    • 遽为变更 - Formal phrase meaning 'it was suddenly changed.'
    • 靡不惊骇 - Formal phrase meaning 'no one was not shocked.'
    • 以状闻于府主 - Formal phrase meaning 'he reported the situation to the governor.'
    • 议者佥云奸计换之 - Formal phrase meaning 'the advisors all said it was a trick to swap it.'
    • 遂遣理曹椽与军吏数人就鞫案其事 - Formal phrase meaning 'they then sent a judge and several military officers to investigate the matter.'
    • 获金之社咸共证焉 - Formal phrase meaning 'the village that found the gold all testified against him.'
    • 宰邑者为众所挤摧沮 - Formal phrase meaning 'the county magistrate was overwhelmed and humiliated by the crowd.'
    • 莫能自白 - Formal phrase meaning 'could not clear his name.'
    • 既而诎辱滋甚 - Formal phrase meaning 'and his humiliation grew even worse.'
    • 遂以为易金伏罪 - Formal phrase meaning 'he was thus convicted of swapping the gold.'
    • 词欵具存 - Formal phrase meaning 'the evidence was all present.'
    • 未穷 - Formal phrase meaning 'but the investigation was not yet complete.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 李汧公 - Refers to Li Qian, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 凤翔 - Refers to Fengxiang, a city in Shaanxi Province, which was an important administrative center during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 马蹄金 - A specific type of gold artifact, often associated with ancient Chinese rituals or treasures. It is shaped like a horse's hoof and is often found in tombs or as offerings.
    • 县令 - Refers to the magistrate of a county, a local administrative official.
    • 府庭 - Refers to the governor's office or the main administrative building.
    • 公藏 - Refers to the public treasury or official storage.
    • 理曹椽 - Refers to a judge or legal official.
    • 军吏 - Refers to military officers.
    • 鞫案 - Refers to an investigation or legal inquiry.

    Proper Nouns

    • 袁相雪 - The name of the commander or magistrate.
    • 李汧公 - The name of the high-ranking official Li Qian.
    • 凤翔 - The name of the city Fengxiang.
    • 马蹄金 - The name of the specific type of gold artifact.
    • 县令 - The title of the magistrate of a county.
    • 府庭 - The name of the governor's office or main administrative building.
    • 公藏 - The name of the public treasury or official storage.
    • 理曹椽 - The title of a judge or legal official.
    • 军吏 - The title of military officers.
    • 鞫案 - The term for an investigation or legal inquiry.

    Commander Yuan Xue, the Magistrate of Jinyang County

    During the time when Li Qian, a high-ranking official, was stationed in Fengxiang, a subordinate county magistrate and his people were plowing the fields and discovered a jar of horse-shoe-shaped gold. [Note: According to the Book of Han, Emperor Wu of Han had previously issued an edict stating that the appearance of gold in the east and the sighting of a white unicorn and divine horse were auspicious signs. It was suggested that gold should be cast into the shape of a unicorn to match these omens, and later, the common people followed this practice.] The villagers delivered the gold to the county office, and the report was about to be submitted to the governor's office. The county magistrate was delighted to have found a treasure and wished to claim this as a special achievement. Worried that the public treasury was not strictly guarded, he placed it in his private room. After a few days, he opened it again with the officials, but it was all turned into clay blocks. When the gold from the jar was unearthed, all the villagers came to inspect it, and it was suddenly changed. No one was not shocked, and he reported the situation to the governor. The advisors all said it was a trick to swap it. They then sent a judge and several military officers to investigate the matter. The village that found the gold all testified against him. The county magistrate was overwhelmed and humiliated by the crowd and could not clear his name. And his humiliation grew even worse. He was thus convicted of swapping the gold, and the evidence was all present, but the investigation was not yet complete. [21]

    用之所遂令拘系仆胁以刑辟或云藏于粪壤或云投于水中纷纭枉挠结成狱具备以词案上闻汧公览之愈怒俄而因有筵席停杯语及斯事列坐賔客咸共惊叹或云效齐人之攫或云有杨震之癖谈笑移时以为胠箧穿窬无足讶也时袁相公滋亦在幕中俛首略无词对李公目之数四曰宰邑非判官亲懿乎袁相曰与之无素李曰闻彼之罪何不乐之甚袁相曰某疑此事未了更请相国详之汧公曰换金之状极明言未了当别有见非判官莫探情伪袁相曰诺因俾移狱府中按问乃令阅瓮间得三十五块诘其初获者即本质在焉 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 用之所遂令拘系仆胁以刑辟 - Complex phrase indicating that someone used their power to detain and threaten a subordinate with legal punishment.
    • 藏于粪壤 - Euphemism for hiding something in a dirty or hidden place.
    • 投于水中 - Euphemism for drowning or disposing of something in water.
    • 纷纭枉挠 - Describes the confusion and unfairness of the situation.
    • 结成狱具备以词案上闻 - Formulated into a case, prepared, and presented to higher authorities.
    • 谈笑移时 - Laughed and chatted for a while, indicating a period of casual conversation.
    • 以为胠箧穿窬无足讶也 - Not worth being surprised about, referring to theft.
    • 换金之状极明 - The situation is very clear, suggesting that the facts of the case are obvious.
    • 当别有见非判官莫探情伪 - There must be another perspective, only the judge can uncover the truth.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汧公 - Refers to a high-ranking official, possibly a duke or governor, during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 袁相公滋 - Refers to a high-ranking official, possibly a chancellor, named 'Zi'.
    • 杨震 - A famous Han dynasty official known for his integrity and refusal to accept bribes.
    • 齐人之攫 - Refers to a story from the *Zuo Zhuan* where a man from Qi tried to steal a cook's pot, symbolizing greed or thievery.
    • 有杨震之癖 - Refers to a story about Yang Zhen, a Han dynasty official known for his integrity, who refused a bribe. Here, it likely means a similar act of integrity or honesty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汧公 - A high-ranking official, likely a duke or governor.
    • 袁相公滋 - A high-ranking official, possibly a chancellor, named 'Zi'.
    • 杨震 - A famous Han dynasty official known for his integrity and refusal to accept bribes.

    The official used his power to order the detention and threatening of a servant or subordinate with legal punishment. Some said he was hidden in filth, others said he was thrown into water. The situation was chaotic and unjust, and a case was formulated and presented to higher authorities. The duke viewed the case and became even angrier. Soon after, there was a banquet where the guests stopped drinking and talked about the matter. All the seated guests expressed amazement, some saying it was like the man from Qi trying to steal a cook's pot, others saying it was like Yang Zhen's integrity. They laughed and chatted for a while, thinking that theft was not worth being surprised about. At the time, Chancellor Yuan Zi was also in the hall, bowing his head with no words. The official looked at him several times and said, "Is the mayor not a close relative or ally of the judge?" Chancellor Yuan said, "I have no prior relationship with him." The official said, "Why are you not happy about his crime?" Chancellor Yuan said, "I suspect this matter is not yet clear; please ask the chancellor to investigate further." The duke said, "The situation of the gold exchange is very clear. There must be another perspective; only the judge can uncover the truth." Chancellor Yuan said, "Yes," and ordered the case to be moved to the government office for investigation. He then ordered the examination of a jar, where thirty-five pieces were found. When questioned about the first piece obtained, it was confirmed to be the original. [22]

    遂于列肆索金镕写与块形相等既成始秤其半已及三百斤矣询其负担人力二农工讵中舁至县境计其负金大数非二人以竹担可举明其即路之时金已化为土矣于是羣情大豁宰邑者遂获清雪汧公叹伏无已每言才智不如其后履歴清途至德宗朝皆为宰相愚尝闻金宝藏于土偶见者或变其质东都敦化坊有麟迹见于兴庆观殿宇悉皆颓毁咸通中毕相国再令营造基址间得巨瓮皆贮白金理财者与工匠三十三人尽惧为官所取乃辇木梯葢之以昏黑及夜各以衣物苞裹而归明旦开之如坚土削成银铤所説与此正同 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 遂于列肆索金镕写与块形相等 - Complex phrase meaning 'he then searched among the shops for gold to cast into ingots of the same shape as the blocks.'
    • 既成始秤其半已及三百斤矣 - Means 'once completed, he weighed half of it, which already amounted to three hundred pounds.'
    • 讵中舁至县境 - Means 'but in the middle of carrying it to the county boundary.'
    • 计其负金大数非二人以竹担可举 - Means 'calculating the total amount of gold, it was not something two people could carry on bamboo poles.'
    • 明其即路之时金已化为土矣 - Means 'it became clear that by the time they started on the road, the gold had already turned to dust.'
    • 羣情大豁 - Means 'the public's anxiety was greatly relieved.'
    • 宰邑者遂获清雪 - Means 'the county magistrate was thus cleared of suspicion.'
    • 汧公叹伏无已 - Means 'the Marquis of Qian was deeply impressed and could not stop praising.'
    • 每言才智不如其后履歴清途 - Means 'he often said that his intelligence and ability were inferior to those who followed him on the path of officialdom.'
    • 至德宗朝皆为宰相 - Means 'by the time of Emperor Dezong, they had all become chancellors.'
    • 愚尝闻金宝藏于土偶见者或变其质 - Means 'I have heard that gold hidden in the earth, when discovered, sometimes changes its form.'
    • 东都敦化坊有麟迹见于兴庆观殿宇悉皆颓毁 - Means 'in Dongdu's Dunhua Ward, there was a trace of a qilin (a mythical creature) seen in the dilapidated halls of Xingqing Temple.'
    • 咸通中毕相国再令营造基址间得巨瓮皆贮白金 - Means 'during the Xiantong period, Duke Bi ordered the reconstruction, and among the foundations, large jars containing white gold were found.'
    • 理财者与工匠三十三人尽惧为官所取 - Means 'the financial officers and thirty-three craftsmen were all afraid that the gold would be taken by the authorities.'
    • 乃辇木梯葢之以昏黑 - Means 'they then used wooden ladders to cover it and waited for it to become dark.'
    • 及夜各以衣物苞裹而归 - Means 'at night, each wrapped the gold in clothing and took it home.'
    • 明旦开之如坚土削成银铤 - Means 'the next morning, when opened, it was like hard soil cut into silver ingots.'
    • 所说与此正同 - Means 'what is said here is exactly the same.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 列肆 - Refers to shops or market stalls.
    • 金镕 - Refers to molten gold.
    • 块形 - Refers to the shape of blocks.
    • 宰邑者 - Refers to the county magistrate.
    • 汧公 - Refers to the Marquis of Qian, a high-ranking official.
    • 德宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 宰相 - Refers to the highest-ranking officials in the imperial government.
    • 东都 - Refers to the eastern capital, Luoyang.
    • 敦化坊 - Refers to a specific ward in Luoyang.
    • 兴庆观 - Refers to a temple in Luoyang.
    • 咸通中 - Refers to the Xiantong period of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 毕相国 - Refers to Duke Bi, a high-ranking official.
    • 理财者 - Refers to financial officers.
    • 工匠 - Refers to craftsmen.

    Proper Nouns

    • 列肆 - Shops or market stalls.
    • 金镕 - Molten gold.
    • 块形 - Shape of blocks.
    • 宰邑者 - County magistrate.
    • 汧公 - Marquis of Qian.
    • 德宗朝 - Reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 宰相 - Highest-ranking officials in the imperial government.
    • 东都 - Eastern capital, Luoyang.
    • 敦化坊 - Specific ward in Luoyang.
    • 兴庆观 - Temple in Luoyang.
    • 咸通中 - Xiantong period of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 毕相国 - Duke Bi.
    • 理财者 - Financial officers.
    • 工匠 - Craftsmen.

    He then searched among the shops for gold to cast into ingots of the same shape as the blocks. Once completed, he weighed half of it, which already amounted to three hundred pounds. Calculating the total amount of gold, it was not something two people could carry on bamboo poles. It became clear that by the time they started on the road, the gold had already turned to dust. The public's anxiety was greatly relieved, and the county magistrate was thus cleared of suspicion. The Marquis of Qian was deeply impressed and could not stop praising. He often said that his intelligence and ability were inferior to those who followed him on the path of officialdom. By the time of Emperor Dezong, they had all become chancellors. I have heard that gold hidden in the earth, when discovered, sometimes changes its form. In Dongdu's Dunhua Ward, there was a trace of a qilin (a mythical creature) seen in the dilapidated halls of Xingqing Temple. During the Xiantong period, Duke Bi ordered the reconstruction, and among the foundations, large jars containing white gold were found. The financial officers and thirty-three craftsmen were all afraid that the gold would be taken by the authorities. They then used wooden ladders to cover it and waited for it to become dark. At night, each wrapped the gold in clothing and took it home. The next morning, when opened, it was like hard soil cut into silver ingots. What is said here is exactly the same. [23]

    郭鄩见穷
    　　通事舎人郭鄩罢栎阳县尉久不得调穷居京辇委困方甚肹蠁间常有二物状如猿玃衣以青衣碧衣出入寝兴无不相遂凡欲举意求索必谓与鄩俱往所造之间如碍枳棘匪惟干禄不遂方且病于寒馁亲友见之俱为雠隟或厌之以符术或避之于山林如此数年竟莫能絶一夕处于净室忽来告别云某等承君厄运不相离者久焉今则晓而行无复至矣鄩既喜其去遂询所之云世路如某者甚多但人不见耳今之所诣乃胜业坊王氏其家大积金帛将往散之不久当竭鄩复问云彼之聚敛丰盈何以遽令散去云先得计于安品子其余冰销雾散而晓钟忽鸣遂失所在鄩既兴盥潄便觉愁愤开 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 穷居京辇 - Living in poverty in the capital city.
    • 委困方甚 - Being deeply impoverished and struggling.
    • 肹蠁间 - The space between the living and the dead, often associated with spirits or ghosts.
    • 状如猿玃 - Describes the appearance of the creatures as resembling apes or gibbons.
    • 衣以青衣碧衣 - Wearing green and blue robes, respectively.
    • 寝兴无不相遂 - Accompanying him in all his daily activities, both sleeping and waking.
    • 欲举意求索 - Intending to take action or seek something.
    • 与鄩俱往 - Going with him.
    • 所造之间如碍枳棘 - The places they go to are obstructed by thorns, symbolizing difficulties or obstacles.
    • 干禄不遂 - Failing to obtain an official position or salary.
    • 病于寒馁 - Suffering from cold and hunger.
    • 亲友见之俱为雠隟 - Friends and relatives all regard him with suspicion or hostility.
    • 厌之以符术 - Trying to repel them using talismans or magic.
    • 避之于山林 - Seeking refuge in the mountains and forests.
    • 如此数年竟莫能絶 - For several years, nothing could be done to get rid of them.
    • 净室 - A clean or sacred room.
    • 晓钟忽鸣 - The morning bell suddenly rings.
    • 失所在 - Disappear from sight.
    • 兴盥潄 - Rise and wash one's mouth and face.
    • 开 - Open (likely referring to opening the door or window, but the exact meaning is unclear without context).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 通事舎人 - A specific title for an official responsible for translating and interpreting, often in a diplomatic or ceremonial context.
    • 栎阳县尉 - The position of a county magistrate in Linyang County.
    • 京辇 - The capital city, likely referring to Chang'an or Luoyang, depending on the time period.
    • 符术 - Talismans and magic, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs and practices.
    • 山林 - Often associated with retreat and escape, symbolizing a place of refuge from worldly troubles.
    • 晓钟 - The morning bell, often used in Chinese literature to signify the start of a new day or a change in circumstances.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郭鄩 - The protagonist of the story.
    • 栎阳县尉 - The position held by De Xun before his current situation.
    • 净室 - A specific room mentioned in the story.
    • 胜业坊王氏 - The Wang family in Shengye坊, who are mentioned as having a large fortune.
    • 安品子 - A person involved in the plan to disperse the Wang family's wealth.

    De Xun and the Ghosts

    De Xun, an interpreter, was once the magistrate of Linyang County but had not been promoted for a long time. Living in poverty in the capital, he was deeply impoverished and struggling. In the space between the living and the dead, there were two creatures that resembled apes or gibbons, wearing green and blue robes. They accompanied him in all his daily activities, both sleeping and waking. Whenever he intended to take action or seek something, they insisted on going with him, and the places they went to were obstructed by thorns, symbolizing difficulties or obstacles. Failing to obtain an official position or salary, he suffered from cold and hunger. Friends and relatives all regarded him with suspicion or hostility, trying to repel them using talismans or magic, or seeking refuge in the mountains and forests. For several years, nothing could be done to get rid of them.

    One night, he was in a clean room when the creatures suddenly came to say goodbye. They said, "We have been with you for a long time due to your misfortune. Now we will wait until the morning bell rings and leave. We will not return." De Xun was happy that they were leaving and asked where they were going. They said, "There are many like you in the world, but people do not see them. We are headed to the Wang family in Shengye坊, who have amassed a large fortune and will soon distribute it."

    De Xun asked again, "Why will their wealth be dispersed?" They replied, "We have already made plans with An Pinzi, leading to the dispersal of the wealth. The rest will melt away like ice and dissipate like fog. The morning bell suddenly rang, and they disappeared from sight."

    De Xun then rose, washed his mouth and face, and felt a sense of relief or change. [24]

    豁是日试诣亲友无不改观相接未渉旬于政事堂见宰相自白遂除通事舎人鄩有表弟张生者为金吾卫佐交游皆豪侠少年骋骏好奇闻之未甚为信知胜业坊王氏于左广列职其后往伺之王氏润屋之资几侔猗顿然为性俭约所费未尝过分家有姬仆声乐其间端丽者至多外之服冶容造次莫其意一旦与賔朋骤过鸣珂曲有妇人靓妆立于门首王氏驻马迟留喜动顔色因召同列者命酒开筵为欢颇甚时张生预其末密访于左右即安品子善歌是日歌数曲王氏悉以金防赠之众皆讶其广费自此舆辇资货日输其门毎欢洽酒酣略无所恡繇是治生之业渐属他门未经数年遂至贫匮 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 豁是日 - Colloquial expression meaning 'on that day' or 'at that time.'
    • 試诣亲友 - Means 'to visit relatives and friends' in a trial or experimental sense.
    • 无不改观 - Means 'everyone changed their opinion' or 'everyone was impressed.'
    • 自白 - Means 'to confess' or 'to make a statement.'
    • 通事舎人 - An official title in the Tang Dynasty, roughly equivalent to a 'translator' or 'interpreter.'
    • 鄩有表弟张生者 - Means 'Xun had a cousin named Zhang Sheng.'
    • 金吾卫佐 - An official title in the Tang Dynasty, roughly equivalent to an 'assistant to the Gold Guards.'
    • 豪侠少年 - Means 'bold and heroic young men.'
    • 骋骏好奇 - Means 'to enjoy speed and novelty.'
    • 润屋之资 - Means 'wealth for beautifying one's home.'
    • 侔猗顿 - Means 'to rival the wealth of Yidun,' referring to the legendary rich man Yidun.
    • 俭约 - Means 'frugal and simple.'
    • 端丽 - Means 'beautiful and elegant.'
    • 冶容 - Means 'adorned or made up.'
    • 造次莫其意 - Means 'in haste, one cannot understand her intentions.'
    • 驻马迟留 - Means 'to stop the horse and linger.'
    • 喜动顔色 - Means 'to show joy on one's face.'
    • 命酒开筵 - Means 'to order wine and start a banquet.'
    • 为欢颇甚 - Means 'to have a very enjoyable time.'
    • 密访于左右 - Means 'to secretly inquire from those around.'
    • 善歌 - Means 'to be good at singing.'
    • 悉以金防赠之 - Means 'to give all the gold and silver as gifts.'
    • 舆辇资货 - Means 'carriages and wealth.'
    • 治生之业 - Means 'the business of making a living.'
    • 渐属他门 - Means 'gradually becoming someone else's.'
    • 贫匮 - Means 'poverty and scarcity.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 通事舎人 - An official title in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for interpreting and translating.
    • 金吾卫佐 - An official title in the Tang Dynasty, responsible for assisting the Gold Guards.
    • 胜业坊 - A district in Chang'an during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 左广 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific area or building.
    • 鸣珂曲 - A street or alley in Chang'an, known for its luxurious and fashionable atmosphere.
    • 王氏 - A family name, referring to a wealthy and influential family in Chang'an.

    Proper Nouns

    • 鄩 - A person's name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 张生 - A person's name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 王氏 - A family name, referring to a wealthy and influential family in Chang'an.
    • 猗顿 - A legendary rich man from the Warring States period.

    On that day, when he visited relatives and friends, everyone was impressed. They interacted for less than ten days. At the political hall, he made a statement and was appointed as a translator. Xun had a cousin named Zhang Sheng, who was an assistant to the Gold Guards and enjoyed the company of bold and heroic young men who enjoyed speed and novelty. When Zhang Sheng heard about this, he did not believe it. Xun knew of a wealthy family named Wang in the Shengye Fang district of Chang'an and went there. He stopped his horse and lingered, showing joy on his face. He then called his companions and ordered wine, starting a banquet, and they had a very enjoyable time. Zhang Sheng was among them and secretly inquired from those around, learning that the woman was good at singing. That day, she sang several songs, and Wang gave all the gold and silver as gifts. Everyone was surprised by her generosity. From then on, carriages and wealth were daily delivered to her door. Every time they gathered and drank, she did not hesitate to give away her wealth. Gradually, the business of making a living became someone else's, and within a few years, she became poor and scarce. [25]

    裴晋公天津桥遇老人
    　　裴晋公度微时羇寓洛中常乘蹇驴入皇城方上天津桥时淮西不庭已数年矣有二老人傍桥柱而立语云蔡州用兵日久徴发甚困于人未知何时得平定忽覩裴公惊愕而退有仆者携书囊后行相去稍逺闻老人云适忧蔡州未平须待此人为将既归仆者具述其事裴公曰见我龙钟相戏尔其秋东府乡荐明年登第及秉钧衡朝廷议授吴元济节既而延英对宪皇以问宰臣裴公奏曰奸臣防扈四十
    余年圣朝姑务含容葢虑动伤一境未闻归心効顺乃坐据一方以旄授之翻恐恣其凶逆以陛下聪明神武藩鎭皆愿勤王臣请一诏追兵可以平荡妖孽于是命晋公为淮西节度使兴师致讨时陈许汴滑三帅先于偃城县屯军晋公统精甲五万防之受律鼓行而进直造蔡州城下才两月擒贼以献淮西遂平后入朝居廊庙六拜正司徒为侍中中书令儒风武德振耀古今洎留守洛师毎话天津桥老人之事 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 裴晋公度微时 - Refers to Pei Jigen in his humble beginnings.
    • 羁寓洛中 - Means 'staying as a guest in Luoyang,' indicating his status as a traveler or resident in the city.
    • 乘蹇驴 - Describes riding a slow or old donkey, symbolizing his modest circumstances.
    • 方上天津桥 - Means 'just crossing the Tianjin Bridge,' indicating the action of crossing the bridge.
    • 淮西不庭 - A phrase meaning 'the Huai West region was in turmoil,' referring to a period of unrest in the Huai West region.
    • 用兵日久 - Means 'warfare has been ongoing for a long time,' indicating prolonged military conflict.
    • 征发甚困 - Describes the situation where conscription and requisitions were causing great hardship.
    • 未知何时得平定 - Means 'it is unknown when it will be pacified,' expressing uncertainty about the end of the conflict.
    • 忽睹裴公惊愕而退 - Describes the sudden appearance of Pei Jigen, causing two elderly men to be surprised and retreat.
    • 携书囊后行 - Means 'carrying a book bag behind,' indicating the servant's action.
    • 相去稍远 - Means 'they were slightly apart,' describing the distance between Pei Jigen and his servant.
    • 闻老人云 - Means 'he heard the old men say,' indicating what Pei Jigen overheard.
    • 适忧蔡州未平 - Means 'just worrying that Cai Prefecture has not been pacified,' expressing the old men's concern.
    • 须待此人 - Means 'must wait for this person,' indicating that Pei Jigen is expected to play a crucial role.
    • 为将 - Means 'to become a general,' referring to Pei Jigen's potential military leadership.
    • 既归仆者具述其事 - Means 'after returning, the servant reported the entire incident,' indicating the servant's recounting of the event.
    • 见我龙钟相戏尔 - Means 'you see me old and frail, just playing a joke,' expressing Pei Jigen's dismissal of the incident as a joke.
    • 其秋东府乡荐 - Means 'that autumn, he was recommended by the Eastern Prefecture,' indicating Pei Jigen's recommendation for a position.
    • 明年登第 - Means 'the following year, he passed the imperial examination,' indicating Pei Jigen's success in the exams.
    • 及秉钧衡 - Means 'when he held the balance of power,' indicating Pei Jigen's rise to a position of authority.
    • 朝廷议授吴元济节钺 - Means 'the court discussed granting the military command to Wu Yuanji,' indicating the court's decision to appoint Wu Yuanji.
    • 延英待对 - Means 'awaiting an audience in the Yanqing Hall,' indicating the formal setting for the meeting.
    • 宪皇以问宰臣 - Means 'the Emperor asked the prime minister,' indicating the emperor's inquiry.
    • 奸臣防扈 - Means 'rebellious officials were guarding,' indicating the resistance of the rebels.
    • 四十有余年 - Means 'more than forty years,' indicating the duration of the rebellion.
    • 圣朝姑务含容 - Means 'the holy court was trying to accommodate,' indicating the court's policy of tolerance.
    • 虑动伤一境 - Means 'fearing that any action would harm the region,' expressing concern about the consequences of military action.
    • 未闻归心效顺 - Means 'no one had heard of their submission,' indicating the lack of surrender from the rebels.
    • 坐据一方 - Means 'sitting in control of one area,' indicating the rebels' control over a region.
    • 以旄钺授之 - Means 'granting them the military command,' indicating the court's decision to appoint a general.
    • 恣其凶逆 - Means 'allowing them to act wickedly,' indicating the potential for further rebellion.
    • 陛下聪明神武 - Means 'Your Majesty is wise and valiant,' praising the emperor's qualities.
    • 藩镇皆愿勤王 - Means 'the regional governors all wish to serve the imperial court,' indicating their loyalty.
    • 臣请一诏追兵可以平荡妖孽 - Means 'I request that a single edict be issued to mobilize troops and pacify the rebels,' indicating Pei Jigen's proposal.
    • 命晋公为淮西节度使 - Means 'appointing Pei Jigen as the military governor of Huai West,' indicating his appointment.
    • 兴师致讨 - Means 'mobilizing troops to attack,' indicating the military campaign.
    • 时陈许汴滑三帅先于偃城县屯军 - Means 'at the time, the generals of Chen, Xu, Bian, and Huai were stationed in Yancheng County,' indicating the military preparations.
    • 晋公统精甲五万防之 - Means 'Pei Jigen led 50,000 elite troops to defend them,' indicating his leadership.
    • 受律鼓行而进 - Means 'receiving orders and advancing with drums beating,' indicating the military advance.
    • 直造蔡州城下 - Means 'directly reaching the walls of Cai Prefecture,' indicating the proximity to the rebel stronghold.
    • 才两月擒贼以献 - Means 'after only two months, the rebels were captured and presented,' indicating the swift victory.
    • 淮西遂平 - Means 'Huai West was finally pacified,' indicating the end of the conflict.
    • 后入朝居廊庙 - Means 'later entering the court and serving in the central government,' indicating Pei Jigen's return to the capital.
    • 六拜正司徒 - Means 'receiving six promotions to the position of Grand Tutor,' indicating his rise in rank.
    • 为侍中中书令 - Means 'serving as Chief Secretary and Director of the Secretariat,' indicating his high-ranking positions.
    • 儒风武德 - Means 'scholarly virtues and martial virtues,' indicating a balance of intellectual and military qualities.
    • 振耀古今 - Means 'glorifying both ancient and modern times,' indicating his lasting impact.
    • 洎留守洛师 - Means 'later serving as the governor of Luoyang,' indicating his appointment to a new position.
    • 每话天津桥老人之事 - Means 'often recounting the story of the old men on Tianjin Bridge,' indicating his fondness for the memory.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 裴晋公 - Refers to Pei Jigen, a prominent statesman and military leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 天津桥 - A famous bridge in Luoyang, often mentioned in historical texts.
    • 淮西 - A region in present-day Henan Province, known for its historical significance and frequent conflicts.
    • 吴元济 - A rebel leader during the late Tang Dynasty, known for his resistance against the central government.
    • 宪皇 - Refers to Emperor Xianzong of Tang, known for his efforts to suppress rebellions.
    • 东府乡荐 - Refers to the recommendation system in the Tang Dynasty, where local officials recommended talented individuals for higher positions.
    • 登第 - Refers to passing the imperial examination, a crucial step in the career advancement of scholars.
    • 秉钧衡 - Refers to holding a position of great power and influence, similar to a prime minister.
    • 节钺 - Refers to the military command and authority granted to a general.
    • 延英待对 - Refers to the formal setting where the emperor would meet with his ministers.
    • 藩镇 - Refers to regional military governors who held significant power and often resisted central authority.
    • 勤王 - Refers to the loyalty and service of regional governors to the imperial court.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 裴晋公度微时羁寓洛中常乘蹇驴入皇城方上天津桥 - This long sequence must be read as a single sentence describing Pei Jigen's humble circumstances and actions.
    • 淮西不庭已数年矣 - This clause introduces the background of the conflict in Huai West.
    • 有二老人傍桥柱而立语云 - This clause describes the two elderly men standing by the bridge and speaking.
    • 忽睹裴公惊愕而退 - This clause describes the sudden appearance of Pei Jigen and the reaction of the elderly men.
    • 携书囊后行相去稍远闻老人云 - This clause describes the servant's actions and what he overheard.
    • 适忧蔡州未平须待此人 - This clause explains the elderly men's concerns and their prediction about Pei Jigen.
    • 既归仆者具述其事 - This clause describes the servant's report back to Pei Jigen.
    • 见我龙钟相戏尔 - This clause shows Pei Jigen's dismissive response.
    • 其秋东府乡荐明年登第 - This clause describes Pei Jigen's subsequent success and promotions.
    • 及秉钧衡朝廷议授吴元济节钺 - This clause describes Pei Jigen's rise to power and the court's decision to appoint Wu Yuanji.

    Péi Jigen, the Duke of Huai West, Encounters Old Men on Tianjin Bridge

    When Péi Jigen was in his humble beginnings, he stayed as a guest in Luoyang and often rode a slow donkey into the imperial city, just crossing the Tianjin Bridge. At that time, the Huai West region had been in turmoil for more than forty years, with prolonged warfare and conscription causing great hardship. It was unknown when it would be pacified. Suddenly, two elderly men standing by the bridge column spoke, saying, "We are worried that Cai Prefecture has not been pacified; we must wait for this person to become a general." Upon seeing Péi Jigen, they were surprised and retreated. A servant carrying a book bag followed behind, slightly apart. He heard the old men say, "We are just worried that Cai Prefecture has not been pacified; we must wait for this person." After returning, the servant reported the entire incident. Péi Jigen said, "You see me old and frail, just playing a joke." That autumn, he was recommended by the Eastern Prefecture and passed the imperial examination the following year. When he held the balance of power, the court discussed granting the military command to Wu Yuanji. Later, in the Yanqing Hall, the Emperor asked the prime minister. Péi Jigen replied, "Rebellious officials have been guarding for more than forty years. The holy court has been trying to accommodate, fearing that any action would harm the region. No one has heard of their submission, and they sit in control of one area. Granting them the military command would allow them to act wickedly. Your Majesty is wise and valiant, and the regional governors all wish to serve the imperial court. I request that a single edict be issued to mobilize troops and pacify the rebels." Thus, Péi Jigen was appointed as the military governor of Huai West and mobilized troops to attack. At the time, the generals of Chen, Xu, Bian, and Huai were stationed in Yancheng County. Péi Jigen led 50,000 elite troops to defend them, receiving orders and advancing with drums beating, directly reaching the walls of Cai Prefecture. After only two months, the rebels were captured and presented, and Huai West was finally pacified. Later, he entered the court and served in the central government, receiving six promotions to the position of Grand Tutor and serving as Chief Secretary and Director of the Secretariat. His scholarly virtues and martial virtues glorified both ancient and modern times. Later, serving as the governor of Luoyang, he often recounted the story of the old men on Tianjin Bridge. [26]

    狄惟谦请雨
    　　防昌中北都晋阳县令狄惟谦梁公之后守官清恪有蒲密之政抚绥勤防不畏强御属州境亢阳渉歴春夏数百里水泉农亩无不耗斁枯竭祷于晋祠者数旬畧无阴曀之兆时有郭天师者本并土女巫少攻符术多行猒胜之道有监军使将至京师因缘中贵出入宫掖其后军牒告归遂以天师为号既而亢旱滋甚阖境莫知所为佥言曰得天师一到晋祠则灾旱不足忧矣惟谦请于主帅主帅难之惟谦曰灾厉流行甿庶焦灼非天师一救万姓恐无聊生于是主帅亲自为请巫者唯而许之惟谦乃具车舆列幡葢迎于私室躬为控马既至祠所盛设供帐丰防饮自旦及昏磬折于 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 守官清恪 - Describes a person who holds office with integrity and diligence.
    • 蒲密之政 - A metaphorical expression referring to efficient and orderly governance, possibly alluding to the governance style of a historical figure or period.
    • 抚绥勤防 - Means 'to pacify and protect diligently,' indicating efforts to maintain peace and security.
    • 不畏强御 - Means 'unafraid of powerful or intimidating forces,' suggesting courage and determination.
    • 属州境亢阳 - '属' means 'belonging to' or 'under the jurisdiction of'; '亢阳' refers to prolonged drought or extreme heat.
    • 渉歴春夏 - Means 'spanning spring and summer,' indicating the duration of the drought.
    • 数百里水泉农亩无不耗斁 - '数百里' means 'hundreds of miles'; '水泉' refers to water sources; '农亩' means 'farmland'; '耗斁' means 'wasted' or 'destroyed.'
    • 枯竭 - Means 'dried up' or 'exhausted,' describing the state of water sources and farmland.
    • 祷于晋祠者数旬 - '祷' means 'to pray'; '晋祠' is a temple or shrine dedicated to the god of water; '数旬' means 'for several weeks.'
    • 略无阴曀之兆 - '阴曀' means 'cloudy' or 'overcast'; '兆' means 'sign' or 'omen'; '略无' means 'almost none.'
    • 少攻符术 - '少' means 'young' or 'early'; '攻' means 'to study' or 'to practice'; '符术' refers to Taoist magic or spells.
    • 多行猒胜之道 - '猒胜' is a term that may refer to a specific method or practice, possibly related to Taoist rituals or magic.
    • 灾厉流行甿庶焦灼 - '灾厉' means 'disaster and plague'; '流行' means 'spread'; '甿庶' means 'common people'; '焦灼' means 'anxious' or 'distressed.'
    • 非天师一救万姓恐无聊生 - '非天师一救' means 'not the Master Taoist's intervention'; '万姓' means 'the common people'; '恐无聊生' means 'fear of being unable to survive.'
    • 自旦及昏磬折于 - '自旦及昏' means 'from morning until evening'; '磬折' means 'to bow deeply'; '于' is a preposition meaning 'at' or 'in.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 狄惟谦 - A historical figure, likely an official during a specific period.
    • 梁公 - A title or reference to a noble or official, possibly indicating a high rank or lineage.
    • 晋阳县令 - The position of magistrate of Jinyang County, a specific administrative role.
    • 晋祠 - A temple or shrine dedicated to the god of water, often associated with rainmaking rituals.
    • 郭天师 - A Taoist priest, likely a specific individual with a significant role in the narrative.
    • 监军使 - A military inspector, a high-ranking official responsible for overseeing military affairs.
    • 京师 - The capital city, likely referring to a specific historical capital.
    • 宫掖 - The palace or court, indicating a high level of authority and influence.
    • 军牒 - A military document, likely used for official communication.
    • 天师 - A title given to a Taoist priest, often associated with religious and magical practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 狄惟谦 - The name of the official.
    • 梁公 - A title or reference to a noble or official.
    • 晋阳县令 - The position of magistrate of Jinyang County.
    • 晋祠 - A temple or shrine dedicated to the god of water.
    • 郭天师 - A Taoist priest.
    • 监军使 - A military inspector.
    • 京师 - The capital city.
    • 宫掖 - The palace or court.
    • 军牒 - A military document.
    • 天师 - A title given to a Taoist priest.

    Duke Wei Qian Requested Rain

    During the period of Fang Chang, the magistrate of Jinyang County in the northern capital, Duke Wei Qian, who was a descendant of Liang Gong, held office with integrity and diligence, implementing efficient and orderly governance. He diligently pacified and protected the region, unafraid of powerful or intimidating forces. Under his jurisdiction, the state suffered from prolonged drought that spanned spring and summer, affecting hundreds of miles of water sources and farmland, all of which were wasted and dried up. People prayed at the Jin Temple for several weeks, but there were almost no signs of clouds or rain.

    At that time, there was a Taoist priest named Guo, who was originally a female shaman from Bingtu. She studied Taoist magic from a young age and practiced a method known as "yansheng." A military inspector was about to travel to the capital city, and due to her connection with a high-ranking official, she had access to the palace. Later, a military document was issued, and she returned home, taking the title of Master Taoist. The drought then became more severe, and the entire region was in distress, with no one knowing what to do. Everyone agreed that if the Master Taoist arrived at the Jin Temple, then the drought and disaster would not be a concern.

    Duke Wei Qian requested the commander to summon the Taoist priest, but the commander hesitated. Duke Wei Qian said, "Disaster and plague are spreading, and the common people are anxious. Without the intervention of the Master Taoist, the common people will likely be unable to survive." Therefore, the commander personally requested the shaman, who agreed and consented.

    Duke Wei Qian then prepared a carriage, arranged flags and canopies, and met her at his private residence, personally controlling the horse. Upon arriving at the temple, he set up elaborate offerings and prepared abundant food and drink. From morning until evening, he bowed deeply in reverence. [27]

    堦庭之下如此者翌日语惟谦曰我为尔飞符于上界请雨已奉天帝之命必在防恳至诚三日雨当足矣繇是四郊士庶奔走云集三夕于兹曾不降雨又曰此土灾沴所兴亦由县令无德我为尔再上天请七日方合有雨惟谦引罪于己奉之愈恭俄而又及所期畧无霑霔郭乃骤索马入州宅惟谦拜留曰天师已为万姓此来更乞至心祈祷于是勃然而怒骂曰庸琐官人不知道理天时未肯下雨留我将复奚为惟谦谢曰非敢更烦天师明旦排比相送耳于是惟谦宿诫左右曰我为巫者所辱岂可复言为官明晨别有指挥汝等或须相禀是非好恶县令当之及晓伺门未开郭已严饬归骑 [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 飛符 - A talisman, a common element in Chinese folklore and religious practices.
    • 上界 - The upper realm, often associated with the divine or supernatural.
    • 天帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a figure in Chinese mythology and religion.
    • 防恳至诚 - A phrase emphasizing sincerity and earnestness, important in Confucian ethics.
    • 四郊 - The four quarters, often used to describe the surrounding areas of a city.
    • 士庶 - Refers to the common people, including both scholars and ordinary citizens.
    • 灾沴 - Calamities or disasters, often attributed to moral or spiritual causes.
    • 县令 - The magistrate of a county, a local government official.
    • 州宅 - The prefectural residence, the official residence of a prefect.
    • 天师 - Master of Heaven, a title for a high-ranking religious or spiritual leader.
    • 万姓 - The common people, often used in a collective sense.
    • 至心祈祷 - Sincere prayer, important in religious and spiritual practices.
    • 巫者 - A witch or sorcerer, often associated with supernatural practices.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 堦庭 - Refers to the courtyard, a common setting in classical Chinese literature.
    • 惟谦 - Likely a name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 飞符 - A talisman, a common element in Chinese folklore and religious practices.
    • 上界 - The upper realm, often associated with the divine or supernatural.
    • 天帝 - The Heavenly Emperor, a figure in Chinese mythology and religion.
    • 防恳至诚 - A phrase emphasizing sincerity and earnestness, important in Confucian ethics.
    • 四郊 - The four quarters, often used to describe the surrounding areas of a city.
    • 士庶 - Refers to the common people, including both scholars and ordinary citizens.
    • 灾沴 - Calamities or disasters, often attributed to moral or spiritual causes.
    • 县令 - The magistrate of a county, a local government official.
    • 郭 - Likely a name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 州宅 - The prefectural residence, the official residence of a prefect.
    • 天师 - Master of Heaven, a title for a high-ranking religious or spiritual leader.
    • 万姓 - The common people, often used in a collective sense.
    • 至心祈祷 - Sincere prayer, important in religious and spiritual practices.
    • 巫者 - A witch or sorcerer, often associated with supernatural practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 惟谦 - A name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 郭 - A name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 天师 - A title for a high-ranking religious or spiritual leader.
    • 万姓 - A collective term for the common people.
    • 县令 - The magistrate of a county, a local government official.
    • 州宅 - The prefectural residence, the official residence of a prefect.

    In the courtyard, where such things were happening, the next day, Guo said to Wei Qian, "I will fly a talisman to the upper realm for you to request rain. I have already received the command of the Heavenly Emperor. It must be done with utmost sincerity and earnestness. In three days, the rain will be sufficient." As a result, people from the four quarters ran and gathered, but after three nights, there was no sign of rain. Guo then said, "This land is where calamities arise, also due to the county magistrate's lack of virtue. I will again ascend to heaven to request. In seven days, there will be rain." Wei Qian took the blame upon himself and served with even greater respect. Soon it reached the expected time, yet there was no sign of rain. Guo suddenly demanded his horse and entered the prefectural residence. Wei Qian knelt and begged to keep him, saying, "The Master of Heaven has come for the benefit of all the people. I beg you to pray with utmost sincerity." Guo suddenly became angry and cursed, "You, an insignificant official, do not understand the ways of the world. The heavenly time is not willing to send rain. What good will it do to keep me here?" Wei Qian apologized, "I dare not trouble the Master of Heaven again. Wait until tomorrow morning, and I will arrange to send you off." Wei Qian then warned his attendants beforehand, "I have been insulted by the witch. How can I still speak of being an official? Tomorrow morning, I will give you other instructions. The right and wrong, the likes and dislikes, are the responsibility of the county magistrate." By dawn, the gate was still closed, and Guo had already prepared to return on horseback. [28]

    常供设肴醴一无所施坐于皇堂大恣呵责惟谦曰左道女巫妖惑日久当须毙在兹日焉敢言归叱左右坐于神前鞭背三十投于潭水祠后有山髙万千丈遽令设席焚香从吏悉皆放还簮笏立于其上于是合县骇愕云长官打杀天师驰走者纷纭观者如堵是时炎旱累月烁石流金晴空万里略无纤翳祠上忽有片云如车盖俄顷渐髙先覆惟谦立所四郊云物随之而合雷震数声甘泽大澍焦原赤野无不滋润于是士庶数千自山顶拥惟谦而下州将以杖杀巫者初亦怒之既而精诚有感深加叹异与监军发表上闻俄有诏书襃奬赐钱五十万宠赐章服为绛隰二州刺史所理咸有政声 [29]

    Final Translation:

    Regularly providing food and drink offerings had no effect. Sitting in the imperial hall, he scolded severely. Only Mr. Hui said that the witch practicing heretical magic has been causing confusion and deception for a long time and must be killed on this day; how dare one speak of returning? He scolded those around, sitting in front of the deity, and whipped her back thirty times, throwing her into the pond water. Behind the shrine was a mountain thousands of feet high. Quickly ordered to set up a mat and burn incense, all the officials were released back with their official attire and stood on it. The whole county was shocked and surprised, saying that the official killed the heavenly master. Those who ran away were in a mess, and the spectators were like a wall. At that time, there had been a severe drought for several months, melting stones and flowing gold, clear sky for thousands of miles, without a single trace of cloud. Suddenly, a cloud like a carriage canopy appeared above the shrine, soon rising higher, first covering the place where Mr. Hui stood. The clouds in the four suburbs followed and gathered. Thunder roared several times, and abundant rain fell. Burned earth and red fields were all moistened. Thousands of commoners and officials gathered around Mr. Hui and followed him down. The official wanted to beat the witch to death, at first being angry with him. But then his sincere and earnest attitude moved him, and he deeply admired and praised him. With the military commander, he reported it to the higher authorities. Soon there was an imperial edict praising him, awarding him fifty thousand coins, bestowing honors and official robes, and appointing him as the governor of the two states of Jiang and Xi. He was well-known for his good governance. [29]

    勑书云狄惟谦剧邑良才忠臣华胄覩此天厉将瘅下民当请祷于晋祠投巫于邺县曝山椒之畏景事等焚躯起天际之油云法同翦爪遂使旱风潜息甘泽旋流天心犹鉴于克诚余志岂忘于襃善特颁朱绂俾耀铜章勿替令名更昭殊绩
    　　王侍中题诗
    　　王侍中智兴武略英奇初授徐方节制雄才磊落有命世间生之誉幕府既开所辟皆是儒者一旦从事于使院防饮与从容赋诗顷之达于王公乃召防军俱至从事乃屏去翰墨但以杯盘迎接良久问之曰适闻判官与诸贤作诗何得见某而罢遽令却取笔砚复以防牋数十幅散于座众宾相顾迟疑将俟行觞举乐复曰本来欲观制作非以饮酒为意时小吏亦以牋翰置于王公之前从事礼为揖王公曰前某以韬略发迹未尝留心章句今日陪奉英髦不免亦陈愚恳遂乃引援毫顷刻而就云平生弓劒自相随刚郎官遣作诗江南花柳从君咏塞北烟尘我自知四座览之惊叹无已咸云忠烈词彩虽曹景宗贺 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 勑书云 - Introduces an imperial edict or decree, indicating that what follows is an official command or directive.
    • 狄惟谦剧邑良才忠臣华胄 - Describes Didi Weiqian, a person of noble lineage, talent, and loyalty. '华胄' (Huazhou) refers to a person of aristocratic or noble birth.
    • 覩此天厉将瘅下民 - Means 'seeing this heavenly scourge will afflict the common people.' '天厉' (Tianli) refers to a divine punishment or calamity.
    • 请祷于晋祠 - Means 'to pray at the Jinci Temple.' '晋祠' (Jinci) is a famous temple in Shanxi Province dedicated to the memory of Duke Wen of Jin.
    • 投巫于邺县 - Means 'to cast the witch into the city of Yexian.' '邺县' (Yexian) is an ancient county in Henan Province.
    • 曝山椒之畏景 - Means 'to expose oneself to the terrifying sun.' '山椒' (Shanjiao) refers to the top of a mountain, and '畏景' (Weijing) means the scorching sun.
    • 事等焚躯 - Means 'the matter is equivalent to burning one's body.' It suggests extreme self-sacrifice.
    • 起天际之油云 - Means 'to summon clouds from the horizon.' '天际' (Tianji) refers to the horizon, and '油云' (Youyun) means dark, heavy clouds.
    • 法同翦爪 - Means 'the method is the same as cutting one's nails.' It suggests a ritual act of self-denial or sacrifice.
    • 特颁朱绂俾耀铜章 - Means 'to specially grant a red sash and a copper seal to shine.' '朱绂' (Zhufu) is a red sash worn by officials, and '铜章' (Tongzhang) is a copper seal.
    • 勿替令名更昭殊绩 - Means 'do not let the good name be forgotten, and further highlight the extraordinary achievements.' '勿替' (Wuti) means do not let it be forgotten, '令名' (Lingming) means good name, '更昭' (Gengzhao) means further highlight, and '殊绩' (Shuji) means extraordinary achievements.
    • 王侍中题诗 - Introduces a poem written by Wang Shizhong, a high-ranking official.
    • 武略英奇 - Means 'military strategy and extraordinary talent.' It describes Wang Shizhong's military prowess.
    • 雄才磊落 - Means 'great talent and noble character.' It describes Wang Shizhong's exceptional qualities.
    • 幕府既开所辟皆是儒者 - Means 'once the military office was established, all those appointed were scholars.' '幕府' (Mufu) refers to a military office or headquarters.
    • 从事于使院 - Means 'to serve in the mission office.' '使院' (Shiyuan) refers to a mission or embassy office.
