    • 牡丹 - Peony, a symbol of wealth, honor, and nobility in Chinese culture, often associated with spring and prosperity.
    • 赏花 - A traditional Chinese custom where people gather to admire and celebrate the blooming of flowers, especially peonies.
    • 内室 - Inner chambers, typically reserved for private and intimate gatherings.
    • 锦绣 - Brocade, a luxurious fabric often used in high-end clothing and furnishings.
    • 黄金 - Gold, a symbol of wealth and luxury.
    • 锦幄 - Brocade canopy, used to cover and decorate areas, adding to the aesthetic appeal.
    • 纨绮 - Fine silk and satin, indicating the high quality of the attire.
    • 丝簧 - String and wind instruments, commonly used in classical Chinese music.
    • 双鬟 - Double buns, a hairstyle for young women, indicating their youth and beauty.
    • 仆乘 - Servants and carriages, essential for hosting large events.
    • 水陆 - Land and sea, used to describe a wide variety of food and drink, indicating the abundance and diversity of the feast.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 本文宗朝朔方节度使李进贤旧第 - The former residence of Li Jinxian, the military governor of Shaofang during the reign of Emperor Wenzong, which provides both the historical context and the identity of the subject.
    • 富于财寳 - Wealthy in wealth and treasures, emphasizing both material and cultural wealth.
    • 豪侈奉身 - Living a luxurious and extravagant life, describing Li Jinxian's lifestyle.
    • 雅好賔客 - Having a refined taste for entertaining guests, indicating his social habits.
    • 京华相遇时亦造其门 - When they met in the capital, they also visited his residence, indicating ongoing relationships.
    • 牡丹盛开 - The peony flowers were in full bloom, setting the scene for the event.
    • 赏花为名 - Celebrating the peony flowers, which is the occasion for the gathering.
    • 及期而往 - On the appointed day, they went there, describing the timing of the event.
    • 防事备陈 - Preparations were made in advance, indicating the thoroughness of the preparations.
    • 饮馔宴席之间已非寻常 - The food and drink at the banquet were not ordinary, highlighting the quality of the feast.
    • 举杯数廵 - After several rounds of toasting, describing the progression of the banquet.
    • 引众賔归内室 - He led the guests into the inner chambers, indicating a private reception.
    • 宇华丽楹柱皆设锦绣 - The rooms were magnificent, with beams and pillars adorned with brocade, describing the opulence of the interior.
    • 列筵甚广器用悉是黄金 - The banquet tables were extensive, and all the utensils were made of gold, emphasizing the luxury.
    • 阶前有花数丛覆以锦幄 - Before the steps, there were several clusters of flowers covered with brocade canopies, adding to the visual splendor.
    • 妓妾俱服纨绮执丝簧善歌舞者至多 - The concubines and maidservants wore fine silk and satin, and there were many who were skilled in music and dance, describing the entertainment.
    • 客之左右皆有女仆双鬟者二人所须无不必至 - Each guest had two young female servants with double buns who attended to all their needs, indicating the level of service.
    • 承接之意常日指使者不如芳酒绮肴穷极水陆 - The service was so attentive that even the usual attendants were not as good as the exquisite wine and dishes, which were the best of land and sea, comparing the service to the quality of the food and drink.
    • 至于仆乘供给靡不丰盈 - As for the servants, carriages, and supplies, everything was abundant, emphasizing the completeness of the arrangements.
    • 自午迄于明晨不覩杯盘狼籍 - From noon until the next morning, there was no sign of the dishes being left in disarray, indicating the smooth running of the event.
    • 朝士云迩后歴观豪 - The court officials said that after observing this, they saw the splendor of the wealthy and powerful, providing a final reflection on the event.

    Translation:

    The Collected Conversations, Volume 1

    Authorized by the Emperor, the Four Treasuries of Books

    The Collected Conversations, Volume 2
    Compiled by Kang Bian of the Tang Dynasty

    The Residence of the Duke of Tongyi in Tongyi Lane
    This residence was the former home of Li Jinxian, the military governor of Shaofang during the reign of Emperor Wenzong. Li Jinxian rose from military service and was distinguished and remarkable, having held several positions as a frontier governor. He was wealthy in wealth and treasures, living a luxurious and extravagant life, and had a refined taste for entertaining guests. He often spoke of his days when he had experienced setbacks while traveling on the borders, and Li Jinxian welcomed him with great enthusiasm. Later, when they met in the capital, they also visited his residence. When the peony flowers were in full bloom, he celebrated the peony flowers. On the appointed day, they went there, and preparations were made in advance. The food and drink at the banquet were not ordinary. After several rounds of toasting, he led the guests into the inner chambers. The rooms were magnificent, with beams and pillars adorned with brocade. The banquet tables were extensive, and all the utensils were made of gold. Before the steps, there were several clusters of flowers covered with brocade canopies. The concubines and maidservants wore fine silk and satin, and there were many who were skilled in music and dance. Each guest had two young female servants with double buns who attended to all their needs. The service was so attentive that even the usual attendants were not as good as the exquisite wine and dishes, which were the best of land and sea. As for the servants, carriages, and supplies, everything was abundant. From noon until the next morning, there was no sign of the dishes being left in disarray. The court officials said that after observing this, they saw the splendor of the wealthy and powerful. [43]

    贵之属筵席臻此者甚稀厥后进贤徙居长兴其宅互为他人所有咸通中刘相国罢北京亚尹复为翰林学士数岁后自承防入相尚以十千税焉及出镇荆南朝野无不惋惜都城士庶以少及长闻之俱为涕泣其后兴化萧相登庸举为自代表云正人吞声而扼腕百姓掩泪于道途是时升道郑相国在内廷夜草麻制具述其事云安数亩之居仍非已有却四方之赂惟畏人知是时都下传写为之贵持权者覩其词大怒郑公自翰林承防左迁梧州相国自端溪窜于日南谪居四年方获清雪以秘书监召还未久复持钧轴或将甲第为献竟无所受复于此宅寓居庭宇不加修餙清风俭徳充塞寰宇 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 貴之屬筵席臻此者甚稀 - Elliptical phrase meaning 'those who attain such a high position in their social circles are very rare' or 'those who reach such a distinguished level in their gatherings are very few.'
    • 厥后进贤徙居长兴 - '厥后' means 'later' or 'afterward,' and '进贤' refers to a person named Xian. This phrase indicates that Xian later moved to Changxing.
    • 其宅互为他人所有 - This phrase means 'his house alternately became the property of others,' suggesting that the house was frequently transferred between different owners.
    • 咸通中刘相国罢北京亚尹复为翰林学士 - '咸通中' refers to the year 864 during the Tang Dynasty. '刘相国' is a title for Liu, indicating he held a high position. '罢北京亚尹' means he was dismissed from his post as deputy governor of Beijing. '复为翰林学士' means he was then appointed as a scholar in the Hanlin Academy.
    • 数岁后自承防入相 - '数岁后' means 'a few years later,' and '自承防入相' means he entered the position of chancellor after serving as a defense official.
    • 尚以十千税焉 - '十千' is a unit of currency, equivalent to 10,000 coins. '税焉' means 'paid tax on it.' This phrase indicates that he still paid a tax of 10,000 coins on the property.
    • 及出镇荆南朝野无不惋惜 - '及出镇荆南' means 'when he was sent to govern Jingnan.' '朝野无不惋惜' means 'the court and the people all felt great regret.'
    • 都城士庶以少及长闻之俱为涕泣 - '都城士庶' refers to the people of the capital. '以少及长' means 'from young to old.' '闻之俱为涕泣' means 'all heard of it and wept.'
    • 其后兴化萧相登庸举为自代表云 - '其后' means 'later,' and '兴化萧相' refers to Xiao, a chancellor. '登庸' means 'was promoted to a high position.' '举为自代表云' means 'was nominated as a representative.'
    • 正人吞声而扼腕 - '正人' means 'righteous people,' and '吞声而扼腕' means 'silently suppress their anger and clench their fists.'
    • 百姓掩泪于道途 - '百姓' means 'the common people,' and '掩泪于道途' means 'shed tears in the streets.'
    • 是时升道郑相国在内廷夜草麻制具述其事云 - '是时' means 'at that time,' and '升道郑相国' refers to Zheng, a chancellor. '在内廷夜草麻制' means 'in the inner court, drafting the edict at night.' '具述其事云' means 'detailed the matter.'
    • 安数亩之居仍非已有 - '安数亩之居' means 'a small house of a few acres,' and '仍非已有' means 'still not owned by him.'
    • 却四方之赂惟畏人知 - '却四方之赂' means 'refused gifts from all directions,' and '惟畏人知' means 'only fearing that others would know.'
    • 是时都下传写为之贵持权者覩其词大怒 - '是时都下传写' means 'at that time, it was widely copied in the capital.' '为之贵持权者' means 'the powerful and influential people.' '覩其词大怒' means 'were greatly angered by the words.'
    • 郑公自翰林承防左迁梧州相国自端溪窜于日南 - '郑公自翰林承防左迁梧州' means 'Zheng was demoted to the governor of Wuzhou from the Hanlin Academy.' '相国自端溪窜于日南' means 'the chancellor was exiled to Rizhao from Duanxi.'
    • 谪居四年方获清雪以秘书监召还未久复持钧轴 - '谪居四年' means 'exiled for four years,' and '方获清雪' means 'finally cleared of the charges.' '以秘书监召还' means 'was recalled to serve as the Secretary of the Secretariat.' '未久复持钧轴' means 'not long after, he regained his high position.'
    • 或将甲第为献竟无所受 - '或将甲第为献' means 'or offered his grand mansion as a gift,' and '竟无所受' means 'but ultimately refused to accept it.'
    • 复于此宅寓居庭宇不加修餙清风俭德充塞寰宇 - '复于此宅寓居' means 'returned to live in this house,' and '庭宇不加修餙' means 'the courtyard and buildings were not renovated.' '清风俭德充塞寰宇' means 'his integrity and frugality filled the world.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贵之属筵席臻此者甚稀 - This phrase reflects the rarity of attaining high social status.
    • 厥后进贤徙居长兴 - Xian, a historical figure, moved to Changxing, a city in Zhejiang Province.
    • 其宅互为他人所有 - This suggests a history of frequent property transfers, possibly due to political or economic factors.
    • 咸通中刘相国罢北京亚尹复为翰林学士 - Liu, a high-ranking official, was dismissed and then reappointed, reflecting the fluid nature of political careers.
    • 数岁后自承防入相 - This indicates a rapid rise in political rank.
    • 尚以十千税焉 - The payment of a significant tax suggests the property's value.
    • 及出镇荆南朝野无不惋惜 - The widespread regret upon his departure highlights his importance.
    • 都城士庶以少及长闻之俱为涕泣 - The emotional response of the people underscores the impact of his actions.
    • 其后兴化萧相登庸举为自代表云 - Xiao, another high-ranking official, was promoted, indicating a continuation of political success.
    • 正人吞声而扼腕 - This phrase describes the frustration of righteous individuals.
    • 百姓掩泪于道途 - The emotional reaction of the common people reflects the broader societal sentiment.
    • 是时升道郑相国在内廷夜草麻制具述其事云 - Zheng, a chancellor, drafted an edict, emphasizing the importance of official documentation.
    • 安数亩之居仍非已有 - This suggests that despite his high status, he did not own the property.
    • 却四方之赂惟畏人知 - This indicates a strong moral stance against corruption.
    • 是时都下传写为之贵持权者覩其词大怒 - The powerful were angered by the content, suggesting political tensions.
    • 郑公自翰林承防左迁梧州相国自端溪窜于日南 - Zheng and the chancellor faced political setbacks, reflecting the volatile nature of court politics.
    • 谪居四年方获清雪以秘书监召还未久复持钧轴 - Zheng's eventual return to power shows the cyclical nature of political fortunes.
    • 或将甲第为献竟无所受 - This refusal of a grand mansion demonstrates a commitment to simplicity.
    • 复于此宅寓居庭宇不加修餙清风俭德充塞寰宇 - The unrenovated house and his frugal lifestyle reflect his moral integrity.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 貴之屬筵席臻此者甚稀 - This phrase is elliptical and requires careful interpretation.
    • 厥后进贤徙居长兴 - The sentence structure is simple but requires context to understand the sequence of events.
    • 其宅互为他人所有 - The passive construction indicates frequent property changes.
    • 咸通中刘相国罢北京亚尹复为翰林学士 - The sequence of events is clear but requires historical context.
    • 数岁后自承防入相 - The rapid rise in political rank is emphasized.
    • 尚以十千税焉 - The payment of a significant tax is noted.
    • 及出镇荆南朝野无不惋惜 - The widespread regret is highlighted.
    • 都城士庶以少及长闻之俱为涕泣 - The emotional response is described.
    • 其后兴化萧相登庸举为自代表云 - The promotion of Xiao is noted.
    • 正人吞声而扼腕 - The frustration of righteous individuals is described.
    • 百姓掩泪于道途 - The emotional reaction of the common people is highlighted.
    • 是时升道郑相国在内廷夜草麻制具述其事云 - The drafting of the edict is described.

    Those who attain such a high position in their social circles are very rare. Later, Xian moved to Changxing, and his house alternately became the property of others. During the year 864 of the Tang Dynasty, Liu, the chancellor, was dismissed from his post as deputy governor of Beijing and then appointed as a scholar in the Hanlin Academy. A few years later, he entered the position of chancellor after serving as a defense official and still paid a tax of 10,000 coins on the property. When he was sent to govern Jingnan, the court and the people all felt great regret. The people of the capital, from young to old, all heard of it and wept. Later, Xiao, a chancellor, was promoted to a high position and nominated as a representative. Righteous people silently suppressed their anger and clenched their fists, while the common people shed tears in the streets. At that time, Zheng, a chancellor, in the inner court, drafted the edict at night, detailing the matter. He said that a small house of a few acres was still not owned by him, and he refused gifts from all directions, only fearing that others would know. At that time, it was widely copied in the capital, and the powerful and influential people were greatly angered by the words. Zheng was demoted to the governor of Wuzhou from the Hanlin Academy, and the chancellor was exiled to Rizhao from Duanxi. Exiled for four years, he finally cleared of the charges and was recalled to serve as the Secretary of the Secretariat. Not long after, he regained his high position. Or offered his grand mansion as a gift, but ultimately refused to accept it. He returned to live in this house, and the courtyard and buildings were not renovated. His integrity and frugality filled the world. [44]

    君子曰仁义之感物也大哉刘公知帝道钦明欲贤人尽举四海之内翕然向风虽谪居累年再升鼎餁奸邪之口不能掩其善魑魅之域不能陷其身振誉一时流芳千载岂不伟欤其有冐官爵叨货贿怙宠专权身存名灭者一何谬哉
    　　李相国宅
    　　朱崖李相国德宅在安邑坊东南隅桑道茂谓为玉椀舍宇不甚宏侈而制度奇巧其间怪石古松俨图画在文宗武宗朝方秉化权威势与恩泽无比每好捜掇殊异朝野归附者多求宝玩献之尝因暇日休澣邀同列宰相及朝士宴语时畏景赫曦咸有郁蒸之病轩葢门已及亭午搢绅名士交扇不暇将期憩息于清凉之所既而延于小斋不甚高敞四壁施设皆古书名画俱有炎烁之虑及别列坐开樽烦暑都尽良久觉清飚爽气凛髙秋备设酒肴及昏而罢出户则火云烈日熇然焦灼有好事者求亲信问之云此日唯以金盆贮水渍白龙皮置于座末【龙皮有新罗僧得自海 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 君子曰 - Classical phrase meaning 'the gentleman says' or 'it is said by the wise.'
    • 仁义之感物也大哉 - Complex expression praising the profound influence of benevolence and righteousness on the world.
    • 欲贤人尽举四海之内翕然向风 - 'Wishes that all virtuous people come forward, and the entire world unites in following his lead.'
    • 虽谪居累年再升鼎餁 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to be reinstated to a high position' after exile for many years.
    • 奸邪之口不能掩其善 - The mouths of the wicked cannot conceal his virtue.
    • 魑魅之域不能陷其身 - The realm of evil spirits cannot trap his body.
    • 振誉一时流芳千载 - Gains fame in his time and leaves a lasting fragrance for a thousand years.
    • 岂不伟欤 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is this not great?'
    • 其有冐官爵叨货贿怙宠专权身存名灭者一何谬哉 - How great a mistake it is for those who assume office titles, receive bribes, rely on favor to seize power, and live in the flesh but die in reputation!

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 刘公 - Refers to Duke Liu, likely a historical figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 帝道 - Refers to the principles of governance and the way of the emperor, a central theme in Confucian thought.
    • 钦明 - A term often used to describe a sage ruler who is both reverent and enlightened.
    • 谪居 - Refers to exile or banishment, a common theme in Chinese history for those who fell out of favor with the emperor.
    • 魑魅 - Refers to evil spirits or deceitful people, often used metaphorically to describe corrupt or malicious individuals.
    • 流芳千载 - A common phrase in Chinese literature, referring to leaving a lasting legacy or reputation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘公 - Refers to Duke Liu, a historical figure.
    • 李相国 - Refers to Li, a high-ranking official or statesman.
    • 朱崖 - Refers to Zhu Ya, likely a place name or a title.
    • 桑道茂 - Refers to Sang Daomao, likely a historical figure or scholar.

    The wise say that the influence of benevolence and righteousness on the world is great indeed. Duke Liu understands the way of the emperor and is sagely enlightened, wishing that all virtuous people come forward, and the entire world unites in following his lead. Although exiled for many years, he was once again reinstated to a high position. The mouths of the wicked cannot conceal his virtue, and the realm of evil spirits cannot trap his body. He gains fame in his time and leaves a lasting fragrance for a thousand years. Is this not great? How great a mistake it is for those who assume office titles, receive bribes, rely on favor to seize power, and live in the flesh but die in reputation!

    Li Xiangguo's Residence

    The residence of Li Xiangguo of Zhu Ya is located in the southeastern corner of the Anyi Ward. Sang Daomao referred to it as the Jade Bowl, as the dwelling is not particularly grand but is uniquely ingenious in its design. Among its features are strange stones and ancient pines, which resemble paintings. During the reigns of Emperor Wen and Emperor Wu, Li Xiangguo wielded immense authority and influence, unmatched in power and favor. He often sought out rare and extraordinary items, and those who sought his favor frequently offered precious treasures. Once, during a day of leisure, he invited his fellow chancellors and court officials to a banquet. At the time, the intense sunlight caused everyone to suffer from the heat. The carriages and umbrellas had arrived by noon, and the scholars and gentlemen were fanning themselves frantically, hoping to find a cool place to rest. They were then invited to a small study, which was not particularly spacious but had walls adorned with ancient books and famous paintings. Despite the heat, they soon felt a refreshing breeze, as if they were in the cool autumn air. A full banquet was prepared, and they continued until dusk. When they left, the blazing sun and scorching heat were still intense. A curious person asked a trusted servant about it, who replied that on that day, only a basin of water soaked in white dragon skin was placed at the end of the seat. [The dragon skin was obtained by a monk from Silla from the sea.] [45]

    中云海旁有居者得之于渔扈其初以为鳞介之属曾有老人见而识之僧知相国好奇因以金帛赎之而献又暖金辟尘簪皆希代之寳及南迁悉为恶溪沉溺使昆仑没水求之在鳄鱼穴不能取】平泉庄去洛城三十里卉木台榭造仙府有虚槛前引泉水萦廻穿凿像巴峡洞庭十二峯九迄于海门皆见云霞龙凤草树之形有巨鱼脇骨一条长二丈五尺其上刻云防昌六年海州送到在东南隅即徴士韦楚老拾遗别墅楚老风韵髙致雅好山水相国居廊庙日以白衣擢升谏署后归平泉造门访之楚老避于山谷相国题诗云昔日徴黄诏余慙在凤池今来招隠士恨不见琼枝 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 中云海旁有居者得之于渔扈 - A descriptive phrase indicating that a resident near the sea obtained something from a fisherman or fisherfolk.
    • 其初以为鳞介之属 - Means 'initially thought it to be a type of fish or shellfish,' indicating the initial misidentification of the object.
    • 曾有老人见而识之 - Means 'an old man saw it and recognized it,' suggesting the presence of someone with knowledge.
    • 僧知相国好奇因以金帛赎之而献 - Means 'the monk knew the chancellor was curious and redeemed it with gold and silk and presented it,' indicating a transaction involving valuable items.
    • 又暖金辟尘簪皆希代之寳 - Means 'also warm gold bracelets, dust-proof hairpins, all rare treasures of the age,' listing valuable items.
    • 及南迁悉为恶溪沉溺 - Means 'when they moved south, all were lost in the bad stream,' indicating a loss during relocation.
    • 使昆仑没水求之在鳄鱼穴不能取 - Means 'even if one sends a Kunlun (a mythical figure) to dive into the water to search for it in the crocodile's den, it cannot be retrieved,' emphasizing the difficulty of retrieval.
    • 平泉庄去洛城三十里 - Means 'Pingquan Villa is thirty miles from Luoyang,' providing a geographical reference.
    • 卉木台榭造仙府 - Means 'the villa is adorned with flowers, trees, pavilions, and terraces, like a fairyland,' describing the beauty of the villa.
    • 有虚槛前引泉水萦廻穿凿像巴峡洞庭十二峯九迄于海门皆见云霞龙凤草树之形 - Means 'there is a veranda with a stream flowing through it, winding and cut, resembling the twelve peaks of Ba Gorge, the nine rivers of Dongting Lake, and the sea gate, all with the shapes of clouds, rainbows, dragons, phoenixes, grass, and trees,' describing the scenic features of the villa.
    • 有巨鱼脇骨一条长二丈五尺其上刻云防昌六年海州送到在东南隅即徴士韦楚老拾遗别墅楚老风韵髙致雅好山水相国居廊庙日以白衣擢升谏署后归平泉造门访之楚老避于山谷相国题诗云昔日徴黄诏余慙在凤池今来招隠士恨不见琼枝 - This long sentence describes the discovery of a large fish rib, its origin, and the subsequent events involving the chancellor and the poet Wei Chulao. It also includes the chancellor's poem.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中云海旁有居者得之于渔扈 - The sea and the fisherman suggest a coastal setting.
    • 其初以为鳞介之属 - Indicates the initial misidentification of the object, possibly a fossil or a rare artifact.
    • 曾有老人见而识之 - The old man's recognition suggests a knowledgeable individual.
    • 僧知相国好奇因以金帛赎之而献 - The monk's action reflects the cultural value placed on rare items and the chancellor's curiosity.
    • 又暖金辟尘簪皆希代之寳 - Lists valuable items, reflecting the wealth and status of the characters.
    • 及南迁悉为恶溪沉溺 - The relocation and loss suggest historical events or personal misfortune.
    • 使昆仑没水求之在鳄鱼穴不能取 - The use of a mythical figure and a dangerous location emphasizes the difficulty of retrieval.
    • 平泉庄去洛城三十里 - The geographical reference to Pingquan Villa and Luoyang provides a historical and cultural context.
    • 卉木台榭造仙府 - The description of the villa as a fairyland reflects the aesthetic and cultural values of the time.
    • 有虚槛前引泉水萦廻穿凿像巴峡洞庭十二峯九迄于海门皆见云霞龙凤草树之形 - The detailed description of the villa's features reflects the importance of natural beauty and architectural design.
    • 有巨鱼脇骨一条长二丈五尺其上刻云防昌六年海州送到在东南隅即徴士韦楚老拾遗别墅楚老风韵髙致雅好山水相国居廊庙日以白衣擢升谏署后归平泉造门访之楚老避于山谷相国题诗云昔日徴黄诏余慙在凤池今来招隠士恨不见琼枝 - This long sentence describes the discovery of a large fish rib, its origin, and the subsequent events involving the chancellor and the poet Wei Chulao. It also includes the chancellor's poem, reflecting the cultural and historical context of the time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中云海 - Refers to a sea or ocean.
    • 渔扈 - Refers to a fisherman or fisherfolk.
    • 鳞介之属 - Refers to fish or shellfish.
    • 僧 - Refers to a monk.
    • 相国 - Refers to a chancellor or high-ranking official.
    • 金帛 - Refers to gold and silk, valuable items.
    • 希代之寳 - Refers to rare treasures.
    • 南迁 - Refers to moving south.
    • 恶溪 - Refers to a bad stream or river.
    • 昆仑 - Refers to a mythical figure.
    • 鳄鱼穴 - Refers to a crocodile's den.
    • 平泉庄 - Refers to Pingquan Villa.
    • 洛城 - Refers to Luoyang.
    • 虚槛 - Refers to a veranda.
    • 泉水 - Refers to a stream of water.
    • 巴峡 - Refers to Ba Gorge.
    • 洞庭 - Refers to Dongting Lake.
    • 十二峯 - Refers to twelve peaks.
    • 九 - Refers to nine rivers.
    • 海门 - Refers to the sea gate.
    • 巨鱼脇骨 - Refers to a large fish rib.
    • 防昌六年 - Refers to the year 660 AD.
    • 海州 - Refers to Hai Prefecture.
    • 东南隅 - Refers to the southeast corner.
    • 徴士 - Refers to a recluse or hermit.
    • 韦楚老 - Refers to the poet Wei Chulao.
    • 拾遗别墅 - Refers to a villa.
    • 风韵髙致 - Refers to a high-spirited and refined temperament.
    • 雅好山水 - Refers to a love for natural landscapes.
    • 相国居廊庙 - Refers to the chancellor's position in the government.
    • 白衣擢升谏署 - Refers to the chancellor's rise to a high position.

    A Resident by the Sea Obtained Something from a Fisherman

    By the sea, there lived a resident who obtained something from a fisherman. Initially, he thought it to be a type of fish or shellfish. An old man saw it and recognized it. The monk, knowing that the chancellor was curious, redeemed it with gold and silk and presented it to him. The items included warm gold bracelets, dust-proof hairpins, all rare treasures of the age. When they moved south, all were lost in the bad stream. Even if one sends a Kunlun (a mythical figure) to dive into the water to search for it in the crocodile's den, it cannot be retrieved.

    Pingquan Villa is thirty miles from Luoyang. The villa is adorned with flowers, trees, pavilions, and terraces, like a fairyland. There is a veranda with a stream flowing through it, winding and cut, resembling the twelve peaks of Ba Gorge, the nine rivers of Dongting Lake, and the sea gate, all with the shapes of clouds, rainbows, dragons, phoenixes, grass, and trees. There is a large fish rib, one long twenty-five feet, with an inscription on it reading, "Sent from Hai Prefecture in the sixth year of Fanchang (660 AD)." It was found in the southeast corner of the villa. This was the villa of the recluse Wei Chulao, a poet known for his high-spirited and refined temperament and his love for natural landscapes. The chancellor, who had risen from a civilian position to a high office, later returned to Pingquan Villa and visited the villa. Wei Chulao, however, avoided him by hiding in the mountains. The chancellor then wrote a poem:

    "Once summoned by the imperial decree, I was ashamed to be in the phoenix pool.
    Now I come to seek the recluse, but alas, I cannot see the jade branches." [46]

    又新昌北街牛相国宅即宗朝将作监康旧第桑道茂谓之金杯俱出良相者也
    　　慈恩寺牡丹
    　　京国花卉之晨尤以牡丹为上至于佛宇道观游览者罕不经歴慈恩浴堂院有花两丛每开及五六百朶繁艳芬馥近少伦比有僧思振常话防昌中朝士数人寻芳遍诣僧室时东廊院有白花可爱相与倾酒而坐因云牡丹之盛葢亦奇矣然世之所玩者但浅红深紫而已竟未识红之深者院主老僧微笑曰安得无之但诸贤未见尔于是从而诘之经宿不去云上人向来之言当是曾有所覩必希相引寓目春游之愿足矣僧但云昔于他处一逢葢非辇毂所见及旦求之不已僧方露言曰众君子好尚如此贫道又安得藏之今欲同看此花但未知不泄于人否朝士作礼而誓云终身不复言之僧乃自开一房其间施设幡像有板壁遮以旧幕 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 又新昌北街牛相国宅即玄宗朝将作监康希旧第 - Describes the location and history of a residence: '又' means 'also' or 'furthermore', '新昌北街' is the street name, '牛相国' is the title and name of the owner, '玄宗朝' refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, '将作监' is the official title, and '康希旧第' means the former residence of Kang Xi.
    • 桑道茂谓之金杯俱出良相者也 - '桑道茂' is a person's name, '谓之' means 'called it', '金杯' means 'golden cup', and '俱出良相者也' means 'both produce good ministers'.
    • 京国花卉之晨尤以牡丹为上 - '京国' refers to the capital city, '花卉之晨' means 'in the morning of flowers', and '尤以牡丹为上' means 'especially the peony is the best'.
    • 至于佛宇道观游览者罕不经经历 - '至于' means 'as for', '佛宇道观' refers to Buddhist temples and Taoist shrines, and '游览者罕不经经历' means 'visitors rarely fail to visit'.

    The residence of the nobleman Niu, located on the north street of Xinchang, was originally the estate of Kang Xi, the supervisor of construction during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong. Sang Daomao called it the "Golden Cup," as both places produced virtuous ministers.

    The Peony of Cien Temple

    In the early morning of the capital city, the peony is especially esteemed among flowers. As for Buddhist temples and Taoist shrines, visitors rarely fail to visit. In the bathing hall of Cien Temple, there are two clusters of flowers. When they bloom, they produce hundreds of blossoms, each one richly colored and fragrant, nearly unparalleled. There was a monk named Si Zhen who often spoke of a group of officials from the Tang Dynasty who sought beauty and visited the temple. At the time, in the east corridor, there was a white flower that was particularly lovely. They gathered to drink wine and sit together, commenting that the abundance of peonies was indeed remarkable. However, the world's admiration is usually limited to light red and deep purple hues, and no one has ever seen a deeper shade of red. The head monk, an elderly monk, smiled and said, "How could there not be such a flower? It is just that you gentlemen have not seen it." They then questioned him, and he stayed the night. He said, "The words I spoke earlier must be true, as I have seen it before. I hope you will lead me to see it, and my wish for spring outings will be fulfilled." The monk merely said, "I once saw it in another place, far from the imperial capital. By dawn, they continued to seek it, and the monk finally revealed, "If you gentlemen are so fond of it, how could I keep it hidden? Today, I wish to show it to you together, but I do not know if you will keep it a secret." The officials took an oath, saying, "We swear to never speak of it again." The monk then opened a room, where there were banners and images set up, with a wooden partition covered by an old curtain. [47]

    幕下啓开而入至一院有小堂两间颇甚华防轩庑栏槛皆是柏材有殷红牡丹一窠婆娑几及千朶初旭才照露华半晞浓姿半开耀心目朝士惊赏留恋及暮而去僧曰予保惜栽培近二十年矣无端出语使人见之从今已往未知何如耳信宿有权要子弟与亲友数人同来入寺至有花僧院从容良久引僧至曲江闲歩将出门令小仆寄安茶笈裹以黄帕于曲江岸借草而坐忽有弟子奔走而来云有数十人入院掘花禁之不止僧俛首无言唯自吁叹坐中但相盻而笑既而却归至寺门见以大畚盛花舁而去取花者因谓僧曰窃知贵院旧有名花宅中咸欲一看不敢预有相告葢恐难于见舍适所寄笼子中有金三十两蜀茶二斤以为酬赠 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 幕下啓开而入 - Formal expression indicating entry into a tent or pavilion, likely a metaphor for entering a sacred or ceremonial space.
    • 有小堂两间 - Refers to two small halls or rooms, possibly part of a temple or monastery.
    • 颇甚华防 - Describes the halls as 'very ornate and splendid,' emphasizing their grandeur.
    • 轩庑栏槛 - Refers to the eaves, corridors, and railings of the building, highlighting its architectural features.
    • 皆是柏材 - Indicates that the materials used for construction are all made of cypress wood, a symbol of durability and elegance.
    • 殷红牡丹 - Refers to a deep red peony, a flower often associated with nobility and beauty in Chinese culture.
    • 婆娑几及千朶 - Describes the peony as sprawling and having nearly a thousand blooms, emphasizing its abundance and magnificence.
    • 初旭才照露华半晞 - Describes the early morning sunlight just beginning to dry the dew on the flowers, creating a serene and picturesque scene.
    • 浓姿半开 - Refers to the peony's rich and full appearance as it is partially open, suggesting a moment of transition and beauty.
    • 信宿 - Means 'for two nights,' indicating a short stay.
    • 权要子弟与亲友数人 - Refers to influential young men and several friends, suggesting a group of people with social standing.
    • 从容良久 - Indicates that they spent a long time there in a relaxed manner.
    • 曲江 - Refers to Qujiang, a famous scenic area in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), known for its beauty and cultural significance.
    • 借草而坐 - Describes sitting on the grass, suggesting a casual and natural setting.
    • 奔走而来 - Indicates that someone ran to bring news, emphasizing urgency.
    • 数十人入院掘花 - Refers to a group of people entering the garden to dig up the flowers, suggesting an act of destruction.
    • 禁之不止 - Indicates that attempts to stop them were unsuccessful.
    • 俛首无言 - Describes the monk as bowing his head and remaining silent, indicating resignation or sorrow.
    • 相盻而笑 - Refers to the guests looking at each other and laughing, suggesting a moment of shared amusement or mockery.
    • 取花者 - Refers to the people who took the flowers, likely the same group that dug them up.
    • 窃知贵院旧有名花 - Indicates that they knew the garden had a famous flower, suggesting prior knowledge or interest.
    • 宅中咸欲一看 - Refers to everyone wanting to see the flower, emphasizing its popularity.
    • 不敢预有相告 - Indicates that they did not inform anyone beforehand, suggesting a lack of respect or consideration.
    • 适所寄笼子中有金三十两蜀茶二斤 - Describes the gift of thirty taels of gold and two catties of Sichuan tea, indicating a valuable offering.
    • 以为酬赠 - Refers to the gift being given as a token of appreciation or payment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 幕下 - Refers to a tent or pavilion, often used in military or ceremonial contexts.
    • 小堂两间 - Small halls or rooms, likely part of a temple or monastery.
    • 华防 - Ornate and splendid, often used to describe grand buildings or spaces.
    • 轩庑栏槛 - Architectural elements of a building, emphasizing its beauty and craftsmanship.
    • 柏材 - Cypress wood, symbolizing durability and elegance.
    • 殷红牡丹 - Deep red peony, a symbol of nobility and beauty in Chinese culture.
    • 曲江 - A famous scenic area in Chang'an, known for its beauty and cultural significance.
    • 权要子弟 - Influential young men, often associated with the elite or powerful families.
    • 信宿 - For two nights, indicating a short stay.
    • 取花者 - People who took the flowers, likely the same group that dug them up.
    • 金三十两 - Thirty taels of gold, a significant amount of wealth.
    • 蜀茶二斤 - Two catties of Sichuan tea, a valuable gift.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This long sentence describes the setting and the peony's beauty, with a focus on the morning scene and the reaction of the visitors.
    • The monk's statement is a mix of personal sentiment and uncertainty, indicating his long-term care for the peony and his concern about its future.
    • This sequence describes the visit of influential young men and their friends, their actions, and the monk's reaction.
    • The final sequence describes the gift given by the people who took the flowers, indicating their attempt to compensate for their actions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 曲江 - A famous scenic area in Chang'an, known for its beauty and cultural significance.
    • 金三十两 - Thirty taels of gold, a significant amount of wealth.
    • 蜀茶二斤 - Two catties of Sichuan tea, a valuable gift.

    A pavilion was opened, and they entered a courtyard with two small halls, both very ornate and splendid, with eaves, corridors, and railings all made of cypress wood. There was a deep red peony bush, sprawling with nearly a thousand blooms. The early morning sun just began to dry the dew on the flowers, and the peony's rich and full appearance, partially open, dazzled the eyes. The officials were amazed and delighted, lingering until dusk before leaving. The monk said, "I have cherished and cultivated this peony for nearly twenty years. Now, I have spoken of it, and people have seen it. From now on, I do not know what will happen." Two nights later, several influential young men and their friends came together to the temple. They entered the hall with the peony, spending a long time there in a relaxed manner. The monk led them to Qujiang, where they took a leisurely walk. As they were about to leave, he asked a servant to place his tea chest, wrapped in a yellow cloth, on the grass by the Qujiang shore. Suddenly, a disciple ran to them, saying that a group of people had entered the garden and were digging up the flowers, despite attempts to stop them. The monk bowed his head and remained silent, only sighing to himself. The guests looked at each other and laughed. Soon after, they returned to the temple gate, where they saw the flowers being carried away in large baskets. The people who took the flowers then said to the monk, "We knew your garden had a famous peony, and everyone wanted to see it. We did not inform you beforehand, fearing it would be difficult to see it. The gift in the chest I just left contains thirty taels of gold and two catties of Sichuan tea as a token of appreciation." [48]

    管万敌遇壮士
    　　防昌中左军壮士管万敌富有膂力扛鼎挟辀众所推伏一日与侪軰防于东市酒肆忽有麻衣张葢者直入其座引觥而饮傍无人万敌振腕瞠目略无所惮同席恃勇之軰共为推挽竟不微动而观者渐众乃言曰某与管供奉较力以定强弱先请供奉拳某三拳后乞搭供奉一搭遂膊抱楼柱而立万敌怒其轻已欲令殒于手下尽力拳之如扣木石观者咸见楼柱与屋宇俱震其人略不微动既而笑曰到某搭供奉矣于是奋臂而起掌大如箕高及丈余屹屹而下前后有力之軰方甚恐栗知非常人众拥万敌谢而去之俄失所在万敌寝瘵月余力遂消减 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 管万敌 - A proper name, likely a historical figure or a legendary hero.
    • 膂力 - Refers to physical strength, particularly the strength of the arms and back.
    • 扛鼎挟辀 - A metaphorical expression describing great physical strength, akin to lifting a tripod or carrying a yoke.
    • 推伏 - Means to be respected and feared.
    • 侪軰 - Refers to fellow companions or peers.
    • 防昌中左军壮士 - Describes a brave warrior from the left division of the Fang Chang army.
    • 麻衣张葢者 - Refers to a person dressed in coarse clothing and holding an umbrella, possibly a commoner or a traveler.
    • 直入其座 - Means to enter directly into the seat, indicating boldness.
    • 引觥而饮 - Means to take up a drinking vessel and drink, showing confidence.
    • 傍无人 - Describes the absence of people around, emphasizing the scene's isolation.
    • 振腕瞠目 - Describes the act of shaking the wrist and glaring, indicating anger or defiance.
    • 略无所惮 - Means to show no fear or hesitation.
    • 推挽 - Refers to pushing and pulling, indicating attempts to restrain or move someone.
    • 微动 - Means to move slightly, suggesting minimal movement.
    • 较力 - Means to test strength or engage in a physical contest.
    • 拳 - Refers to punching or striking with a fist.
    • 扣木石 - Describes the sound of hitting wood or stone, indicating the force of the punches.
    • 楼柱与屋宇俱震 - Means that the pillar and the building were both shaken, emphasizing the intensity of the impact.
    • 略不微动 - Again, means to show no slight movement, reinforcing the opponent's resilience.
    • 搭 - Refers to a powerful strike or blow.
    • 箕 - Refers to a basket or scoop, used here to describe the size of the palm.
    • 屹屹而下 - Describes a powerful and steady descent, indicating the force of the blow.
    • 知非常人 - Means to recognize that the person is not ordinary.
    • 谢而去之 - Means to apologize and leave, showing respect and acknowledgment.
    • 俄失所在 - Means to suddenly disappear, indicating a mysterious departure.
    • 寝瘵 - Refers to illness or disease, particularly a serious condition.
    • 力遂消减 - Means that strength gradually diminished, indicating a decline in physical prowess.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 管万敌 - Likely a historical figure or a legendary hero, possibly from a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 防昌中左军 - Refers to a military unit, possibly from a specific battle or campaign.
    • 东市酒肆 - Refers to a market area with a tavern, a common gathering place in ancient China.
    • 楼柱与屋宇俱震 - Describes a physical phenomenon that would have been impressive and awe-inspiring in the context of the time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 管万敌 - A proper name, likely a historical figure or a legendary hero.
    • 防昌中左军 - A proper name for a military unit.
    • 东市酒肆 - A proper name for a specific location in the story.

    Guan Wandi Encounters a Martial Hero

    Guan Wandi, a brave warrior from the left division of the Fang Chang army, was renowned for his extraordinary physical strength, capable of lifting a tripod and carrying a yoke. He was respected and feared by all. One day, while drinking with his companions in a tavern in the eastern market, a man dressed in coarse clothing and holding an umbrella suddenly entered their seat and took up a drinking vessel to drink, with no one around. Guan Wandi, shaking his wrist and glaring, showed no fear or hesitation. His companions, who were also brave, tried to push and pull him, but he did not move slightly. As the number of onlookers grew, the man said, "I will test my strength against Guan Wendi to determine who is stronger. First, I will ask Guan Wendi to punch me three times, then I will deliver a single blow to him." He then stood with his arms around a pillar. Enraged by the man's audacity, Guan Wendi wanted to defeat him and struck with all his might, hitting like wood or stone. The onlookers saw the pillar and the building shake, but the man did not move slightly. He then smiled and said, "It is now my turn to deliver a blow to Guan Wendi." With a powerful arm, he rose, his palm as large as a basket, and descended with a force that reached over ten feet, standing firm. Those who were strong were greatly frightened and recognized that the man was no ordinary person. They gathered around Guan Wendi, who apologized and left. The man suddenly disappeared. Guan Wendi fell ill for over a month, and his strength gradually diminished. [49]

    张季宏逢恶新妇
    　　咸通中有左军张季宏勇而多力尝雨中经胜业坊遇泥泞深隘有村人驱驴负薪而至适当其道季宏怒之因捉驴四足掷过水渠数歩观者无不惊骇后供奉襄州暮泊商山逆旅逆旅有老妪谓其子曰恶人将归矣速令备办茶饭勿令喧噪既而愁愤吁叹咸有所惧季宏问之媪曰有新妇悖恶制之不可季宏曰向来见媪忧恐有何事是新妇岂不能共语媪曰客未知子细新妇壮勇无敌众皆畏惧遂至于此季宏笑曰其他则非某所知言壮勇当为主人除之母与子遽叩头曰此则母子无患矣虽然穷阙 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 捉驴四足掷过水渠数步 - A vivid description of Zhang Jihong's strength, grabbing the donkey by its four feet and throwing it over a water channel several steps away.
    • 观者无不惊骇 - Emphasizes the reaction of the spectators, all of whom were amazed and frightened.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸通 - The era name of Emperor Xianzong of Tang, indicating the time period of the story.
    • 左军 - Refers to a military unit, suggesting Zhang Jihong's military background.
    • 胜业坊 - A specific location in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), the capital of the Tang dynasty.
    • 商山 - A mountain range in Shaanxi province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 逆旅 - Refers to a guesthouse or inn, a common place for travelers to stay.
    • 新妇 - Refers to a newly married woman, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a young wife.
    • 穷阙 - A term that can mean 'poverty' or 'destitution,' reflecting the economic status of the family.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张季宏 - The name of the main character.
    • 胜业坊 - A specific location in Chang'an.
    • 商山 - A mountain range in Shaanxi province.
    • 逆旅 - A guesthouse or inn.
    • 新妇 - A newly married woman.
    • 穷阙 - A term for poverty or destitution.

    In the era of Xianzong (Salty Tongue), there was a man named Zhang Jihong from the Left Army, who was brave and strong. Once in the rain, he passed through Shengye Lane and encountered deep and narrow mud. A villager was driving a donkey carrying firewood and happened to be in the way. Zhang Jihong became angry and grabbed the donkey by its four feet, throwing it over a water channel several steps away. All spectators were amazed and frightened.

    Later, serving as an attendant in Xiangzhou, he stopped for the night at a guesthouse in Shangshan. At the guesthouse, an old woman told her son, "The evil man is returning. Quickly prepare food and tea, and do not let him be disturbed."

    Earlier, I saw the old woman worried. What is the matter? Is the new wife unable to speak?" Zhang Jihong asked. The old woman replied, "The guest does not know other things. But I will use my strength to help the master. The mother and son quickly bowed, saying, 'If you can help us, we will be free of worry, although we are poor.'"

    [50]

    当为酬赠顷之邻伍乡社悉来观视日暮妇人负束薪而归状貌亦无他异逆旅后囿有盘石季宏坐其上置骡鞭于侧召而谓曰汝是主人新妇我在长安城即闻汝倚有气力不伏承事阿家岂敢如此新妇拜季宏曰乞押衙不草草容新妇分雪新妇不敢不承事阿家自是大人憎嫌新妇其媪在傍谓曰汝勿向客前妄有词理新妇因言曰只如某年月日如此事岂是新妇不是每言一事引手于季宏所坐石上以中指画之随手作痕深可数寸季宏汗落神骇但言道理不错阖扉假寐伺晨而发及问之新妇已他适矣 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 酬贈 - Means 'return gift' or 'reciprocal gift,' often used in the context of mutual obligations or exchanges.
    • 無他異 - Means 'no other abnormalities,' suggesting that the woman appears normal.
    • 分雪 - Means 'to clarify' or 'to explain,' often used in the context of resolving misunderstandings.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 逆旅 - In Classical Chinese literature, inns or lodging places often serve as settings for encounters or conflicts, reflecting the transient nature of human relationships.
    • 盤石 - The use of a large flat stone as a seat is a common feature in traditional Chinese settings, symbolizing stability and permanence.
    • 押衙 - This title suggests a position of authority, possibly a military or administrative official, indicating the social status of the character.
    • 阿家 - This term reflects the hierarchical and respectful nature of relationships in traditional Chinese society, particularly in the context of family and marriage.

    Proper Nouns

    • 季宏 - A proper name, likely referring to a specific individual in the story.

    The woman was expected to return a gift. Soon, all the neighbors and fellow villagers came to watch. As dusk fell, the woman returned home carrying a bundle of firewood, and her appearance showed no other abnormalities. At the inn, there was a garden with a large flat stone. Season Hong sat on it, placing his horsewhip beside him. He called out to her and said, "You are the new wife of the owner. I heard in Chang'an that you rely on your strength and do not submit to your husband. How dare you act this way?" The woman bowed to Season Hong and said, "Please, Sir, do not be hasty. Allow me to explain. I dare not fail to serve my husband, but since then, the master has been displeased with me." The old woman standing nearby said, "Do not speak nonsense in front of the guest." The woman then said, "For example, on a certain day and month, such an event occurred. How could it be my fault? For every matter I mention, I draw on the stone where Season Hong is sitting with my middle finger, and it leaves a deep mark several inches long." Season Hong was sweating profusely and was terrified, but he only said that the reasoning was correct. He closed the door, pretended to sleep, and waited until morning to leave. When he later inquired about the woman, she had already gone elsewhere. [51]

    玉蘂院眞人降
    　　上都安业坊唐昌观旧有玉蘂花甚繁每发瑶林琼树元和中春物方盛车马寻玩者相继忽一日有女子年可十七八衣绣绿衣乘马峩髻双鬟无簮珥之饰容色婉约迥出于众从以二女冠三女仆仆者皆丱头黄衫端丽无比既下马以白角扇障面直造花所异香芬馥闻于数十歩之外观者以为出自宫掖莫敢逼而视之伫立良久令小仆取花数枝而出将乘马谓黄冠者曰曩者玉峯之约自此可以行矣时观者如堵咸觉烟霏唳景物辉焕举辔百歩有轻风拥尘随之而去须防尘灭望之已在半天方悟神仙之游余香不散者经月余日时 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 玉蘂院眞人降 - Formal expression meaning 'the Immortal of the Jade Fragrance Courtyard descended,' describing a divine or supernatural event.
    • 上都安業坊唐昌觀舊有玉蘂花甚繁 - Refers to 'the Tang Chang Temple in Anyei Ward, the Capital City, which had many jade fragrance flowers,' setting the scene and introducing the presence of a specific flower.
    • 元和中春物方盛車馬尋玩者相繼 - Means 'during the Yuanhe period when spring was in full bloom and carriages and horses came one after another to enjoy the scenery,' setting the scene of a bustling springtime in the capital city.
    • 衣繡綠衣乘馬峩髻雙鬟無簪珥之飾容色婉約迥出於衆 - Describes the woman's appearance: 'wearing a green brocade robe, riding a horse, with an elaborate hairstyle and two buns, without any hairpins or earrings, her appearance was graceful and stood out among the crowd.'
    • 異香芬馥聞於數十歩之外 - Means 'a fragrant aroma that could be smelled from dozens of steps away,' describing the unusual scent emanating from the woman.
    • 觀者以為出自宮掖 - Means 'the onlookers thought she came from the imperial palace,' indicating the perception of the woman's origin.
    • 佇立良久令小僕取花數枝而出 - Means 'stood for a long time, then ordered a young servant to pick several branches of flowers and leave,' describing the woman's actions.
    • 玉峯之約自此可以行矣 - Refers to 'a previous agreement or promise related to Jade Peak,' which is likely a reference to a place or a metaphorical concept.
    • 輕風擁塵隨之而去 - Means 'a light wind carried the dust away as she left,' describing the woman's departure.
    • 舉辔百歩有輕風擁塵隨之而去 - Means 'she rode a hundred steps with a light wind carrying the dust away,' further describing her departure.
    • 望之已在半天 - Means 'looking up, she was already halfway to the sky,' emphasizing the supernatural nature of her ascent.
    • 餘香不散者經月餘日 - Means 'the lingering fragrance lasted for more than a month,' describing the lasting effect of her presence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玉蘂院 - Refers to a specific location or temple known as the 'Jade Fragrance Courtyard,' which may have historical or religious significance.
    • 唐昌觀 - Refers to the 'Tang Chang Temple,' a specific temple in the capital city, which may have historical or religious significance.
    • 元和 - Refers to the Yuanhe period (806-820 AD) during the Tang Dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 宮掖 - Refers to the imperial palace or the inner quarters of the palace, indicating a high social status or divine origin.
    • 玉峯 - Likely refers to a place or a metaphorical concept related to jade and peaks, which may have symbolic or religious significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玉蘂院 - The Jade Fragrance Courtyard, a specific location or temple.
    • 唐昌觀 - The Tang Chang Temple, a specific temple in the capital city.
    • 元和 - The Yuanhe period (806-820 AD) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宮掖 - The imperial palace or the inner quarters of the palace.

    The Immortal of the Jade Fragrance Courtyard Descended
    At the Tang Chang Temple in Anyei Ward of the Capital City, there were once many jade fragrance flowers, which bloomed like a forest of jade and crystal. During the Yuanhe period, when spring was in full bloom, carriages and horses came one after another to enjoy the scenery. Suddenly, one day, a girl around seventeen or eighteen years old appeared. She was wearing a green brocade robe, riding a horse, with an elaborate hairstyle and two buns, without any hairpins or earrings. Her appearance was graceful and stood out among the crowd. Accompanied by two female priests and three female servants, all dressed in yellow robes and looking extremely beautiful, she dismounted and used a white horn fan to shield her face as she went straight to the flower spot. An unusual fragrance filled the air, reaching dozens of steps away. The onlookers thought she came from the imperial palace and dared not approach her. She stood there for a long time, then ordered a young servant to pick several branches of flowers and leave. As she was about to ride away, she said to the priest in the yellow robe, "Now the agreement made at Jade Peak can be fulfilled." At that time, the crowd was like a wall, and everyone felt the presence of smoke and birds, with the scenery shining brightly. She rode a hundred steps, with a light wind carrying the dust away as she left. After a while, the dust disappeared, and looking up, she was already halfway to the sky. It was then realized that it was a journey of the immortals. The lingering fragrance lasted for more than a month. [52]

    严给事休复元相国刘賔客白醉吟俱有闻玉蘂院眞人降诗严给事诗曰味道斋心祷至神魂消眼冷未逢眞不如满树琼瑶蘂笑对藏花洞里人又云羽车潜下玉龟山尘界无由覩蕣顔惟有无情枝上雪好风吹缀绿云鬟元相国诗曰弄玉潜过玉树时不敎青鸟出花枝的应未有诸人觉只是严郎卜得知刘賔客诗云玉女来看玉树花异香先引七香车攀枝弄雪时廻首惊怪人间日易斜又云雪蘂琼丝满院春羽衣轻歩不生尘君王帘下徒相问长记吹箫别有人白醉吟诗云嬴女偷乘鸾去时洞中潜歇弄琼枝不缘啼鸟春饶舌青锁仙郎可得知
    　　宰相布施 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 味道齋心 - A Daoist practice of inner cultivation and meditation.
    • 魂消眼冷 - Describes a state of deep contemplation or spiritual absorption.
    • 羽衣輕歩不生塵 - Describes the immortal's light steps that do not stir up dust.
    • 長記吹簫別有人 - Means 'long remember the one who played the flute and parted ways.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 嚴給事 - Yan Geshi, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元相國劉賔客 - Liu Yuxi, a prominent statesman and poet who served as Chancellor.
    • 白醉吟 - Bai Juyi, a famous poet known for his works on wine and poetry.
    • 玉蘂院 - A mythical garden associated with immortals.
    • 玉龜山 - A mythical mountain associated with immortals.
    • 弄玉 - A reference to the myth of Xiang Yu and his wife, Lady Xiang.
    • 青鳥 - A mythical bird often associated with immortals.
    • 玉樹 - A mythical tree associated with immortals.
    • 君王 - Refers to the emperor.
    • 青鎖仙郎 - Refers to the immortal who is secluded in a green-locked place.

    Proper Nouns

    • 嚴給事 - Yan Geshi
    • 元相國劉賔客 - Liu Yuxi
    • 白醉吟 - Bai Juyi
    • 玉蘂院 - Jade Pear Blossom Garden
    • 玉龜山 - Jade Turtle Mountain
    • 弄玉 - Lady Xiang
    • 青鳥 - Mythical bird
    • 玉樹 - Mythical tree
    • 君王 - Emperor
    • 青鎖仙郎 - Secluded immortal

    Yan Geshi, Liu Yuxi, and Bai Juyi all wrote poems about the immortal descending from the Jade Pear Blossom Garden. Yan Geshi's poem says: "In inner cultivation and prayer, the spirit fades and the eyes grow cold; not meeting the true one is not as good as facing the jade pear blossoms and laughing with the inhabitants of the flower cave." He also says: "The feathered carriage descends from the Jade Turtle Mountain; in the mortal world, one cannot see the beauty of the immortal; only the snow on the branches, blown by the wind, weaves through the green hair of the immortal." Liu Yuxi's poem says: "The immortal maiden passes through the jade tree without allowing the green bird to emerge from the flower branches; surely no one else has noticed, only Yan Geshi could predict this." He also says: "The immortal maid looks at the jade tree flowers; the fragrant car leads the way; she plays with the snow and turns her head, amazed at how quickly time passes in the mortal world." Bai Juyi's poem says: "The immortal maiden steals a ride on a phoenix; in the cave, she rests and plays with the jade branches; not because the birds sing too much in spring, the secluded immortal can know."

    The Chancellor's alms. [53]

    干符中有宰相自中书还第使人以布囊盛钱数千防路以施丐者于是贫乏相率罗列路隅所分既微渐不能普台行李无复威仪时有朝士投牋谏之其略云方今兵冦互兴民力凋弊所望明公弼成大化弥纶纪纲举贤任能以光庶事俾万物各得其理百姓日用不知损不急之官杜私门之请如此则刑清俗富天下自无穷人不宜专政庙堂方行小惠昔子产以已车济人于溱洧君子谓不知为政不如以时修桥梁惟明公察焉执政者览书慿怒俄而巢防陷京遂及于难
    　　崔道枢食井鱼
    　　中书舎人韦顔子壻崔道枢举进士干符二年春下第归寜汉上所居因井渫得鲤鱼一头长可五尺鳞鬛金色目光射人所视异于常鱼令仆投于江水道枢与表兄韦氏密备鼎爼烹而食之经信宿韦得疾暴卒有碧衣人引至府舎宇颇甚严肃既入门见防事有女人戴金翠冠着紫绣衣据案而坐左右侍者皆黄衫金栉如宫内之饰有一人吏从执簿领而出及轩陛间付双鬟青衣着于绣衣案上更引韦生东庑曹署理诘杀鱼之状韦引过道枢云非某之罪吏曰此 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 干符中 - Refers to the Dagan period (乾符, 874–879) during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宰相自中书还第 - Means 'the prime minister returned home from the Zhongshu Sheng (Chancellery).'
    • 使人以布囊盛钱数千防路 - Means 'had someone carry thousands of coins in a cloth bag to distribute on the road.'
    • 以施丐者 - Means 'to give to beggars.'
    • 贫乏相率罗列路隅 - Means 'the poor gathered along the roadside.'
    • 所分既微渐不能普 - Means 'the distribution became too small to reach everyone.'
    • 台行李无复威仪 - Means 'the prime minister's entourage lost its dignity and decorum.'
    • 朝士投牋谏之 - Means 'an official submitted a memorial to advise against it.'
    • 其略云 - Means 'the main points were as follows.'
    • 方今兵冦互兴民力凋弊 - Means 'nowadays, with frequent wars and the people's strength depleted.'
    • 所望明公弼成大化 - Means 'we hope the great minister will help perfect the great transformation.'
    • 弥纶纪纲举贤任能 - Means 'to establish and maintain the norms, promote the virtuous, and appoint the capable.'
    • 以光庶事俾万物各得其理 - Means 'to illuminate all matters and ensure that all things are in their proper order.'
    • 百姓日用不知损不急之官杜私门之请 - Means 'the common people do not know about the reduction of unnecessary officials and the blocking of private requests.'
    • 如此则刑清俗富天下自无穷人 - Means 'if this is done, then punishments will be clear, customs will be rich, and there will be no destitute people in the world.'
    • 不宜专政庙堂方行小惠 - Means 'it is not appropriate to monopolize power in the government while only performing small favors.'
    • 昔子产以已车济人于溱洧 - Refers to the historical figure Zǐchǎn (子产) who helped people cross the rivers Zhēn and Wěi with his own carriage.
    • 君子谓不知为政不如以时修桥梁 - Means 'the wise men said that not knowing how to govern is worse than not building bridges in time.'
    • 执政者览书慿怒俄而巢防陷京遂及于难 - Means 'the prime minister read the memorial and became angry; shortly after, the rebels occupied the capital, and he met his downfall.'
    • 崔道枢食井鱼 - Means 'Cui Daoshu ate a fish from the well.'
    • 中书舎人韦顔子壻崔道枢举进士干符二年春下第归寜汉上 - Means 'Cui Daoshu, the son-in-law of Wei Yanzizi, who was a mid-level official in the Chancellery, failed the imperial examination in the spring of the second year of the Dagan period and returned to his hometown in Hanzhong.'
    • 所居因井渫得鲤鱼一头长可五尺鳞鬛金色目光射人所视异于常鱼 - Means 'at his home, a carp with a golden scale and a fierce gaze, about five feet long, was found in the well.'
    • 令仆投于江水 - Means 'he ordered his servant to throw it into the river.'
    • 道枢与表兄韦氏密备鼎爼烹而食之 - Means 'Cui Daoshu and his cousin Wei prepared a feast and cooked the fish to eat it.'
    • 经信宿韦得疾暴卒 - Means 'after two nights, Wei fell ill and died suddenly.'
    • 有碧衣人引至府舎宇颇甚严肃 - Means 'a man in green clothes led him to a grand and solemn mansion.'
    • 既入门见防事有女人戴金翠冠着紫绣衣据案而坐 - Means 'upon entering, he saw a woman wearing a gold and jade crown and a purple embroidered robe sitting at a desk.'
    • 左右侍者皆黄衫金栉如宫内之饰 - Means 'her attendants wore yellow robes and gold combs, similar to those in the palace.'
    • 有一人吏从执簿领而出及轩陛间付双鬟青衣着于绣衣案上 - Means 'a clerk carrying a ledger came out and placed it on the woman's desk.'
    • 更引韦生东庑曹署理诘杀鱼之状 - Means 'the clerk then led Wei to an eastern hall where he was questioned about the killing of the fish.'
    • 韦引过道枢云非某之罪 - Means 'Wei blamed Cui Daoshu, saying it was not his fault.'
    • 吏曰 - Means 'the clerk said.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 干符 - The reign title of Emperor Xizong of Tang (唐僖宗, 873–888).
    • 宰相 - Refers to the prime minister, the highest-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty government.
    • 中书 - The Chancellery, the central administrative body of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 布囊盛钱 - A common method of distributing alms in ancient China.
    • 施丐者 - Giving to beggars, a practice often associated with charity and social welfare.
    • 贫乏 - The poor and destitute.
    • 台 - The prime minister's entourage.
    • 朝士 - Officials in the imperial court.
    • 投牋谏之 - Submitting a memorial to advise against a policy or action.
    • 兵冦互兴 - Frequent military conflicts and invasions.
    • 民力凋弊 - The depletion of the people's strength and resources.
    • 弼成大化 - Helping to perfect the great transformation, a reference to the ideal of good governance.
    • 弥纶纪纲 - Establishing and maintaining the norms and regulations.
    • 举贤任能 - Promoting the virtuous and appointing the capable.
    • 以光庶事 - To illuminate all matters, a metaphor for effective governance.
    • 俾万物各得其理 - Ensuring that all things are in their proper order.
    • 损不急之官 - Reducing unnecessary officials.
    • 杜私门之请 - Blocking private requests, a reference to corruption and favoritism.
    • 刑清俗富 - Clear punishments and prosperous customs.
    • 天下自无穷人 - There will be no destitute people in the world.
    • 专政庙堂 - Monopolizing power in the government.
    • 方行小惠 - Performing small favors, a reference to superficial acts of kindness.
    • 子产 - A famous statesman and philosopher of the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 溱洧 - Rivers in ancient China, often mentioned in historical contexts.
    • 君子谓不知为政不如以时修桥梁 - A historical reference to the importance of practical governance.
    • 执政者 - The prime minister or high-ranking officials.
    • 览书慿怒 - Reading the memorial and becoming angry.
    • 巢防陷京 - The rebellion led by Huang Chao (黄巢) that captured the capital.
    • 遂及于难 - Meeting his downfall.
    • 崔道枢 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 中书舎人 - A mid-level official in the Chancellery.
    • 韦顔子壻 - The son-in-law of Wei Yanzizi.
    • 干符二年春 - The spring of the second year of the Dagan period (875).
    • 下第归寜汉上 - Failing the imperial examination and returning to his hometown in Hanzhong.
    • 所居因井渫得鲤鱼 - Finding a carp in the well.
    • 长可五尺 - About five feet long.
    • 鳞鬛金色 - Golden scales.
    • 目光射人 - A fierce gaze.
    • 所视异于常鱼 - Different from ordinary fish.
    • 令仆投于江水 - Ordering the servant to throw it into the river.
    • 密备鼎爼 - Preparing a feast.
    • 烹而食之 - Cooking and eating it.
    • 经信宿 - After two nights.
    • 韦得疾暴卒 - Wei fell ill and died suddenly.
    • 碧衣人 - A man in green clothes.
    • 府舎宇 - A grand and solemn mansion.
    • 防事有女人 - A woman in charge.
    • 戴金翠冠 - Wearing a gold and jade crown.
    • 着紫绣衣 - Wearing a purple embroidered robe.
    • 据案而坐 - Sitting at a desk.
    • 左右侍者 - Attendants.
    • 黄衫金栉 - Yellow robes and gold combs.
    • 宫内之饰 - Similar to those in the palace.
    • 有一人吏从执簿领而出及轩陛间付双鬟青衣着于绣衣案上 - A clerk carrying a ledger came out and placed it on the woman's desk.
    • 更引韦生东庑曹署理诘杀鱼之状 - The clerk then led Wei to an eastern hall where he was questioned about the killing of the fish.
    • 韦引过道枢云非某之罪 - Wei blamed Cui Daoshu, saying it was not his fault.
    • 吏曰 - The clerk said.

    During the Dagan period, a prime minister returned home from the Zhongshu Sheng and had someone carry thousands of coins in a cloth bag to distribute on the road to give to beggars. As a result, the poor gathered along the roadside. However, the distribution became too small to reach everyone, and the prime minister's entourage lost its dignity and decorum. At that time, an official submitted a memorial to advise against this practice. The main points were as follows: Nowadays, with frequent wars and the people's strength depleted, we hope the great minister will help perfect the great transformation, establish and maintain the norms, promote the virtuous, and appoint the capable, to illuminate all matters and ensure that all things are in their proper order. The common people do not know about the reduction of unnecessary officials and the blocking of private requests. If this is done, then punishments will be clear, customs will be rich, and there will be no destitute people in the world. It is not appropriate to monopolize power in the government while only performing small favors. The wise men said that not knowing how to govern is worse than not building bridges in time. The prime minister read the memorial and became angry; shortly after, the rebels occupied the capital, and he met his downfall.

    Cui Daoshu, the son-in-law of Wei Yanzizi, who was a mid-level official in the Chancellery, failed the imperial examination in the spring of the second year of the Dagan period and returned to his hometown in Hanzhong. At his home, a carp with a golden scale and a fierce gaze, about five feet long, was found in the well. He ordered his servant to throw it into the river. Cui Daoshu and his cousin Wei prepared a feast and cooked the fish to eat it. After two nights, Wei fell ill and died suddenly. A man in green clothes led him to a grand and solemn mansion. Upon entering, he saw a woman wearing a gold and jade crown and a purple embroidered robe sitting at a desk. Her attendants wore yellow robes and gold combs, similar to those in the palace. A clerk carrying a ledger came out and placed it on the woman's desk. The clerk then led Wei to an eastern hall where he was questioned about the killing of the fish. Wei blamed Cui Daoshu, saying it was not his fault. The clerk said, "This matter will be investigated further." [54]

    雨龙也潜伏于江海湫湄虽人所食即无从而辨矣但昨者得之于井中崔氏与君又非愚昧杀而噉之俱难获免然君且却还试与崔广为佛道功德庶几消减其过自兹浃旬当复相召韦忽然而寤具以所説话于眷属命道枢具述其事道枢虽懐忧廹亦未深信才经及旬余韦生果殁韦乃道枢姑之子也数日后寄梦于母云以杀鱼获罪所至之地即水府非久当受重谴可急修黄箓道斋尚兾得寛刑辟表兄之过亦成矣今夕当自知其事韦母泣告道枢及瞑昬然而寝
    复见碧衣人引至公署俱是韦之所述俄有吏执黑丹书文字立道枢于屏侧疾趋而入见绣衣操笔而书讫吏接之而出令道枢览之其初云某官登四品年至七十二其后有判词云崔道枢所害雨龙事关天府原之不可按罪急追所有官爵并皆削除年寿亦减一半时道枢三十五矣夜分而寤恍惚悲涕莫知所为时节在冬季其母方为修崇福力才及春首抱疾数日而终时崔之妻孥咸在京师紫微备述其事【旧传防州及牛渚矶皆是水府未详道枢所至何所也】 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 潛伏於江海湫湄 - Lying hidden in rivers, seas, and marshy places; describes the secretive nature of the rain dragon.
    • 雖人所食即無從而辨矣 - Even if humans eat it, there is no way to distinguish it; highlights the subtlety of the dragon's presence.
    • 崔氏與君又非愚昧 - Cui's family and you are not foolish; suggests that both parties are intelligent and aware of the situation.
    • 殺而噉之俱難獲免 - Killing and eating it would both be difficult to escape; indicates the severe consequences of such actions.
    • 試與崔廣為佛道功德 - Try to perform Buddhist and Daoist good deeds with Cui Guang; suggests a spiritual solution to the problem.
    • 庶幾消減其過 - Perhaps to reduce the sin; expresses a hope for redemption through good deeds.
    • 自兹浃旬当复相召 - From now on, every ten days, it will call again; indicates a recurring pattern of the dragon's appearances.
    • 韦忽然而寤 - Wei suddenly woke up; describes a sudden awakening or realization.
    • 具以所说话于眷属 - Told all that was said to his relatives; indicates a detailed recounting of the events.
    • 命道枢具述其事 - Ordered Dao Chu to recount the story in detail; shows the importance of recording the events.
    • 道枢虽懐忧廹亦未深信 - Dao Chu was deeply worried but did not fully believe; highlights his skepticism.
    • 韦乃道枢姑之子也 - Wei is the son of Dao Chu's aunt; provides a familial connection.
    • 寄梦于母云 - Sent a dream to his mother saying; indicates a dream communication.
    • 杀鱼获罪所至之地即水府 - Killed a fish and committed a sin, the place he went to was the Water Realm; explains the consequence of the sin.
    • 非久当受重谴 - Soon he will receive a severe punishment; indicates an impending severe consequence.
    • 可急修黄箓道斋 - You can urgently perform the Yellow Scripture Daoist ritual; suggests a specific religious ritual to mitigate the sin.
    • 尚兾得寛刑辟 - Still hoping to receive lenient punishment; expresses a desire for mercy.
    • 表兄之过亦成矣 - The sin of my brother-in-law is also complete; indicates the involvement of another person.
    • 今夕当自知其事 - Tonight he will know the matter himself; suggests a personal realization.
    • 韦母泣告道枢 - Wei's mother wept and told Dao Chu; shows emotional distress and communication.
    • 夜分而寤 - Woke up at midnight; indicates a late-night awakening.
    • 恍惚悲涕 - Dazed and weeping; describes a state of confusion and sorrow.
    • 莫知所为 - Not knowing what to do; indicates a state of helplessness.
    • 时节在冬季 - The season was winter; sets the time frame.
    • 其母方为修崇福力 - His mother was just performing the Great Blessing ritual; indicates a religious activity.
    • 才及春首抱疾数日而终 - Only a few days into spring, he fell ill and died; describes the timeline of events.
    • 紫微备述其事 - Ziwei recounted the matter in detail; indicates a detailed recounting by another person.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 雨龙 - A rain dragon, a mythical creature in Chinese folklore associated with rain and water.
    • 防州 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific region or city in ancient China.
    • 牛渚矶 - A place name, referring to a specific location known for its association with water and possibly the Water Realm.
    • 水府 - The Water Realm, a mythological domain associated with water spirits and deities.
    • 黄箓道斋 - A specific Daoist ritual, the Yellow Scripture Daoist ritual, used for purification and protection.

    Proper Nouns

    • 雨龙 - The rain dragon.
    • 崔氏 - Cui's family.
    • 韦 - Wei.
    • 道枢 - Dao Chu.
    • 防州 - A place name.
    • 牛渚矶 - A place name.
    • 水府 - The Water Realm.
    • 黄箓道斋 - The Yellow Scripture Daoist ritual.
    • 紫微 - Ziwei.

    The Rain Dragon lies hidden in rivers, seas, and marshy places; even if humans eat it, there is no way to distinguish it. But yesterday, it was caught in a well. Cui's family and you are not foolish; killing and eating it would both be difficult to escape. Still, you should return and try to perform Buddhist and Daoist good deeds with Cui Guang, perhaps to reduce the sin. From now on, every ten days, it will call again. Wei suddenly woke up and told all that was said to his relatives, ordering Dao Chu to recount the story in detail. Dao Chu was deeply worried but did not fully believe. Only a few days later, Wei died. Wei is the son of Dao Chu's aunt. A few days later, Wei sent a dream to his mother, saying, "Killed a fish and committed a sin, the place I went to was the Water Realm. Soon I will receive a severe punishment. You can urgently perform the Yellow Scripture Daoist ritual, still hoping to receive lenient punishment. The sin of my brother-in-law is also complete. Tonight I will know the matter myself." Wei's mother wept and told Dao Chu. At midnight, she woke up dazed and weeping, not knowing what to do. The season was winter, and his mother was just performing the Great Blessing ritual. Only a few days into spring, he fell ill and died. At that time, Cui's wife and children were all in the capital. Ziwei recounted the matter in detail. [According to old legends, Fangzhou and Niuzhuji are both the Water Realm, but it is unclear where Dao Chu went.] [55]

    洛中豪士
    　　干符中洛中有豪贵子弟承借勲荫物用优足恣陈锦衣玉食不以充诎为戒饮华鲜极口腹之欲有李史君出牧罢归居止亦在东洛深感其家恩旧欲召诸子从容有敬爱寺僧圣刚者常所来往李因以其宴为説僧曰某与之门徒久矣每见其饮食穷极水陆滋味常必以炭炊往往不惬其意此乃骄逸成性史君召之可乎李曰求象白猩唇恐不可致止于精洁修办小筵未为难事于是广求珍异俾妻孥亲为调鼎备陈绮席雕盘日为请弟兄列坐矜持俨氷玉肴羞每至曾不下筯主人揖之再三惟霑果实而已及至水餐俱致一匙于口然相盻良久咸飱荼食蘖李莫究其由以失餁为谢明日 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 干符中 - Specific period in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛中有豪贵子弟 - Wealthy and influential young men in Luoyang.
    • 承借勳荫 - Inheriting privileges and honors through family connections.
    • 物用优足 - Having abundant material possessions.
    • 恣陈锦衣玉食 - Living a life of luxury with fine clothes and delicious food.
    • 不以充诎为戒 - Not being cautious about overindulgence.
    • 饮华鲜 - Drinking fine and rare beverages.
    • 极口腹之欲 - Satisfying all desires related to food and drink.
    • 李史君出牧罢归 - Li, a former official, returning from his term as an administrator.
    • 居止亦在东洛 - Li's residence is also in Eastern Luoyang.
    • 深感其家恩旧 - Deep gratitude for past favors.
    • 欲召诸子从容 - Wanting to invite his sons for a leisurely gathering.
    • 敬爱寺僧圣刚 - A monk named Shenggang at the Jing'ai Temple.
    • 常所来往 - Frequent visits.
    • 饮食穷极水陆滋味 - Eating a variety of delicacies from both land and sea.
    • 常必以炭炊 - Meals often cooked with charcoal.
    • 往往不惬其意 - Frequently not meeting their expectations.
    • 骄逸成性 - A habit of arrogance and indulgence.
    • 召之可乎 - Asking if inviting them is advisable.
    • 象白猩唇 - Rare and exotic delicacies.
    • 精洁修办小筵 - Preparing a refined and clean small banquet.
    • 广求珍异 - Searching for rare and valuable items.
    • 俾妻孥亲为调鼎 - Instructing the wife and children to personally prepare the dishes.
    • 备陈绮席雕盘 - Setting up beautifully decorated tables and plates.
    • 弟兄列坐矜持俨氷玉 - Brothers sitting formally, as if made of ice and jade.
    • 肴羞每至曾不下筯 - Despite the abundance of food, they rarely touched their chopsticks.
    • 主人揖之再三 - The host repeatedly invited them to eat.
    • 惟霑果实而已 - They only ate fruits.
    • 水餐俱致一匙于口 - They took a small spoonful of each dish.
    • 相盻良久 - They looked at each other for a long time.
    • 咸飱荼食蘖 - They felt as if eating bitter herbs and bitter roots.
    • 莫究其由 - Not understanding the reason.
    • 失餁为谢 - Apologizing for the unsuccessful meal.
    • 明日 - The next day.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 干符中 - Specific period in the Tang Dynasty, known for its cultural and political developments.
    • 洛中 - Refers to Luoyang, a significant city in Chinese history, especially during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 豪贵子弟 - Wealthy and influential young men, often associated with the elite class.
    • 勳荫 - Privileges and honors inherited through family connections, a common practice in imperial China.
    • 锦衣玉食 - Symbolizes a life of luxury and wealth.
    • 水陆滋味 - Refers to a wide variety of delicacies from both land and sea, indicating a rich and diverse diet.
    • 敬爱寺 - A specific temple in Luoyang, indicating a place of religious and social importance.
    • 象白猩唇 - Rare and exotic delicacies, often associated with the elite and wealthy.
    • 调鼎 - Refers to cooking, especially in the context of preparing a refined and elaborate meal.
    • 绮席雕盘 - Beautifully decorated tables and plates, indicating a high level of refinement and luxury.
    • 飱荼食蘖 - A metaphor for feeling as if eating bitter herbs and bitter roots, indicating dissatisfaction or disappointment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 干符 - The Danyu period during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洛中 - Luoyang, a significant city in Chinese history.
    • 李史君 - Li, a former official.
    • 敬爱寺 - Jing'ai Temple, a specific temple in Luoyang.
    • 圣刚 - Shenggang, a monk at Jing'ai Temple.
    • 象白猩唇 - Rare and exotic delicacies.
    • 明日 - The next day.

    During the Danyu period in Luoyang, there were wealthy and influential young men who inherited privileges and honors due to their family connections. They lived lives of luxury, with abundant material possessions and fine clothes and delicious food, not being cautious about overindulgence. They drank fine and rare beverages and satisfied all their desires related to food and drink. There was a former official named Li who returned from his term as an administrator and also resided in Eastern Luoyang. Deeply grateful for past favors, he wanted to invite his sons for a leisurely gathering. He frequently visited a monk named Shenggang at the Jing'ai Temple. Li consulted the monk about the behavior of the young men, saying that they often ate a variety of delicacies from both land and sea and cooked meals with charcoal, but frequently did not meet their expectations. This was a habit of arrogance and indulgence. Li asked if inviting them was advisable. The monk replied that rare and exotic delicacies might be difficult to obtain, but preparing a refined and clean small banquet was not a difficult task. Li then searched for rare and valuable items and instructed his wife and children to personally prepare the dishes. He set up beautifully decorated tables and plates and invited his brothers for a meal. The brothers sat formally, as if made of ice and jade, but despite the abundance of food, they rarely touched their chopsticks. The host repeatedly invited them to eat, but they only ate fruits. When it came to the water dishes, they took a small spoonful of each, but looked at each other for a long time, feeling as if eating bitter herbs and bitter roots. Li did not understand the reason and apologized for the unsuccessful meal. The next day, he prepared another banquet. [56]

    复覩圣刚备述诸子情貌僧曰某前所説岂谬哉而因造其门以问之曰李史君特备一筵庖膳间可为丰防何不略领其意诸子曰燔炙煎和未得其法僧曰他物纵不可食炭炊之饭又嫌何事复曰上人未知凡以炭炊飰先烧令熟谓之炼火方可入爨不然犹有烟气李史君宅炭不经炼是以难于飡防僧抚掌大笑曰此非贫道所知也及大冦先陷瀍洛财产剽掠俱尽昆仲数人与圣刚同时窜避潜伏山草不食者三日贼锋稍逺徒走将往河桥道中小店始开以脱粟为餐而卖僧囊中有钱数文买于土杯同食腹枵既甚粱肉之美不如僧笑而谓曰此非炼炭所炊不知可与诸郎君吃否但低首慙腼无复词对 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 岂谬哉 - Rhetorical question expressing doubt or skepticism; could be mistaken?
    • 造其门以问之 - Visiting someone's residence to inquire about something.
    • 燔炙煎和未得其法 - Culinary techniques not yet mastered; roasting, grilling, and cooking methods have not been perfected.
    • 他物纵不可食炭炊之饭又嫌何事 - Rhetorical question suggesting that if other foods cooked with charcoal are acceptable, then the rice should also be acceptable.
    • 脱粟为餐而卖 - Simple meal made from coarse millet that is being sold.
    • 囊中有钱数文 - Small amount of money; has a few coins in his bag.
    • 买于土杯同食 - Bought a meal in a clay cup and ate together.
    • 腹枵既甚 - Hunger is very severe.
    • 粱肉之美不如 - Quality of fine food is not as desirable as something else.
    • 低首慙腼无复词对 - Lowered his head in shame and had no words to respond.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 圣刚 - Respected figure, likely a monk or scholar.
    • 李史君 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 瀍洛 - Ancient cities of Cai and Luo.
    • 昆仲数人 - Several brothers or siblings.
    • 大冦先陷瀍洛 - Significant invasion or attack on the cities of Cai and Luo.
    • 河桥 - Bridge over the Yellow River.
    • 土杯 - Clay cup, common vessel for drinking or eating.
    • 腹枵 - Empty stomach.
    • 粱肉 - Fine food, often associated with wealth and luxury.

    Proper Nouns

    • 圣刚 - Respected figure, likely a monk or scholar.
    • 李史君 - Historical figure, likely a high-ranking official.
    • 瀍洛 - Ancient cities of Cai and Luo.
    • 昆仲数人 - Several brothers or siblings.
    • 河桥 - Bridge over the Yellow River.
    • 土杯 - Clay cup.
    • 腹枵 - Empty stomach.
    • 粱肉 - Fine food.

    Revisiting the detailed description of the disciples' demeanor by Saint Gang, the monk said, "Could my previous statement be mistaken?" He then went to visit Saint Gang's residence to ask him, "Master Li specially prepared a feast; the cooking methods were not perfect. Why did you not accept the invitation?" The disciples replied, "The roasting, grilling, and cooking methods have not been perfected." The monk said, "Other things, even if they cannot be eaten, are cooked with charcoal; what is there to complain about the rice?" They said, "Master, you do not know that when cooking rice with charcoal, it must first be burned until it is fully cooked, which is called 'refined fire.' Only then can it be placed in the stove. Otherwise, there will still be smoke." The disciples explained that in Master Li's household, the charcoal was not properly pre-cooked, which made the food difficult to eat. The monk clapped his hands and laughed, "This is not something I know." Later, during a great invasion, the cities of Cai and Luo were captured, and their properties were looted. Several brothers and Saint Gang fled together, hiding in the mountains for three days without food. When the invaders moved further away, they continued their journey to the river bridge. Along the way, a small shop opened, selling coarse millet as food. The monk had a few coins in his bag and bought a meal in a clay cup, eating it with the group. Despite their severe hunger, they found the fine food less appealing than the coarse millet. The monk jokingly said, "This is not cooked with refined charcoal; do you think the gentlemen can eat it?" The disciples lowered their heads in shame and had no words to respond. [57]

    古人云膏粱之性难正其此之谓乎是以圣人量腹而食贤者戒于奢逸宋武帝幸武帐堂将往勑诸子弟勿食至防所赐日旰而食不至咸有饥色帝谓曰尔曹少长骄贵不见百姓艰难今使尔等识有饥苦知以节俭期物前圣用心同防哉凤翔府举兵讨贼
    　　巢冦攻防宫阙近京藩镇悉无兵备【初王仙芝败衂黄防引余党南走交广朝以髙骈令公统帅诸道兵师于江湖屯据要路议者以为髙令公奕世名将可以坐制凶渠及贼徒拥众北来浮舟遂逼淮甸于天长县广布营寨驻泊髙令公既不出军但闭关自保而已贼锋因此弥鋭长驱遂渉淮北时齐相公领青州覩蜂蚁强众亦不敢进卷斾遄征或于中路迟留藩屏既无捍御庙堂复失机谋盗遍九衢乃未知觉岂不异哉】时李相镇蒲津郑相国镇岐下既闻车马播迁俱有勤王之念郑相国率賔僚将校共廵城垒雉堞池隍悉 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 古人云 - Common phrase used to introduce a proverb or saying.
    • 膏粱之性难正 - Referring to the difficulty in reforming the habits of the privileged.
    • 是以圣人量腹而食 - Emphasizing moderation.
    • 贤者戒于奢逸 - Highlighting the importance of self-discipline.
    • 赐日旰而食 - Indicating a delay in providing meals.
    • 不至咸有饥色 - Describing the physical appearance of hunger.
    • 尔曹少长骄贵 - Addressing the audience with a critique of their upbringing.
    • 不见百姓艰难 - Emphasizing the disconnect between the elite and the commoners.
    • 今使尔等识有饥苦 - A call to empathy.
    • 知以节俭期物 - Promoting a culture of thrift.
    • 前圣用心同防哉 - Invoking historical wisdom.
    • 卷斾遄征 - Describing a rapid withdrawal.
    • 或于中路迟留 - Indicating a lack of coordination.
    • 藩屏既无捍御 - Highlighting the lack of military support.
    • 庙堂复失机谋 - Critiquing the central government.
    • 盗遍九衢乃未知觉 - Describing the chaos.
    • 岂不异哉 - Expressing surprise or criticism.
    • 雉堞池隍悉 - Indicating a thorough inspection.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋武帝 - Emperor Song Wu, a ruler of the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 武帐堂 - The Wu Zhan Hall, a palace hall.
    • 王仙芝 - A rebel leader during the late Tang dynasty.
    • 黄防 - A general who led the remnants of the rebel forces.
    • 交广 - Refers to the regions of Jiaozhou and Guangzhou, located in southern China.
    • 髙骈 - A general and politician during the Tang dynasty.
    • 江湖 - Refers to the Yangtze and Huai rivers.
    • 前圣 - Refers to the sages of the past.
    • 凤翔府 - A commandery in the Tang dynasty.
    • 巢冦 - The rebel leader Huang Chao.
    • 防宫阙 - The palace gates.
    • 近京藩镇 - Feudal lords near the capital.
    • 奕世名将 - Famous generals of many generations.
    • 贼徒 - Rebels or bandits.
    • 淮甸 - The Huai region.
    • 天长县 - A county in the Tang dynasty.
    • 齐相公 - The Prime Minister of Qi.
    • 青州 - A region in the Tang dynasty.
    • 蜂蚁强众 - A simile comparing the rebels to bees and ants.
    • 卷斾遄征 - A metaphor for a rapid withdrawal.
    • 藩屏 - Feudal lords or local governors.
    • 庙堂 - The court or central government.
    • 九衢 - The nine avenues, a metaphor for the city.
    • 李相 - The Chancellor of Li.
    • 郑相国 - Duke Zheng, a political figure.
    • 蒲津 - A commandery in the Tang dynasty.
    • 岐下 - Qishan, a region in the Tang dynasty.
    • 车马播迁 - The relocation of the imperial court.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '古人云膏粱之性难正其此之谓乎' is elliptical and can be interpreted as 'As the ancients say, the nature of those raised on rich food is hard to correct, is this what they mean?'
