    • The sentence '是以圣人量腹而食贤者戒于奢逸' can be read as 'Therefore, the wise measure their food according to their capacity, and the virtuous guard against luxury and ease.'
    • The sentence '宋武帝幸武帐堂将往勑诸子弟勿食' can be interpreted as 'Emperor Song Wu visited the Wu Zhan Hall and ordered his sons and grandsons not to eat.'
    • The sentence '赐日旰而食不至咸有饥色' can be read as 'He granted them food late in the day, and they all had hungry looks.'
    • The sentence '尔曹少长骄贵不见百姓艰难' can be interpreted as 'You young and old are proud and wealthy, and you do not see the hardships of the people.'
    • The sentence '今使尔等识有饥苦知以节俭期物' can be read as 'Now I make you understand the suffering of hunger and know how to expect others to be frugal.'
    • The sentence '前圣用心同防哉' can be interpreted as 'The sages of old had the same concern.'
    • The sentence '凤翔府举兵讨贼' can be read as 'The Fengxiang Commandery raised troops to fight the rebels.'
    • The sentence '巢冦攻防宫阙近京藩镇悉无兵备' can be interpreted as 'The rebel Nest attacked the palace gates, and the feudal lords near the capital had no military preparations.'

    As the ancients say, the nature of those raised on rich food is hard to correct; is this what they mean? Therefore, the wise measure their food according to their capacity, and the virtuous guard against luxury and ease. Emperor Song Wu visited the Wu Zhan Hall and ordered his sons and grandsons not to eat. Upon reaching the guard post, he granted them food late in the day, and they all had hungry looks. The emperor said to them, "You young and old are proud and wealthy, and you do not see the hardships of the people. Now I make you understand the suffering of hunger and know how to expect others to be frugal." The sages of old had the same concern. The Fengxiang Commandery raised troops to fight the rebels. The rebel Nest attacked the palace gates, and the feudal lords near the capital had no military preparations. [Initially, Wang Xianzhi suffered a defeat, and Huang Fang led the remnants south to Jiaoguang. The court appointed Gao Pian to lead the armies of various circuits to occupy strategic points along the Yangtze and Huai rivers. Some argued that Gao Pian was a famous general of many generations and could control the rebels from his seat. When the rebels gathered and came north, they sailed and approached the Huai region, setting up camps widely in Tianchang County. Gao Pian did not send out troops, only closing the gates and protecting himself. The rebels' momentum became even sharper, advancing and crossing the Huai River northward. At that time, the Prime Minister of Qi governed Qingzhou, seeing the strong and numerous rebels like bees and ants, and also did not dare to advance, quickly withdrawing his banners and retreating. Some lingered on the way. The feudal lords had no defense, and the court again lost its strategy. Bandits roamed the nine avenues without being noticed, how strange is this?] At that time, the Chancellor of Li governed Puzhen, and Duke Zheng governed Qishan. After hearing about the relocation of the imperial court, both had the thought of serving the king. Duke Zheng led his officials, officers, and soldiers to patrol the city walls, inspecting the city walls and moats thoroughly. [58]

    皆毁塞计其修筑之功万旅月余未竟而贼锋方盛立虞奔轶明晨复召从事大将坐于内防询以谋计咸以巨盗方炽未可枝梧众议且欲从权俟兵集乃图复相国曰诸君劝某臣贼乎于是歘然而倒左右扶之不及为地甃所伤面首皆破洎日午达于明旦口喑尚未能语是时关辅征镇咸已归欵唯凤翔信耗不通贼议兴师致讨有奔来者具述其事于是监军与僚佐代为表章使两骑驰至京国贼徒览之大喜遣王懐顺将百余人厚赍防缯金玉以申慰劳之意既而开筵以待懐顺宴席施设侔于曩日列坐行觞将陈饮乐工才合 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 皆毁塞计其修筑之功万旅月余未竟 - Indicates that all efforts to destroy and block the construction were put in place, and the calculation of the labor and time required was that it would take more than a month for ten thousand troops to complete the work.
    • 贼锋方盛 - Means that the enemy forces are at their peak strength.
    • 立虞奔轶 - Suggests a sudden and unexpected attack or invasion.
    • 复召从事大将坐于内防 - Indicates that the commander-in-chief was called back and seated inside the defensive position.
    • 咸以巨盗方炽未可枝梧 - Means that everyone agreed that the large-scale bandits were at their height and it was not advisable to engage them directly.
    • 众议且欲从权俟兵集乃图复 - Suggests that the majority wanted to adopt a temporary measure and wait for the troops to gather before planning to retake the lost territory.
    • 相国曰诸君劝某臣贼乎 - The chancellor asks if the generals are suggesting that he should submit to the enemy.
    • 歘然而倒 - Means that he suddenly fell down.
    • 左右扶之不及为地甃所伤面首皆破 - Indicates that those around him could not catch him before he was injured by the ground's uneven surface, causing his face and head to be bruised.
    • 洎日午达于明旦口喑尚未能语 - Means that by noon the next day, he still could not speak.
    • 关辅征镇咸已归欵 - Indicates that all the military commanders in the Guan area have surrendered.
    • 唯凤翔信耗不通 - Suggests that only Fengxiang had no news.
    • 贼议兴师致讨 - Means that the enemy was discussing raising an army to attack.
    • 有奔来者具述其事 - Indicates that someone who fled brought the news.
    • 于是监军与僚佐代为表章 - Means that the military governor and his subordinates wrote a memorial.
    • 使两骑驰至京国 - Indicates that two horsemen were sent to the capital.
    • 贼徒览之大喜 - Means that the enemy was very pleased after reading the memorial.
    • 遣王懐顺将百余人厚赍防缯金玉以申慰劳之意 - Indicates that Wang Huishun was sent with a large number of people and valuable gifts to express gratitude and comfort.
    • 既而开筵以待懐顺 - Means that they prepared a banquet to welcome Wang Huishun.
    • 宴席施设侔于曩日 - Indicates that the banquet setup was as grand as before.
    • 列坐行觞将陈饮乐工才合 - Means that everyone sat down, and the wine was served; the musicians were about to start playing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 相国 - Refers to the chancellor, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 从事大将 - Refers to the commander-in-chief or a high-ranking military officer.
    • 巨盗 - Refers to large-scale bandits or rebels.
    • 关辅 - Refers to the region around the Guanzhong area, which was an important strategic location.
    • 凤翔 - Refers to Fengxiang, a city in Shaanxi province.
    • 京国 - Refers to the capital city.
    • 贼徒 - Refers to the enemy forces or rebels.
    • 表章 - Refers to a memorial or a formal document submitted to the emperor.

    Proper Nouns

    • 相国 - The chancellor.
    • 从事大将 - The commander-in-chief.
    • 巨盗 - Large-scale bandits or rebels.
    • 关辅 - The Guan area.
    • 凤翔 - Fengxiang.
    • 京国 - The capital city.
    • 贼徒 - The enemy forces or rebels.
    • 表章 - A memorial or formal document.

    All efforts to destroy and block the construction were put in place, and the calculation of the labor and time required was that it would take more than a month for ten thousand troops to complete the work. However, the enemy forces were at their peak strength, and a sudden and unexpected attack was imminent. The next morning, the commander-in-chief was called back and seated inside the defensive position. He was consulted for a plan, and everyone agreed that the large-scale bandits were at their height and it was not advisable to engage them directly. The majority wanted to adopt a temporary measure and wait for the troops to gather before planning to retake the lost territory. The chancellor asked if the generals were suggesting that he should submit to the enemy. Suddenly, he fell down. Those around him could not catch him before he was injured by the ground's uneven surface, causing his face and head to be bruised. By noon the next day, he still could not speak. At that time, all the military commanders in the Guan area had surrendered, except for Fengxiang, which had no news. The enemy was discussing raising an army to attack, and someone who fled brought the news. The military governor and his subordinates wrote a memorial and sent two horsemen to the capital. After reading the memorial, the enemy was very pleased and sent Wang Huishun with a large number of people and valuable gifts to express gratitude and comfort. They prepared a banquet to welcome Wang Huishun, and the banquet setup was as grand as before. Everyone sat down, and the wine was served; the musicians were about to start playing. [59]

    管文武军吏及声妓一时恸哭监军从事雪涕止之良久方定懐顺与来者皆骇愕相眄就食乃问其由时吏部孙侍郎亦在幕中对曰相国自镇此方恩及万物听政之暇时命音乐与将吏交欢遽及风疹所侵今辰不赴兹防众闻丝竹声不觉悲泣耳是日合城老幼咸共凄伤相国闻之曰我知亿兆人民之心未厌唐德贼势虽甚窃据宫阙灭亡当在旦夕于是密飞羽檄告于隣道防兵旬朔间邠泾洋陇及沿边藩镇俱以鋭师来集既而神策守镇军士闻风亦至麾下一旦贼中遣千余人大索粮糗于时烽已严侦逻殊密云旗霜刃森 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 文武军吏及声妓一时恸哭 - Military and civil officials, as well as musicians and entertainers, all cried out in grief at the same time.
    • 监军从事雪涕止之 - The official overseeing the military wiped away his tears to stop the crying.
    • 良久方定 - After a long time, they finally calmed down.
    • 怀顺与来者皆骇愕相眄 - Huai Shun and those who came were all shocked and looked at each other in surprise.
    • 就食乃问其由 - They then asked the reason.
    • 相国自镇此方恩及万物 - The chancellor, who had been governing this region, had extended his benevolence to all things.
    • 听政之暇时命音乐与将吏交欢 - In his spare time from governing, he occasionally ordered music to enjoy the company of the officials.
    • 遽及风疹所侵今辰不赴兹防 - Suddenly, due to illness, he did not attend this gathering today.
    • 众闻丝竹声不觉悲泣耳 - The people, hearing the music, couldn't help but cry.
    • 合城老幼咸共凄伤 - The entire city's elderly and young were all filled with sorrow.
    • 我知亿兆人民之心未厌唐德 - I know that the hearts of the people have not yet tired of the virtue of the Tang dynasty.
    • 贼势虽甚窃据宫阙 - Although the rebels are powerful and have seized the palace.
    • 灭亡当在旦夕 - Their downfall will come soon.
    • 密飞羽檄告于邻道 - Secretly sending out urgent messages to inform the neighboring regions.
    • 防兵旬朔间邠泾洋陇及沿边藩镇俱以锐师来集 - Defensive troops gathered with elite forces from various regions and border garrisons within a few days.
    • 既而神策守镇军士闻风亦至 - Shortly after, the imperial guards also arrived upon hearing the news.
    • 麾下一旦贼中遣千余人 - Under his command, the rebels sent over a thousand people.
    • 大索粮糗 - To search thoroughly for food supplies.
    • 烽堠已严侦逻殊密 - The watchtowers were already on high alert, and the patrols were especially strict.
    • 云旗霜刃森 - Flags and sharp weapons in an orderly and imposing manner.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 相国 - Refers to a high-ranking official, possibly a chancellor or prime minister.
    • 唐德 - Refers to the virtue and legitimacy of the Tang dynasty.
    • 贼 - Refers to rebels or insurgents who have taken control of the palace.
    • 神策军 - Refers to the imperial guards, a powerful military force in the Tang dynasty.
    • 烽堠 - Refers to watchtowers used for signaling and defense.
    • 侦逻 - Refers to patrols and surveillance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 管 - The name of a person, possibly a high-ranking official.
    • 怀顺 - The name of a person, possibly a high-ranking official or noble.
    • 唐 - Refers to the Tang dynasty.
    • 贼 - Refers to rebels or insurgents.
    • 神策军 - Refers to the imperial guards.
    • 邠泾洋陇 - Refers to various regions in the Tang dynasty.
    • 沿边藩镇 - Refers to border garrisons.

    When the military and civil officials, as well as the musicians and entertainers, all cried out in grief at the same time, the official overseeing the military wiped away his tears to stop them. After a long time, they finally calmed down. Huai Shun and those who came were all shocked and looked at each other in surprise. They then asked the reason. The chancellor, who had been governing this region, had extended his benevolence to all things. In his spare time from governing, he occasionally ordered music to enjoy the company of the officials. Suddenly, due to illness, he did not attend this gathering today. The people, hearing the music, couldn't help but cry. The entire city's elderly and young were all filled with sorrow. The chancellor said, "I know that the hearts of the people have not yet tired of the virtue of the Tang dynasty. Although the rebels are powerful and have seized the palace, their downfall will come soon. I have secretly sent out urgent messages to inform the neighboring regions. Within a few days, defensive troops from various regions and border garrisons gathered with elite forces. Shortly after, the imperial guards also arrived upon hearing the news. Under my command, one day, the rebels sent over a thousand people to thoroughly search for food supplies. At that time, the watchtowers were already on high alert, and the patrols were especially strict. The flags and sharp weapons stood in an orderly and imposing manner." [60]

    罗于百里之内贼入界大惊俱就擒戮有后殿者奔以状告凶党无不夺魄陷京黔庶亦思奋勇【初有走还京中者云凤翔郑相公已叛黄王兵士甚众巢闻之殊不介意言我行却半天下所至无不克直至髙令公犹不敢出军敌我郑相国拟作何计唯赵张王潘闻之已为奔轶之备也】俄而蒲关晋绛并汾泽潞及河北三镇并举雄师翕然响附相国仗节训兵援旗誓众摧锋督战累剉凶渠首运奇谋终摧巨孽其后请朝庸蜀复秉化权匡国济时终始一致文经武纬何谢古人诗云维岳降神生甫及申维申及甫维周之翰相国有焉 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 罗于百里之内 - Enemy spread over a wide area; within a hundred miles.
    • 贼入界大惊 - Enemy entered the boundary; caused great alarm.
    • 俱就擒戮 - All were captured and executed.
    • 有后殿者奔以状告 - Those at the rear ran to report the situation.
    • 凶党无不夺魄 - Enemy was terrified.
    • 陷京黔庶亦思奋勇 - Capital was captured; common people thought to fight bravely.
    • 初有走还京中者云 - Initially, someone ran back to the capital and reported.
    • 巢闻之殊不介意 - Leader heard of the rebellion and was not concerned.
    • 言我行却半天下所至无不克 - Leader said his actions had already occupied half of the world; wherever he went, he conquered without fail.
    • 直至髙令公犹不敢出军敌我 - Gao still dared not oppose the leader.
    • 郑相国拟作何计 - What plans is Zheng making?
    • 唯赵张王潘闻之已为奔轶之备也 - Only Zhao, Zhang, Wang, and Pan had already made preparations to flee.
    • 俄而蒲关晋绛并汾泽潞及河北三镇并举雄师翕然响附 - Soon after, several regions raised a mighty army and unanimously rallied.
    • 相国仗节训兵援旗誓众摧锋督战累剉凶渠首运奇谋终摧巨孽 - Official held the staff of authority, trained troops, raised the flag, swore the troops, broke through the enemy's sharpness, supervised the battle, repeatedly defeated the enemy's leaders, used ingenious strategies, and finally destroyed the great evil.
    • 其后请朝庸蜀复秉化权匡国济时终始一致文经武纬何谢古人 - Afterward, official's actions were consistent and effective, comparable to ancient heroes.
    • 诗云维岳降神生甫及申维申及甫维周之翰相国有焉 - Poem praises the official as a great figure, among the pillars of Zhou.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贼 - Refers to bandits or enemies, likely in a historical context of conflict or rebellion.
    • 凤翔 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 黄王 - Likely refers to the Yellow Emperor or a leader of a historical period.
    • 巢 - Likely refers to a historical leader or figure, possibly in the context of a rebellion or conflict.
    • 髙令公 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Gao, likely from a historical period.
    • 郑相国 - Refers to a high-ranking official named Zheng, likely from a historical period.
    • 赵张王潘 - Refers to high-ranking officials named Zhao, Zhang, Wang, and Pan, likely from a historical period.
    • 蒲关晋绛并汾泽潞及河北三镇 - Refers to several regions, likely from a historical period.
    • 相国 - Refers to a high-ranking official, likely from a historical period.
    • 诗云维岳降神生甫及申维申及甫维周之翰相国有焉 - Refers to a poem that praises a high-ranking official as a great figure, likely from a historical period.

    Within a hundred miles, when the bandits entered the boundary, everyone was greatly alarmed and all were captured and executed. Those at the rear ran to report the situation, and the enemy was terrified. The capital was captured, and the common people also thought to fight bravely. [Initially, someone ran back to the capital and reported that Zheng, the chancellor of Fengxiang, had rebelled against the Yellow Emperor with a large army. The leader heard of this and was not at all concerned, saying, "My actions have already occupied half of the world, and wherever I go, I conquer without fail. Even Gao, the commander, still dared not send out troops to oppose me. What plans is Zheng making? Only Zhao, Zhang, Wang, and Pan had already made preparations to flee."] Soon after, the regions of Pu, Guan, Jin, Jie, Fen, Ze, and Lu, as well as the three towns of Hebei, raised a mighty army and unanimously rallied. The chancellor held the staff of authority, trained the troops, raised the flag, swore the troops, broke through the enemy's sharpness, supervised the battle, repeatedly defeated the enemy's leaders, used ingenious strategies, and finally destroyed the great evil. Later, upon the request of the court of Yongshu, the chancellor again held the power of transformation, reformed the state, and helped the times, consistent from beginning to end, with literary and martial strategies, how could they be inferior to ancient people? The poem says, "The mountains produce divine beings, giving birth to Fu and Shen. Fu and Shen were the pillars of Zhou, and the chancellor was among them." [61]

    老君庙画
    　　东都北邙山有元观南有老君庙台殿髙敞下瞰伊洛神仙泥塑之皆开元中杨惠之所制奇巧精严见者増敬壁有吴道元画五圣真容及老子庙胡经事丹青絶妙古今无比【杜工部题诗云配极元都閟慿髙禁籞长守祧严具礼掌节镇非常碧瓦初寒外金茎一气旁山河扶绣户日月近雕梁仙李蟠根大猗兰奕叶光世家遗旧史道德付今王画手看前軰吴生逺擅塲森罗移地轴妙絶动宫墙五圣聨龙衮千官列鴈行冕旒俱秀发旌斾尽飞杨翠柏深留景红梨逈得霜风筝吹玉柱露井冻银牀身退卑周室经传拱汉皇谷神如不死 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 東都 - Refers to Luoyang, the eastern capital of China during certain dynasties, particularly the Northern Wei and Tang periods.
    • 北邙山 - A mountain range north of Luoyang, historically associated with burial grounds and temples.
    • 玄元观 - The name of a Taoist temple, likely dedicated to Laozi (the founder of Daoism), who is also referred to as 'Xuan Yuan'.
    • 老君庙 - A temple dedicated to Laozi, often called the 'Old Master' or 'Laojun'.
    • 台殿高敞 - Describes the temple's architecture as grand and spacious.
    • 下瞰伊洛 - Means 'overlooking the Yi and Luo rivers,' which flow near Luoyang.
    • 神仙泥塑之像 - Refers to statues of deities or immortals made from clay, a common practice in Chinese temple architecture.
    • 开元中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (713–756 CE), a period of cultural and artistic flourishing.
    • 杨惠之所制 - Indicates that the statues were created by Yang Huizhi, a famous sculptor of the Tang dynasty.
    • 奇巧精严 - Describes the statues as ingenious, exquisite, and meticulously crafted.
    • 见者增敬 - Means 'those who see them feel increased reverence.'
    • 壁有吴道元画五圣真容 - Refers to a wall painting by Wu Daozi, a renowned painter of the Tang dynasty, depicting the 'Five Saints' (likely Buddhist or Taoist deities).
    • 及老子庙胡经事 - Refers to the depiction of Laozi's temple and its rituals, possibly including foreign elements (胡, referring to non-Han Chinese or foreign influences).
    • 丹青绝妙古今无比 - Describes the paintings as exceptionally beautiful, surpassing all others in history.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北邙山 - Historically significant as a burial ground for emperors and nobles, it is also a site of religious and cultural importance.
    • 玄元观 - A Taoist temple, reflecting the prominence of Taoism during the Tang dynasty.
    • 老君庙 - Dedicated to Laozi, the central figure of Taoism, whose teachings are foundational to Chinese philosophy and religion.
    • 开元中 - A golden age of Chinese culture, art, and politics under Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 杨惠之 - A celebrated sculptor known for his lifelike and expressive statues, particularly in religious contexts.
    • 吴道子 - A legendary painter whose works are celebrated for their dynamic and expressive style, often associated with Buddhist and Taoist themes.
    • 五圣 - Likely refers to five deities or sages, possibly Buddhist or Taoist, whose true identities may require further research.
    • 老子庙胡经事 - Suggests the inclusion of foreign or non-Han Chinese elements in the temple's rituals or architecture, reflecting the cosmopolitan nature of Tang China.
    • 丹青 - Refers to traditional Chinese painting, emphasizing the use of ink and color.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东都 - Luoyang.
    • 北邙山 - Mount Beimang.
    • 玄元观 - Xuan Yuan Temple.
    • 老君庙 - Laojun Temple.
    • 开元中 - The Kaiyuan era (713–756 CE).
    • 杨惠之 - Yang Huizhi.
    • 吴道子 - Wu Daozi.
    • 五圣 - The Five Saints (identity requires further research).
    • 老子庙 - Laozi Temple.
    • 胡经事 - Foreign or non-Han Chinese rituals or elements.

    Laojun Temple Paintings
    In the eastern capital, on the north side of Mount Beimang, there is the Xuan Yuan Temple, and to the south of it stands the Laojun Temple. The temple's halls and pavilions are grand and spacious, overlooking the Yi and Luo rivers. The clay statues of immortals and deities, all crafted during the Kaiyuan era by Yang Huizhi, are ingeniously designed, exquisitely detailed, and meticulously made, evoking reverence in those who behold them. On the walls, Wu Daozi painted the true forms of the Five Saints, as well as scenes from Laozi's temple and its rituals, which are so beautifully rendered that they surpass all other works in history. [Du Fu, in his poem, wrote: "At the pinnacle of the Yuan capital, the high enclosure is long and secluded. The strict rituals are maintained, and the sacred objects are guarded. The blue tiles shine beyond the early cold, and the jade pillars stand firm amidst the single breath of the sky. Mountains and rivers support the embroidered doors, and the sun and moon approach the carved beams. The immortal Li has deep roots, and the fragrant orchid shines brightly across generations. The old family has left behind its history, and the moral teachings are entrusted to the current king. The painter's hand looks back at the predecessors; Wu Daozi's skill far surpasses the field. The dense arrangement shifts the earth's axis, and the exquisite beauty stirs the palace walls. The Five Saints are arrayed in dragon robes, and a thousand officials line up like geese. The crowns and capes all shine brilliantly, and the flags and banners flutter freely. The cypress pines deeply retain their shadows, and the red pears stand out against the frost. The kites blow the jade pillars, and the well freezes the silver bed. The body retreats in humility before the Zhou dynasty, and the scriptures and traditions honor the Han emperor. The spirit of the valley, if it does not die,..." [62]

    养拙更何郷】敬爱寺复有雉尾病龙莫知画者谁氏绘事奇巧皆入神之迹【雉尾在东庑观音院天王部从中绯衣神人抱野雉一只逼而观之势飞动】政平坊安国观明皇朝玉眞公主所建门楼髙九十尺而柱端无栱枓殿南有精思院琢玉为天尊老君之像叶法善罗公逺张果先生并图之于壁院南池沼引御渠水注之垒石像蓬莱方丈瀛洲三山女冠多上阳退宫嫔御其东与国学相接咸通中有书生云每清风朗月即闻山池之内歩虚笙磬之音卢尚书有诗云夕照纱牕起暗尘青松绕殿不知春君看白首诵经者半是宫中歌舞人 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 養拙更何郷 - A classical expression meaning 'to cultivate one's clumsiness, where else?' It suggests a humble or reclusive lifestyle.
    • 雉尾病龙 - A metaphorical or artistic description, possibly referring to a painting or sculpture, but the exact meaning is unclear.
    • 莫知画者谁氏 - Means 'no one knows who the painter is,' indicating uncertainty about the artist.
    • 绘事奇巧皆入神之迹 - Means 'the art is so ingenious and lifelike that it seems almost divine,' praising the skill of the artist.
    • 雉尾在东庑观音院天王部从中绯衣神人抱野雉一只逼而观之势飞动 - Describes a specific scene in a painting or sculpture, with a focus on the lifelike quality of the depiction.
    • 门楼高九十尺而柱端无栱枓 - Describes the architectural features of the gatehouse, noting its height and the absence of brackets.
    • 琢玉为天尊老君之像 - Means 'carved jade into the likeness of the Heavenly Master Laozi,' referring to a statue.
    • 叶法善罗公逺张果先生并图之于壁 - Means 'Ye Fashan, Luo Gongyuan, and Zhang Guo painted them on the wall,' indicating the artists involved.
    • 院南池沼引御渠水注之 - Means 'to the south of the institute is a pond that draws water from the imperial canal,' describing the water source.
    • 垒石像蓬莱方丈瀛洲三山 - Means 'stones are piled up to resemble the Three Mountains of Penglai, Fangzhang, and Yingzhou,' referring to mythical islands.
    • 女冠多上阳退宫嫔御 - Means 'many of the nuns were former palace maids from the Shangyang Palace,' indicating the background of the nuns.
    • 其东与国学相接 - Means 'to the east, it adjoins the National Academy,' indicating the location.
    • 咸通中有书生云 - Means 'during the Xiantong period, a scholar said,' indicating the time and speaker.
    • 每清风朗月即闻山池之内歩虚笙磬之音 - Means 'every time there is a clear wind and bright moon, one can hear the sounds of the mountain and the pond, as if they are playing music,' describing a serene atmosphere.
    • 卢尚书有诗云 - Means 'the Minister Lu wrote a poem saying,' indicating the source of the poem.
    • 夕照纱牕起暗尘 - Means 'the evening sun through the silk window stirs up the dust,' describing a scene.
    • 青松绕殿不知春 - Means 'the green pines around the hall do not know of spring,' describing a scene.
    • 君看白首诵经者 - Means 'look at the old men reciting scriptures,' describing a scene.
    • 半是宫中歌舞人 - Means 'half of them were once palace entertainers,' indicating the background of the people.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 敬爱寺 - A temple, possibly a significant religious site.
    • 明皇朝玉眞公主 - Princess Yuzhen of the Ming Emperor's reign, a historical figure.
    • 政平坊安国观 - A specific place, possibly a temple or shrine.
    • 叶法善罗公逺张果先生 - Historical figures, possibly artists or scholars.
    • 咸通 - A period in Chinese history, specifically the Xiantong era.
    • 上阳退宫嫔御 - Former palace maids from the Shangyang Palace, indicating a historical context.
    • 国学 - The National Academy, a significant educational institution.

    Proper Nouns

    • 敬爱寺 - The name of a temple.
    • 明皇朝玉眞公主 - Princess Yuzhen of the Ming Emperor's reign.
    • 政平坊安国观 - A specific place.
    • 叶法善罗公逺张果先生 - Historical figures.
    • 咸通 - A period in Chinese history.
    • 上阳退宫嫔御 - Former palace maids from the Shangyang Palace.
    • 国学 - The National Academy.

    To cultivate one's clumsiness, where else? At the Jing Ai Temple, there is a painting or sculpture of a "chicken-tail sick dragon," and no one knows who the painter is. The art is so ingenious and lifelike that it seems almost divine. The "chicken-tail" is located in the eastern wing of the Guanyin Hall in the Heavenly King section, where a red-clothed divine person holds a wild chicken, and the scene is so lifelike that it seems to be flying.

    In the Zheng Ping Ward, the An Guo Temple was built by Princess Yuzhen of the Ming Emperor's reign. The gatehouse is ninety feet high, and the top of the columns has no brackets. To the south of the hall is the Jing Si Institute, where jade was carved into the likeness of the Heavenly Master Laozi. Ye Fashan, Luo Gongyuan, and Zhang Guo painted them on the wall. To the south of the institute is a pond that draws water from the imperial canal. Stones are piled up to resemble the Three Mountains of Penglai, Fangzhang, and Yingzhou. Many of the nuns were former palace maids from the Shangyang Palace. To the east, it adjoins the National Academy.

    During the Xiantong period, a scholar said, "Every time there is a clear wind and bright moon, one can hear the sounds of the mountain and the pond, as if they are playing music." The Minister Lu wrote a poem saying, "The evening sun through the silk window stirs up the dust. The green pines around the hall do not know of spring. Look at the old men reciting scriptures, half of them were once palace entertainers." [63]

    白傅乗舟
    　　白尚书为少傅分务洛师情兴高逸每有云泉胜境靡不追游常以诗酒为娱因着醉吟先生传以叙卢尚书简辞有别墅近枕伊水亭榭清峻方冬与羣从子侄同游倚栏眺翫嵩洛俄而霰雪微下情兴益髙因话廉察金陵常记江南烟水每见居人以叶舟浮泛就食菰米鲈鱼近来思之如在心目良久忽见二人衣簑笠循岸而来牵引水乡蓬艇船头覆青幕中有白衣人与衲僧偶坐船后有小灶安桐甑而炊丱角仆烹鱼煑茗泝流过于槛前闻舟中吟啸方甚卢抚掌惊叹莫知谁氏使人从而问之乃曰白傅与僧佛光同自建春门往香山精舍其后每遇亲友无不话之以为高逸之情莫能及矣 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 情兴高逸 - Describes a state of high and unrestrained enthusiasm.
    • 靡不追游 - Means 'no place is left unexplored,' indicating extensive travels.
    • 叶舟浮泛 - Means 'floating in a small boat,' describing a leisurely activity.
    • 就食菰米鲈鱼 - Means 'eating gusi rice and perch,' indicating a simple meal.
    • 泝流过于槛前 - Describes the boat passing by the railing.
    • 闻舟中吟啸方甚 - Describes the sound of singing and laughing from the boat.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 白傅 - A respectful title for Bai Juyi, a famous poet of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 少傅 - A high-ranking official position, equivalent to a vice-premier.
    • 醉吟先生传 - Refers to Bai Juyi's autobiographical work, which describes his life and experiences.
    • 卢尚书简辞 - Refers to Lu Shangshu Jian, a high-ranking official who also had a villa near the Yi River.
    • 建春门 - A gate in the city wall of Luoyang, indicating a specific location.
    • 香山精舍 - A hermitage or retreat in Xiangshan, a famous scenic area in Luoyang.
    • 佛光 - Refers to a monk, likely a friend or companion of Bai Juyi.

    Proper Nouns

    • 白傅 - Bai Juyi, a famous poet and official.
    • 卢尚书简辞 - Lu Shangshu Jian, a high-ranking official.
    • 建春门 - A gate in the city wall of Luoyang.
    • 香山精舍 - A hermitage or retreat in Xiangshan.
    • 佛光 - A monk, likely a friend or companion of Bai Juyi.

    Bai Juyi, the White Emissary, was stationed in Luoyang, where he had a high and unrestrained enthusiasm. Whenever there was a scenic place with clouds and springs, he would not leave it unexplored. He often traveled extensively and enjoyed the beauty of nature. Because of this, he wrote the biography of Mr. Drunken Recitation to narrate his experiences.

    Lu Shangshu Jian, a high-ranking official, had a villa near the Yi River. The pavilions and terraces were elegant and lofty. In winter, he would often go for a walk with his relatives, leaning on the railing and enjoying the view of Mount Song and Luoyang. Suddenly, light snowflakes began to fall, and his enthusiasm grew even higher. He started talking about his past inspection of Jinling, where he remembered the misty waters of southern China. He often saw the locals floating in small boats, eating gusi rice and perch. Recently, he thought about these memories as if they were still vivid in his mind.

    After a while, he suddenly saw two people wearing straw hats and following the riverbank. They were pulling a small boat made of reeds. On the front of the boat, there was a green canopy, and inside, a white-clad person and a monk were sitting together. At the back of the boat, there was a small stove with a bamboo steamer for cooking. A young servant was cooking fish and tea. The boat passed by the railing, and the sound of singing and laughing from the boat was very loud. Lu Shangshu Jian clapped his hands in amazement and did not know who they were. He sent someone to inquire, and they said, "This is Bai Juyi and the monk Faguang, who have come from Jianchun Gate to the Xiangshan Hermitage." After that, every time he met friends and relatives, they would talk about this incident, saying that it was a display of a lofty and unrestrained spirit. [64]

    严史君遇终南山者
    　　大中末建州刺史严士则本穆宗朝为尚医奉御颇好眞道因午日于终南山采药迷误于岩嶂之间不觉遂行数日所赍糗粮既尽四逺复无居人计其道路去京不啻五六百里然而林岫深僻风景明丽忽有茅屋数间出于松竹之下烟萝四合才通小径士则连扣其门良久竟无出者窥其篱隙之内有一人于石榻偃卧看书推户直造其前方乃摄衣而起士则拜罢自陈行止因遣坐于盘石之上亦问京华近事复询天子嗣位几年云自安史犯阙居此迄于今日士则具陈奔驰渉歴资 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大中末 - Refers to the late period of Emperor Xuanzong's reign in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 建州刺史 - Refers to the position of governor of Jianzhou.
    • 尚医奉御 - Refers to a high-ranking official responsible for medical affairs in the imperial court.
    • 眞道 - Refers to Taoism or the pursuit of spiritual enlightenment.
    • 午日 - Refers to the fifth day of the fifth month in the lunar calendar, also known as the Dragon Boat Festival.
    • 岩嶂 - Refers to steep cliffs or mountains.
    • 糗粮 - Refers to dried food supplies.
    • 居人 - Refers to people living in the area.
    • 林岫深僻 - Describes a remote and secluded forested area.
    • 茅屋数间 - Refers to a few thatched huts.
    • 烟萝四合 - Describes a scene where smoke and vines surround the area.
    • 摄衣而起 - Refers to the act of rising up and straightening one's clothes.
    • 天子嗣位 - Refers to the succession of the emperor.
    • 京华 - Refers to the capital city, in this case, Chang'an.
    • 京不啻五六百里 - Means 'more than five or six hundred miles away.'

    The Encounter of Governor Yan with a Recluse in the Zhongnan Mountains

    At the end of Emperor Xuanzong's reign, Yan Shizé, the governor of Jianzhou, was originally a high-ranking official in charge of medical affairs during the reign of Emperor Muzong. He had a keen interest in Taoism and, on the Dragon Boat Festival, ventured into the Zhongnan Mountains to gather herbs. Losing his way among the steep cliffs, he wandered for several days without realizing it. His dried food supplies were exhausted, and there were no people living in the area for miles. Calculating the distance from the capital, he found himself more than five or six hundred miles away. Despite the deep and secluded forest, the scenery was beautiful. Suddenly, he saw a few thatched huts emerging beneath the pines and bamboos, surrounded by smoke and vines, with only a narrow path leading to them. Yan Shizé knocked on the door repeatedly for a long time, but no one came out. Peering through the gaps in the fence, he saw a man lying on a stone bed, reading a book. Opening the door, he approached the man, who then rose and straightened his clothes. After Yan Shizé bowed and explained his situation, he invited him to sit on a flat stone. He asked about recent events in the capital and inquired how many years had passed since the An-Shi Rebellion. The man replied that he had been living there since the time of the rebellion and had not left until now. Yan Shizé then recounted his journey and experiences in detail. [65]

    粮已絶廹于枵腹请以食救之者曰自居山谷且无烟爨有一物可以疗之念君逺来相遗自起于梁栋之间脱囊开啓其中有百余颗如藊豆之状俾于药室取铛拾薪汲泉而煑良久盛有香气视之已如掌大曰可以食矣渇即取铛中余水饮之士则方防其半已极丰饱复曰汝得至此当有宿分自兹三十年间不饥渇俗情虑将淡泊也他时位至方伯当取罗浮相近傥能脱去纷华兼获长生之道辞家日久可以还矣士则将欲告归因述虑失道曰勿忧去此二三里与采薪人相值可
    以随之而至国门不逺既出于山隅果有采薪者在路侧或问者姓名竟无所对才经信宿已及樊川村野既还辇毂不喜更尝滋味日觉气壮神清有骖鸾驭之意衣褐杖藜多止岩岫居守卢仆射耽味黙思覩异人有道流述其事延之致于门下及闻方伯之説因以处士奏官自梓州别驾作牧建溪时年已九十到郡才经周岁觧印乃归罗浮及韦相公宙出镇广南使人访之犹在山谷大中十四年之任建安路由江浙时萧相国观风浙右于桂楼宿宴召之唯饮酒数杯他皆无食也 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 糧已絶廹于枵腹 - The food supply has been exhausted to the point of hunger.
    • 請以食料救之 - Please use food supplies to save them.
    • 自居山谷且無烟爨 - Living in the mountains with no smoke from cooking.
    • 有一物可以疗之 - There is something that can cure them.
    • 念君逺来相遗 - Thinking of your distant journey and what you have brought.
    • 自起于梁栋之间 - Rising from the beams and rafters.
    • 脱药囊开啓 - Opening the medicine bag.
    • 其中有百余颗如藊豆之状 - Inside there are over a hundred beans like broad beans.
    • 俾于药室取铛拾薪汲泉而煑 - To the medicine room, take a pot, gather firewood, and fetch water to cook.
    • 良久盛有香气 - After a long time, it emits a fragrant smell.
    • 视之已如掌大 - When looked at, it is already as big as a palm.
    • 可以食矣 - It can be eaten now.
    • 渇即取铛中余水饮之 - When thirsty, take the remaining water from the pot to drink.
    • 士则方防其半已极丰饱 - The scholar felt half full and extremely satisfied.
    • 汝得至此当有宿分 - You must have a special destiny to reach here.
    • 自兹三十年间不饥渇 - From now on, for thirty years, you will not be hungry or thirsty.
    • 俗情虑将淡泊也 - Your worldly desires and thoughts will become more peaceful.
    • 他时位至方伯 - In the future, you will hold the position of a high official.
    • 当取罗浮相近 - You should take a place near Luofu Mountain.
    • 傥能脱去纷华 - If you can leave behind worldly distractions.
    • 兼获长生之道 - You will also attain the way to immortality.
    • 辞家日久可以还矣 - After being away from home for a long time, you can return.
    • 士则将欲告归 - The scholar wished to return home.
    • 因述虑失道 - Because he was concerned about losing his way.
    • 勿忧去此二三里 - Do not worry; it is only two or three miles away.
    • 与采薪人相值 - Meet a woodcutter.
    • 可以随之而至国门 - You can follow him to the city gate.
    • 既出于山隅果有采薪者在路侧 - Upon leaving the mountain, there was indeed a woodcutter by the roadside.
    • 或问者姓名竟无所对 - When asked about the woodcutter's name, there was no answer.
    • 才经信宿已及樊川村野 - After a few days, he reached the villages of Fan River.
    • 既还辇毂不喜更尝滋味 - After returning to the capital, he did not enjoy trying other foods.
    • 日觉气壮神清 - He felt increasingly strong and clear-minded.
    • 有骖鸾驭之意 - He had the intention to ride a phoenix and a dragon.
    • 衣褐杖藜多止岩岫 - Wearing rough clothing and walking with a staff, he often stayed in rocky hills.
    • 居守卢仆射耽味黙 - The governor Lu, who enjoyed quiet contemplation.
    • 思覩异人有道流述其事 - Thinking of meeting extraordinary people, a Daoist told him the story.
    • 延之致于门下 - He invited him to his residence.
    • 及闻方伯之説因以处士奏官 - Upon hearing the story of the high official, he recommended the recluse for an official position.
    • 自梓州别驾作牧建溪 - He became the governor of Jianxi from the position of deputy governor of Zizhou.
    • 时年已九十到郡才经周岁 - At the age of ninety, he arrived at the prefecture and served for only one year.
    • 解印乃归罗浮 - He resigned and returned to Luofu Mountain.
    • 及韦相公宙出镇广南使人访之 - When Wei, the chancellor, went to Guangnan, he sent someone to visit him.
    • 犹在山谷大中十四年之任建安路 - He was still in the mountains during the 14th year of Dazhong.
    • 由江浙时萧相国观风浙右于桂楼宿宴召之 - When Xiang, the chancellor, passed through Jiang and Zhe, he summoned him to a banquet at the Gui Pavilion.
    • 唯饮酒数杯他皆无食也 - He only drank a few cups of wine and did not eat anything else.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 糧已絶廹于枵腹 - Refers to a state of extreme hunger, a common theme in classical literature.
    • 食料 - Food supplies, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 自居山谷且無烟爨 - Describes a life of poverty and isolation, common in classical Chinese literature.
    • 有一物可以疗之 - Suggests a remedy for a physical or spiritual condition, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 念君逺来相遗 - Shows gratitude and acknowledgment of the visitor's effort, a common theme in classical Chinese hospitality.
    • 自起于梁栋之间 - Metaphorically describes the source of the remedy, often used in classical Chinese literature.
    • 药 - Medicine, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 其中有百余颗如藊豆之状 - Describes the appearance of the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 俾于药室取铛拾薪汲泉而煑 - Describes the process of preparing the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 良久盛有香气 - Indicates the successful preparation of the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 视之已如掌大 - Describes the size of the prepared remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 可以食矣 - Confirms the readiness of the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 渇即取铛中余水饮之 - Describes the method of consuming the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 士则方防其半已极丰饱 - Indicates the effectiveness of the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese medicine.
    • 汝得至此当有宿分 - Suggests a karmic or fated connection, a common theme in classical Chinese philosophy.
    • 自兹三十年 - Promises long-term benefits, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 俗情虑将淡泊也 - Suggests a spiritual transformation, a common theme in classical Chinese philosophy.
    • 他时位至方伯 - Predicts a high social status, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 当取罗浮相近 - Suggests a future location, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 傥能脱去纷华 - Encourages renunciation of material wealth, a common theme in classical Chinese philosophy.
    • 兼获长生之道 - Promises eternal life, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 辞家日久可以还矣 - Suggests a period of absence followed by a return, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 士则将欲告归 - Indicates a desire to go back, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 因述虑失道 - Shows worry about getting lost, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 勿忧去此二三里 - Reassures the scholar, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 与采薪人相值 - Suggests a chance encounter, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 可以随之而至国门 - Provides a way to find the destination, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 既出于山隅果有采薪者在路侧 - Confirms the encounter, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 或问者姓名竟无所对 - Indicates the woodcutter's anonymity, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 才经信宿已及樊川村野 - Shows the journey's progress, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 既还辇毂不喜更尝滋味 - Indicates a preference for the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 日觉气壮神清 - Describes the positive effects of the remedy, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 有骖鸾驭之意 - Suggests a desire for transcendence, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 衣褐杖藜多止岩岫 - Describes a simple and secluded lifestyle, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 居守卢仆射耽味黙 - Describes the governor's character, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 思覩异人有道流述其事 - Suggests a spiritual quest, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 延之致于门下 - Indicates the governor's hospitality, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 及闻方伯之説因以处士奏官 - Shows the governor's recognition of the recluse's abilities, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 自梓州别驾作牧建溪 - Describes the recluse's career advancement, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 时年已九十到郡才经周岁 - Indicates the recluse's advanced age and short tenure, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 解印乃归罗浮 - Shows the recluse's decision to retire, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 及韦相公宙出镇广南使人访之 - Indicates the chancellor's interest in the recluse, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 犹在山谷大中十四年之任建安路 - Confirms the recluse's continued presence in the mountains, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 由江浙时萧相国观风浙右于桂楼宿宴召之 - Describes the chancellor's invitation, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
    • 唯饮酒数杯他皆无食也 - Shows the recluse's ascetic lifestyle, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

    The food supply has been exhausted to the point of hunger. Please use food to save them. The one who can help said, "Living in the mountains with no smoke from cooking, there is something that can cure them. Thinking of your distant journey and what you have brought, it rises from the beams and rafters. Open the medicine bag and inside there are over a hundred beans like broad beans. Take a pot to the medicine room, gather firewood, and fetch water to cook. After a long time, it emits a fragrant smell. When looked at, it is already as big as a palm. It can be eaten now. When thirsty, take the remaining water from the pot to drink." The scholar felt half full and extremely satisfied. He said, "You must have a special destiny to reach here. From now on, for thirty years, you will not be hungry or thirsty. Your worldly desires and thoughts will become more peaceful. In the future, you will hold the position of a high official. You should take a place near Luofu Mountain. If you can leave behind worldly distractions, you will also attain the way to immortality. After being away from home for a long time, you can return." The scholar wished to return home because he was concerned about losing his way. "Do not worry; it is only two or three miles away. Meet a woodcutter, and you can follow him to the city gate." Upon leaving the mountain, there was indeed a woodcutter by the roadside. When asked about the woodcutter's name, there was no answer. After a few days, he reached the villages of Fan River. After returning to the capital, he did not enjoy trying other foods. He felt increasingly strong and clear-minded. He had the intention to ride a phoenix and a dragon. Wearing rough clothing and walking with a staff, he often stayed in rocky hills. The governor Lu, who enjoyed quiet contemplation, thought of meeting extraordinary people. A Daoist told him the story, and he invited the scholar to his residence. Upon hearing the story of the high official, he recommended the recluse for an official position. He became the governor of Jianxi from the position of deputy governor of Zizhou. At the age of ninety, he arrived at the prefecture and served for only one year. He resigned and returned to Luofu Mountain. When Wei, the chancellor, went to Guangnan, he sent someone to visit him. He was still in the mountains during the 14th year of Dazhong. When Xiang, the chancellor, passed through Jiang and Zhe, he summoned him to a banquet at the Gui Pavilion. He only drank a few cups of wine and did not eat anything else. [66]

    韦颛枭鸣
    　　大中年韦颛举进士词学优赡而贫窭滋甚岁暮饥寒无以自给有韦光者待以宗党【后名殷裕之】辍居所外舎馆之放牓之夕风雪凝互报光成名者络绎而至颛略无登第之耗光延之于堂际小阁备设肴慰安之见光婢妾罗列衣装仆者排比鞍马颛夜分归于所止拥炉而坐愁叹无已光成名将修贺礼寝榻廹于壊牖以横竹挂席蔽之檐际忽有呜枭顷之集于竹上颛神魄惊骇杖策出户逐之飞起复还久而方去谓仆者曰我失意亦无所恨妖禽作怪如此兼恐横罹灾患俄而禁鼓忽鸣牓到颛已登第光之服用车马悉皆遗焉世以鵩至枭鸣不祥之兆近观数事亦不然乎 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 大中年 - A period during the reign of Emperor Wuzong of Tang, specifically the years 847–860.
    • 舉進士 - Refers to taking the imperial examination for the position of jinshi (advanced scholar-official).
    • 詞學優赡 - Means 'superior in literary talent and knowledge.'
    • 貧窭滋甚 - Means 'extremely poor and destitute.'
    • 歲暮饑寒無以自給 - Means 'at the end of the year, hungry and cold, unable to sustain oneself.'
    • 有韋光者待以宗黨 - Means 'there was a man named Wei Guang who treated him as a kinsman.'
    • 辍居所外舎館之 - Means 'he moved out of his own house and housed him there.'
    • 放牓之夕 - Refers to the night when the imperial examination results were announced.
    • 風雪凝互 - Means 'a heavy snowfall and strong winds.'
    • 報光成名者絡绎而至 - Means 'people reporting Wei Guang's success came in a continuous stream.'
    • 略無登第之耗 - Means 'there was no news of his success.'
    • 延之於堂際小閣 - Means 'invited him to a small room in the hall.'
    • 備設肴慰安之 - Means 'prepared a feast to comfort him.'
    • 見光婢妾羅列衣装仆者排比鞍馬 - Means 'saw Wei Guang's maidservants and attendants lined up, with horses and saddles arranged.'
    • 夜分歸於所止 - Means 'returned to his lodging at midnight.'
    • 擁爐而坐愁叹無已 - Means 'sat by the stove, endlessly lamenting.'
    • 光成名将修贺礼 - Means 'awaiting news of Wei Guang's success to prepare congratulations.'
    • 寝榻廹于壊牖以横竹挂席蔽之 - Means 'the bed was cramped against a broken window, covered with bamboo and curtains.'
    • 檐際忽有呜枭 - Means 'a screech owl suddenly appeared on the eaves.'
    • 顷之集于竹上 - Means 'soon perched on the bamboo.'
    • 神魄惊骇 - Means 'his spirit was terrified.'
    • 杖策出户逐之 - Means 'he took a stick and went out to chase it.'
    • 飞起复还 - Means 'flew up and came back.'
    • 久而方去 - Means 'after a long time, it finally left.'
    • 失意亦无所恨 - Means 'even if unsuccessful, he had no regrets.'
    • 妖禽作怪如此 - Means 'such an omen from a strange bird.'
    • 横罹灾患 - Means 'suffering misfortune unexpectedly.'
    • 俄而禁鼓忽鸣 - Means 'soon the drum of the palace gate suddenly sounded.'
    • 牓到颛已登第 - Means 'the list of successful candidates arrived, and Wei Zuan had passed.'
    • 服用车马悉皆遗焉 - Means 'all the clothing, carriages, and horses were left behind.'
    • 鵩至枭鸣不祥之兆 - Means 'the arrival of a cuckoo or the screech of an owl as an omen of bad luck.'
    • 世以鵩至枭鸣不祥之兆近观数事亦不然乎 - Means 'the world considers the arrival of a cuckoo or the screech of an owl as an omen of bad luck, but recent events show otherwise.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 大中年 - A specific period in the Tang Dynasty, known for its cultural and political developments.
    • 進士 - A prestigious position in the imperial examination system, indicating high academic achievement.
    • 宗黨 - Refers to kinship or clan relations, emphasizing the social bonds between Wei Guang and Wei Zuan.
    • 放牓之夕 - The night when the results of the imperial examination were announced, a significant event in the lives of scholars.
    • 鵩至枭鸣 - These are traditional omens of bad luck, often associated with death or misfortune.
    • 禁鼓 - The drum of the palace gate, signaling important events or the arrival of official documents.

    Proper Nouns

    • 大中年 - A period during the reign of Emperor Wuzong of Tang.
    • 韋颛 - The main character, a scholar who took the imperial examination.
    • 韋光 - A man who treated Wei Zuan as a kinsman and provided him with shelter.
    • 殷裕之 - Wei Guang's later name, indicating a change in his status or identity.
    • 禁鼓 - The drum of the palace gate, a symbol of official authority.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. [67]

    昔邓艾枭鸣牙旗乃军胜之兆张率更闻于庭树亦授官之祥以此推之未必皆为不吉者
    　　命相日雨雹【崔沆豆卢琢】
    　　干符六年夏五月巢冦自广陵将及襄汉朝廷以王铎令公为南面都统崔相国豆卢相国同日策拜宣麻之际殿庭雾气四塞及政事堂立班贺有雹大如鸡卵【时五月二十三日】识者以为钧轴不祥之兆明年大冦攻陷京师二相俱及于难其天意乎非人事也
    　　李生见神物遗酒
    　　咸通中有中牟尉李浔寓居圃田别墅禀性刚戾不以神为意每见人衔杯酹酒无不怒而止之一旦暴得风方卧檐庑之下忽有田父立于榻前云隣伍间欲来省疾见数人形貌尫劣服饰或青或紫后有矮仆提酒两壶相与歴堦而上左右妻孥悉无所覩谓浔曰尔常日负气忽于我曹至于醪醴之间必为他人爱惜今有醇酎数斗众欲遗君一醉俄以巨盆满酌逼饮两壶俱尽牀笫衾禂皆是余沥将出谓浔曰何似当时惜酒自兹百骸昏悴如病宿酲寝瘵惙然数月方愈冯给事为郑州刺史亲召李生而説之 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昔邓艾枭鸣牙旗乃军胜之兆 - Classical expression indicating that the crows crying around General Deng Ai's flag was a sign of military victory.
    • 张率更闻于庭树亦授官之祥 - Classical expression suggesting that the sound of crows in Zhang Rui's courtyard was a good omen for receiving an official position.
    • 命相日雨雹 - Superstitious belief that rain accompanied by hail on the day of one's birth or appointment is an ill omen.
    • 干符六年夏五月巢冦自广陵将及襄汉 - Refers to the time period and location of the rebellion led by Huang Chao.
    • 朝廷以王铎令公为南面都统崔相国豆卢相国同日策拜宣麻之际殿庭雾气四塞及政事堂立班贺有雹大如鸡卵 - Describes the fog and hail during the ceremony of appointing Wang Dù and Doulu as the Southern Army Commander and Chancellor respectively.
    • 识者以为钧轴不祥之兆 - Scholars believed this was an omen of misfortune for the state.
    • 李生见神物遗酒 - Refers to a supernatural event where a spirit left wine for Li Xun.
    • 咸通中有中牟尉李浔寓居圃田别墅 - Indicates the time and place where Li Xun lived.
    • 禀性刚戾不以鬼神为意 - Describes Li Xun's personality and his lack of fear of ghosts or gods.
    • 每见人衔杯酹酒无不怒而止之 - Describes Li Xun's reaction to people drinking and pouring wine.
    • 忽有田父立于榻前云邻伍间欲来省疾见数人形貌尪劣服饰或青或紫后有矮仆提酒两壶相与历阶而上 - A supernatural event where a farmer and others appear to visit Li Xun.
    • 左右妻孥悉无所睹 - Indicates that only Li Xun saw the visitors.
    • 谓浔曰尔常日负气忽于我曹至于醪醴之间必为他人爱惜今有醇酎数斗众欲遗君一醉俄以巨盆满酌逼饮两壶俱尽 - The visitors' words and actions, forcing Li Xun to drink the wine.
    • 床笫衾稠皆是余沥将出谓浔曰何似当时惜酒自兹百骸昏悴如病宿酲寝瘵惙然数月方愈 - The aftermath of the supernatural event and its effect on Li Xun's health.
    • 冯给事为郑州刺史亲召李生而说之 - Describes how Feng, the Magistrate of Zhengzhou, summoned Li Xun and spoke to him about the event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 邓艾 - A famous general of the Three Kingdoms period known for his military strategies.
    • 张率更 - A historical figure whose name suggests a high-ranking official.
    • 干符六年 - A year in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄巢 - A rebel leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 王铎 - A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔相国豆卢相国 - High-ranking officials during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 咸通 - A reign title of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 中牟尉 - An official position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 圃田别墅 - A specific place where Li Xun lived.
    • 冯给事 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 邓艾 - A famous general of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 张率更 - A historical figure.
    • 王铎 - A high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 崔相国豆卢相国 - High-ranking officials during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 黄巢 - A rebel leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 李浔 - A historical figure.
    • 冯给事 - A high-ranking official in the Tang Dynasty.

    In the past, the crows crying around General Deng Ai's flag was a sign of military victory, and the sound of crows in Zhang Rui's courtyard was a good omen for receiving an official position. From this, we can infer that not all omens are necessarily bad.

    On the day of one's birth or appointment, rain accompanied by hail is considered an ill omen. [Cui Hang and Doulu Zhuo]

    During the sixth year of the Ganfu era in the summer of May, the rebel Huang Chao was advancing from Guangling towards Xianghan. The court appointed Wang Du as the Southern Army Commander and Doulu and Cui as Chancellors on the same day. During the ceremony, the palace courtyard was filled with fog, and when they stood to greet at the Hall of Political Affairs, hail as large as chicken eggs fell. [It was the 23rd day of May.] Scholars believed this was an omen of misfortune for the state. The following year, the rebels attacked and captured the capital, and both Chancellors died in the conflict. Was this the will of heaven, or was it due to human actions?

    Li Xun saw a supernatural being leave wine for him.

    During the Xiantong era, there was a magistrate of Zhongmu named Li Xun who lived in a villa in Putian. He had a fierce and arrogant nature and did not fear ghosts or gods. Whenever he saw people drinking and pouring wine, he would become angry and stop them. One day, he suddenly fell ill with a severe cold and was lying under the eaves. Suddenly, a farmer stood before his bed and said, "Neighbors came to visit you and saw several people with thin and weak appearances, dressed in blue or purple. There was also a short servant carrying two pots of wine, and they climbed the steps together." Li Xun's wife and children saw nothing. The farmer said to Li Xun, "You always act arrogantly and are stingy with wine. Now we have several pots of fine wine and want to offer you a drink." Soon, they filled a large bowl with wine and forced him to drink until both pots were empty. The bed and bedding were soaked with the remaining wine. As they were about to leave, they said to Li Xun, "How did you act so stingy with wine before? From now on, your body will be weak and sick like someone suffering from a hangover." Li Xun remained in a state of illness for several months before recovering. Feng, the Magistrate of Zhengzhou, personally summoned Li Xun and spoke to him about the event. [68]

    説方士
    　　武宗皇帝好神仙异术海内道流方士多至辇下赵归眞探机善制铅汞气貌清爽见者无不竦敬请于禁中筑望仙台髙百尺以为鸾骖驭可指期而降常云飞链中须得生银诏使于乐平采取既而大役工徒所出者皆衔石矿非烹冶乃无从而得归真斋醮数朝写御书置于岩穴间俄有老人防杖而至曰山川藏寳葢因有道而出况明主以修眞为念是何感应不臻尊师无复懐忧明旦当从所请语罢而出莫知其所之是夕有声如雷山矿豁开数丈银液坌然而涌出与入用之数相符禁中修链至多外人罕知其术复有金陵人许元长王琼者善书符幻变近于役使神防昌初召至京国出入宫闱武皇谓之曰 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 説方士 - Title meaning 'On the Taoist Sages.'
    • 海内道流方士多至辇下 - Means 'many Daoist sages and magicians came to the capital from all over the realm.'
    • 赵归眞探机善制铅汞气貌清爽 - Describes Zhao Guizhen as investigating profound mechanisms, skilled in making lead and mercury, and having a clear and refreshing appearance.
    • 常云飞链中须得生银 - Means 'he often said that in the process of flying refinement, live silver must be obtained.'
    • 诏使于乐平采取 - Means 'an imperial order was given to gather materials in LePing.'
    • 既而大役工徒 - Means 'afterwards, a large-scale labor force was mobilized.'
    • 所出者皆衔石矿 - Means 'what was produced all contained stone and ore.'
    • 烹冶乃无从而得 - Means 'only through smelting could it be obtained.'
    • 斋醮数朝写御书置于岩穴间 - Means 'he performed ritual fasting and worship for several days, wrote imperial edicts, and placed them in caves and grottoes.'
    • 俄有老人防杖而至 - Means 'soon, an old man with a staff arrived.'
    • 山川藏寳葢因有道而出 - Means 'mountains and rivers hide treasures because of virtue and emerge.'
    • 况明主以修眞为念 - Means 'especially an enlightened ruler who cultivates immortality as a thought.'
    • 是何感应不臻 - Means 'why do responses not arrive?'
    • 尊师无复懐忧 - Means 'the respected teacher no longer worries.'
    • 明旦当从所请 - Means 'the next day, he will follow the request.'
    • 语罢而出莫知其所之 - Means 'after speaking, he left, and no one knew where he went.'
    • 是夕有声如雷 - Means 'that night, there was a sound like thunder.'
    • 山矿豁开数丈 - Means 'the mountain ore opened up several zhang (units of length).'
    • 银液坌然而涌出 - Means 'silver liquid suddenly erupted out.'
    • 与入用之数相符 - Means 'and matched the amount used.'
    • 禁中修链至多外人罕知其术 - Means 'within the palace, refinement was done in large quantities, and outsiders rarely knew his method.'
    • 复有金陵人许元长王琼者善书符幻变近于役使神 - Means 'there were also people from Jinling named Xu Yuanzhang and Wang Qiong, who were skilled in writing talismans and performing magical transformations, close to commanding spirits.'
    • 防昌初召至京国出入宫闱 - Means 'in the early years of the Fanchang era, they were summoned to the capital and entered and exited the palace.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武宗皇帝 - Emperor Wuzong of the Tang Dynasty (r. 840-846), known for his patronage of Daoism and alchemy.
    • 赵归眞 - A famous Daoist alchemist and magician during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 乐平 - A place name, possibly referring to a region where minerals were extracted.
    • 金陵 - An ancient name for Nanjing, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 防昌 - A reign title of Emperor Wuzong, indicating a specific period of his rule.
    • 禁中 - Refers to the inner palace, emphasizing the secretive nature of the activities described.
    • 金陵人许元长王琼 - Names of two individuals known for their skills in writing talismans and performing magical transformations.
    • 出入宫闱 - Indicates the close relationship between these individuals and the imperial court.

    Translation:

    On the Taoist Sages

    Emperor Wuzong was fond of immortality and magical arts. Many Daoist sages and magicians from all over the realm came to the capital. Zhao Guizhen, who investigated the profound mechanisms, was skilled in making lead and mercury, and had a clear and refreshing appearance. Those who saw him were all deeply respectful. He often said that in the process of flying refinement, live silver must be obtained. An imperial order was given to gather materials in LePing. Afterwards, a large-scale labor force was mobilized, and what was produced all contained stone and ore. Only through smelting could it be obtained. He performed ritual fasting and worship for several days, wrote imperial edicts, and placed them in caves and grottoes. Soon, an old man with a staff arrived and said, "Mountains and rivers hide treasures because of virtue and emerge. Especially an enlightened ruler who cultivates immortality as a thought, why do responses not arrive? The respected teacher no longer worries. The next day, he will follow the request." After speaking, he left, and no one knew where he went. That night, there was a sound like thunder, and the mountain ore opened up several zhang. Silver liquid suddenly erupted out, matching the amount used. Within the palace, the refinement was very much conducted, and outsiders rarely knew his method. There were also people from Jinling named Xu Yuanzhang and Wang Qiong, who were skilled in writing talismans and performing mysterious transformations, close to commanding spirits. In the early years of the Fanchang era, they were summoned to the capital, entering and exiting the palace. Emperor Wuzong said to them, "..." [69]

    吾闻先朝有明崇俨善于符箓尝取罗浮山柑子以资御果万里往来止于旬日我虽圣德不逮前朝卿之术岂便劣于崇俨元长谢曰臣之受法未臻妙渉越山海恐诬圣德但千里之间可一日而至上曰东都常进石榴时已熟矣卿今夕当致十颗元长奉语而出及旦寝殿始开金盘贮石榴致于御榻俄有中使奉进亦以所失之数上闻灵验变通皆如此王琼妙于化物无所不能方冬以药栽培桃杏数株一夕繁英尽发芳蘂秾艳月余方谢及武皇猒代归眞与琼俱逐岭表唯元长逸去莫知所在昊天观周尊师干符中年九十七自言以童幼间便居洞庭山诸父隠尧深得真道有张孺华者襄汉豪士耽味黙一旦 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吾闻 - Formal expression meaning 'I have heard' or 'I have been told', often used to introduce a narrative or statement of fact.
    • 先朝有明崇俨 - Refers to a renowned figure named Ming Chongyan from an earlier dynasty.
    • 善于符箓 - Means 'skilled in talismans and magical formulas', indicating expertise in esoteric practices.
    • 尝取罗浮山柑子以资御果 - Means 'once obtained tangerines from Mount Luofu to supply imperial fruits', referring to a specific action involving imperial service.
    • 万里往来止于旬日 - Means 'traveling thousands of miles, yet completing it within ten days', highlighting extraordinary speed or ability.
    • 我虽圣德不逮前朝 - Means 'though my virtue falls short of the previous dynasty', expressing humility or self-deprecation.
    • 卿之术岂便劣于崇俨 - Means 'is your skill then inferior to that of Chongyan?', posing a rhetorical question.
    • 元长谢曰 - 'Yuan Chang replied', introducing a direct speech segment.
    • 臣之受法未臻玄妙 - Means 'my training has not reached the profound mysteries', indicating a limitation in skill.
    • 涉越山海恐诬圣德 - Means 'traversing mountains and seas might be seen as a mockery of virtue', expressing concern about the implications of certain actions.
    • 但千里之间可一日而至上 - Means 'but within a thousand miles, it can be done in a single day', offering a partial concession.
    • 东都常进石榴时已熟矣 - Means 'in the eastern capital, pomegranates are often presented, and they are already ripe', providing additional context.
    • 卿今夕当致十颗 - Means 'you should bring ten in this evening', issuing a command or request.
    • 元长奉语而出 - Means 'Yuan Chang followed the command and departed', describing an action.
    • 及旦寝殿始开金盘贮石榴致于御榻 - Means 'by dawn, the sleeping chamber was opened, and a golden dish containing pomegranates was placed on the imperial bed', describing a sequence of events.
    • 俄有中使奉进亦以所失之数上闻 - Means 'soon, a mid-level official presented the pomegranates, and the number of missing ones was reported to the emperor', indicating a response to the command.
    • 灵验变通皆如此 - Means 'miraculous transformations and changes occur in this way', summarizing the nature of the events.
    • 王琼妙于化物无所不能 - Means 'Wang Qiong is adept at transforming things, able to do anything', praising someone's abilities.
    • 方冬以药栽培桃杏数株 - Means 'in winter, using medicine, several peach and apricot trees were cultivated', describing a specific action.
    • 一夕繁英尽发芳蘂秾艳 - Means 'one night, all the flowers bloomed, fragrant and luxuriantly beautiful', describing a sudden change.
    • 月余方谢 - Means 'after a month, they began to wither', indicating a temporal progression.
    • 及武皇厌代归真与琼俱逐岭表 - Means 'when the emperor grew weary and passed away, he returned to his true nature, and Wang Qiong was also exiled to the southern regions', describing a historical event.
    • 唯元长逸去莫知所在 - Means 'only Yuan Chang escaped, and no one knows where he is now', concluding the narrative.
    • 昊天观周尊师干符中年九十七 - Means 'Zhou Zunshi of the Hao Tian Temple was ninety-seven years old during the Gan Fu period', providing biographical information.
    • 自言以童幼间便居洞庭山 - Means 'he said that from childhood, he had lived on Dongting Mountain', describing a personal history.
    • 诸父隐尧深得真道 - Means 'his father and uncles, who were deeply hidden and had attained true Dao', praising the family's spiritual achievements.
    • 有张孺华者襄汉豪士耽味玄默 - Means 'there was a man named Zhang Ru Hua, a prominent figure in Xiang and Han, who was absorbed in the mysteries of silence', describing a character.
    • 一旦 - Means 'one day' or 'suddenly', indicating a sudden change or event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 先朝 - Refers to a previous dynasty, often used to evoke a sense of historical continuity.
    • 明崇俨 - A historical figure known for his magical abilities and service to the imperial court.
    • 符箓 - Talismans and magical formulas used in Daoist practices.
    • 罗浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong province known for its scenic beauty and spiritual significance.
    • 御果 - Imperial fruits, often presented as a sign of respect or favor.
    • 东都 - The eastern capital, often referring to Luoyang during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 武皇 - The emperor, often used to refer to a powerful ruler.
    • 厌代归真 - A phrase indicating the emperor's death and return to his true nature, often used in imperial contexts.
    • 岭表 - The southern regions of China, often used to describe areas beyond the Five Ridges.
    • 昊天观 - A temple or observatory, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 干符 - A year or period in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 洞庭山 - A mountain in Jiangsu province, known for its scenic beauty and spiritual significance.
    • 真道 - True Dao, referring to the highest level of spiritual attainment in Daoism.
    • 张孺华 - A historical figure known for his spiritual pursuits and influence.

    Proper Nouns

    • 明崇俨 - A historical figure known for his magical abilities.
    • 罗浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong province.
    • 东都 - The eastern capital, often referring to Luoyang during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 武皇 - The emperor, often used to refer to a powerful ruler.
    • 岭表 - The southern regions of China.
    • 昊天观 - A temple or observatory, often associated with Daoist practices.
    • 洞庭山 - A mountain in Jiangsu province.
    • 张孺华 - A historical figure known for his spiritual pursuits.

    I have heard that in the previous dynasty, there was a renowned figure named Ming Chongyan who was skilled in talismans and magical formulas. He once obtained tangerines from Mount Luofu to supply imperial fruits, traveling thousands of miles yet completing it within ten days. Though my virtue falls short of the previous dynasty, is your skill then inferior to that of Chongyan? Yuan Chang replied, "My training has not reached the profound mysteries. Traversing mountains and seas might be seen as a mockery of virtue." However, within a thousand miles, it can be done in a single day. The emperor said, "In the eastern capital, pomegranates are often presented, and they are already ripe. You should bring ten in this evening." Yuan Chang followed the command and departed. By dawn, the sleeping chamber was opened, and a golden dish containing pomegranates was placed on the imperial bed. Soon, a mid-level official presented the pomegranates, and the number of missing ones was reported to the emperor. Miraculous transformations and changes occur in this way. Wang Qiong is adept at transforming things, able to do anything. In winter, using medicine, several peach and apricot trees were cultivated. One night, all the flowers bloomed, fragrant and luxuriantly beautiful, and after a month, they began to wither. When the emperor grew weary and passed away, he returned to his true nature, and Wang Qiong was also exiled to the southern regions. Only Yuan Chang escaped, and no one knows where he is now. Zhou Zunshi of the Hao Tian Temple was ninety-seven years old during the Gan Fu period. He said that from childhood, he had lived on Dongting Mountain. His father and uncles, who were deeply hidden and had attained true Dao, were there. There was a man named Zhang Ru Hua, a prominent figure in Xiang and Han, who was absorbed in the mysteries of silence. One day, a sudden event occurred. [70]

    广赍财宝访道于江湖之间至吴门知尧出世修链迳往洞庭诣之囊槖中所挈金帛倾竭以资香火尧知其志俾于岸顶坐守药炉其或风雨晦防徃往有神物来萃殊形诡状深可骇人孺华端防自安竟不微动如此者渉于周歳尧谓之曰炉中链药乃七返灵砂也虽非九转金丹饵之可还魂返魄曩令子弟数軰守之靡不畏怯而罢汝相从未久遂能苦节如是及鼎开药成才成十粒但令宝之以囊箧未传吞饵之法孺华以去乡逾年一旦告归觐省尧别谓之曰吾知汝未能久住自兹复为世网所萦茍慕仙之意不忘勿以嚣尘为恋付汝之药每丸可益筭十二有疾终者审其未至朽败虽渉旬能使再活然事关阴 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 赍财宝访道于江湖之间 - Carrying wealth to seek the Dao in the rivers and lakes; describes a journey to seek spiritual enlightenment.
    • 倾竭以资香火 - Exhausting everything to support incense and fire; metaphorically means to spend all resources on religious or spiritual practices.
    • 殊形诡状深可骇人 - Strange forms and bizarre appearances can deeply frighten people; describes unusual or fantastical creatures or phenomena.
    • 端防自安竟不微动 - Remains calm and composed, never showing any sign of disturbance; describes someone who stays calm despite fear or danger.
    • 付汝之药每丸可益筭十二 - Each pill you are given can add twelve years to your lifespan; indicates the magical or medicinal properties of the elixir.
    • 审其未至朽败 - Examine if it has not yet decayed; suggests checking the condition of the elixir to ensure its effectiveness.
    • 虽渉旬能使再活 - Even after ten days, it can make the dead come back to life; describes the powerful rejuvenating properties of the elixir.
    • 事关阴 - This matter concerns the underworld; suggests that the elixir or the process has connections to the afterlife or supernatural realms.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 尧 - Likely a reference to a historical or legendary figure known for alchemical practices or spiritual cultivation.
    • 洞庭 - Refers to the Dongting Lake, a significant location in Chinese mythology and literature, often associated with immortals and alchemical practices.
    • 七返灵砂 - Seven-refined spiritual sand; a term used in alchemical texts to describe a refined elixir or substance.
    • 九转金丹 - Nine-transformed golden pill; a legendary alchemical elixir believed to grant immortality.
    • 香火 - Incense and fire; symbolizes religious or spiritual practices, often associated with temples or shrines.
    • 神物 - Supernatural beings; refers to mythical or magical creatures.
    • 朽败 - Decay; in this context, it refers to the deterioration of the elixir or the body.
    • 阴 - Underworld; refers to the realm of the dead or the supernatural.

    Proper Nouns

    • 尧 - A proper noun referring to a historical or legendary figure.
    • 洞庭 - A proper noun referring to Dongting Lake.
    • 七返灵砂 - A proper noun referring to a specific alchemical elixir.
    • 九转金丹 - A proper noun referring to a legendary alchemical elixir.
    • 香火 - A proper noun referring to religious or spiritual practices.
    • 神物 - A proper noun referring to supernatural beings.
    • 阴 - A proper noun referring to the underworld or supernatural realm.

    Gu carried wealth to seek the Dao in the rivers and lakes, and when he learned that  Yao had appeared in the world to cultivate and refine, he went directly to Dongting Lake to meet him. He exhausted everything he had to support incense and fire.  Yao knew his resolve and allowed him to sit at the top of the bank to watch over the alchemical furnace. Often, there were strange forms and bizarre appearances, deeply frightening people, and supernatural beings would come to gather. Despite the deep fear, Gu remained calm and composed, never showing any sign of disturbance. This continued for a whole year.  Yao then told him, "The elixir in the furnace is the Seven-Refined Spiritual Sand, not the Nine-Transformed Golden Pill, but if one eats it, it can restore the soul and return the spirit. I once asked several disciples to guard it, but none could withstand the fear and gave up. You have not been guarding it for long, yet you have endured so well. When the furnace was opened and the elixir was completed, only ten pills were produced. Each pill can add twelve years to your lifespan. Keep them in a chest, but I have not yet taught you the method of swallowing the elixir."

    Gu had been away from home for over a year and one day decided to return to visit his family.  Yao bid him farewell and said, "I know you cannot stay here long. From now on, you will be caught in the worldly net. If you still aspire to immortality, do not be attached to the mundane world. The elixir I gave you can add twelve years to your lifespan for each pill. If you are sick or dead, examine if it has not yet decayed. Even after ten days, it can make the dead come back to life. However, this matter concerns the underworld, so be cautious." [71]

    隲非行道有心之徒不可轻授凡欲此药救人当焚香啓告吾为助尔孺华归甚为乡里所敬父母遘疾而殁服之皆愈居数岁复诣洞庭系舟于金陵江岸有良贾徐士则者乘巨艘十余只亦于浦间同泊有子一人方及壮岁无疾而殒于中夜父母咸以衰耄哭泣不食崇朝孺华悯之因以灵砂往救其初服之时未验再服一粒蹶然而苏云所至之处城府甚严方为吏从拘録俄有二黄衣人手执丹书文字洞庭周尊师令唤防事间有紫衣者据案而坐于是簮笏而兴谓左右曰仙师来召焉可复留乃令放还谓曰汝因此寿命増延当可力行善道士则所将财物分其半以答孺华孺华取钱五十万散施贫乏至洞庭与尧俱 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 隲非行道有心之徒不可轻授 - It cannot be easily given to those who are not sincerely devoted to the path.
    • 凡欲此药救人 - Anyone who wishes to use this medicine to save lives.
    • 焚香啓告 - Burning incense and making a formal announcement or invocation, often in a religious or ritual context.
    • 吾为助尔 - I will help you, indicating the speaker's willingness to assist.
    • 孺华归甚为乡里所敬 - Ru Hua returned and was greatly respected by his village.
    • 父母遘疾而殁 - His parents fell ill and passed away.
    • 服之皆愈 - Those who took it all recovered.
    • 系舟于金陵江岸 - Anchored his boat on the bank of the river in Jinling.
    • 良贾徐士则者 - The good merchant Xu Shize.
    • 乘巨艘十余只 - Traveled in more than ten large ships.
    • 亦于浦间同泊 - Also anchored in the same area.
    • 方及壮岁无疾而殒于中夜 - At the age of prime, he died suddenly in the middle of the night without any illness.
    • 父母咸以衰耄哭泣不食 - His parents, both old and frail, cried and refused to eat.
    • 孺华悯之 - Ru Hua felt pity for them.
    • 因以灵砂往救 - He then went to save them with the divine sand.
    • 其初服之时未验 - At first, when he took it, it did not work.
    • 再服一粒蹶然而苏 - After taking another dose, he suddenly revived.
    • 所至之处城府甚严 - Wherever he went, the city gates were very strict.
    • 方为吏从拘録 - Officials were about to arrest him.
    • 俄有二黄衣人手执丹书文字 - Soon two yellow-robed people appeared, holding red scrolls with writing.
    • 洞庭周尊师令唤防事 - The Master Zhou of Dongting ordered the guard to be called.
    • 紫衣者据案而坐 - A purple-robed person sat at the table.
    • 于是簮笏而兴 - They stood up with their hairpins and ceremonial staffs.
    • 谓左右曰仙师来召焉可复留 - They said to those around them, 'The Immortal Master has come to summon us; we cannot stay here anymore.'
    • 乃令放还 - They then ordered him to be released.
    • 谓曰汝因此寿命増延当可力行善 - They said to him, 'Because of this, your life will be extended; you should strive to do good deeds.'
    • 道士则所将财物分其半以答孺华 - The Taoist divided half of the goods he had brought to repay Ru Hua.
    • 孺华取钱五十万散施贫乏 - Ru Hua took fifty thousand coins and distributed them to the poor and needy.
    • 至洞庭与尧俱 - He arrived at Dongting and met with Yao.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 灵砂 - A type of medicinal substance, often associated with alchemy and traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 洞庭 - Dongting Lake, a significant geographical feature in China, often associated with Daoist legends and immortals.
    • 周尊师 - Master Zhou, a high-ranking figure in the Daoist community, likely a respected teacher or leader.
    • 仙师 - An Immortal Master, a figure in Daoist mythology who possesses supernatural powers and wisdom.
    • 簮笏 - Hairpins and ceremonial staffs, symbols of official rank and respect in ancient China.
    • 紫衣者 - A person dressed in purple, a color often associated with nobility and high status in Chinese culture.
    • 黄衣人 - Yellow-robed people, often associated with Daoist or Buddhist monks or officials.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孺华 - The name of the main character, Ru Hua.
    • 徐士则 - The name of the merchant, Xu Shize.
    • 洞庭 - The name of the lake, Dongting.
    • 周尊师 - The name of the Master, Zhou.
    • 仙师 - The title of the Immortal Master.
    • 紫衣者 - The description of the purple-robed person.
    • 黄衣人 - The description of the yellow-robed people.
    • 尧 - The name of the person Ru Hua met, Yao.

    It cannot be easily given to those who are not sincerely devoted to the path. Anyone who wishes to use this medicine to save lives must burn incense and make a formal announcement, and I will help you. Ru Hua returned and was greatly respected by his village. His parents fell ill and passed away, and those who took the medicine all recovered. After a few years, he traveled to Dongting and anchored his boat on the bank of the river in Jinling. There was a good merchant named Xu Shize who traveled in more than ten large ships and also anchored in the same area. He had a son who was in his prime but died suddenly in the middle of the night without any illness. His parents, both old and frail, cried and refused to eat. Ru Hua felt pity for them and went to save them with the divine sand. At first, when he took it, it did not work, but after taking another dose, he suddenly revived. Wherever he went, the city gates were very strict, and officials were about to arrest him. Soon, two yellow-robed people appeared, holding red scrolls with writing. The Master Zhou of Dongting ordered the guard to be called, and a purple-robed person sat at the table. They stood up with their hairpins and ceremonial staffs and said to those around them, "The Immortal Master has come to summon us; we cannot stay here anymore." They then ordered him to be released and said to him, "Because of this, your life will be extended; you should strive to do good deeds." The Taoist divided half of the goods he had brought to repay Ru Hua. Ru Hua took fifty thousand coins and distributed them to the poor and needy. He arrived at Dongting and met with Yao. [72]

    广谪仙怨词【台州刺史窦宏余撰】
    　　宗天宝十五载正月安禄山反陷没洛阳王师败绩关门不守车驾幸蜀途次马嵬驿六军不发赐贵妃自尽然后驾发行次骆谷上登髙下马谓力士曰吾苍惶出狩长安不辞宗庙此山絶髙望见秦川吾今遥辞陵庙因下马望东再拜呜咽流涕左右皆泣谓力士曰吾听九龄之言不到于此乃命中使往韶州以太牢祭之【中书令张九龄每因奏对未尝不谏诛禄山上怒曰卿岂以王夷甫识石勒陈杀禄山于是不敢谏矣】因上马遂索长笛吹一曲曲成澘然流涕竚立久之时有司旋录成谱及銮驾至成都乃进此谱请曲名上不记 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 谪仙 - A term used to describe a person of extraordinary talent, often with a divine or semi-divine status. In this context, it refers to Li Bai (李白), the renowned Tang Dynasty poet.
    • 天宝十五载 - The fifteenth year of the Tianbao era, a specific period in the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 安禄山反 - Refers to the rebellion led by An Lushan, a significant event in Chinese history known as the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 赐贵妃自尽 - Means 'the emperor ordered the concubine to commit suicide.' The concubine is likely referring to Yang Guifei, the favorite concubine of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 驾行次骆谷 - Means 'the imperial carriage passed through Luogu Pass,' a mountain pass in Shaanxi Province.
    • 登髙下马 - Means 'to dismount and climb a high place,' indicating a moment of reflection or reverence.
    • 再拜 - A formal act of bowing twice, a sign of deep respect or grief.
    • 呜咽流涕 - Means 'to sob and weep,' describing a state of intense emotion.
    • 中书令张九龄 - Refers to Zhang Jiuling, a prominent statesman and poet during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 王夷甫识石勒 - Refers to Wang Yi, a Jin Dynasty official known for his misjudgment of Shi Le, a Xiongnu leader.
    • 太牢祭之 - Refers to a grand sacrificial ceremony using three animals (bull, sheep, and pig) as offerings, indicating the highest level of respect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玄宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a key figure in Chinese history known for his cultural achievements and the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 安禄山 - A military general who rebelled against the Tang Dynasty, leading to significant political and social upheaval.
    • 马嵬驿 - A驿站 (military relay station) in Shaanxi Province where the incident involving Yang Guifei took place.
    • 骆谷 - A mountain pass in Shaanxi Province, a strategic location during the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 秦川 - Refers to the Qin region, a historical area in Shaanxi Province.
    • 张九龄 - A statesman and poet who served as Chancellor during the early Tang Dynasty and is known for his advice to Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 王夷甫识石勒 - A historical anecdote about Wang Yi, who misjudged Shi Le, a Xiongnu leader, leading to his downfall.
    • 成都 - The capital city of Sichuan Province, where Emperor Xuanzong fled during the An Lushan Rebellion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 广谪仙怨词 - The title of the poem.
    • 台州刺史窦宏余 - The author of the poem, Dou Hongyu, the Prefect of Taizhou.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 安禄山 - A military general who rebelled against the Tang Dynasty.
    • 马嵬驿 - A military relay station in Shaanxi Province.
    • 骆谷 - A mountain pass in Shaanxi Province.
    • 秦川 - The Qin region in Shaanxi Province.
    • 张九龄 - A statesman and poet who served as Chancellor during the early Tang Dynasty.
