    • 王夷甫识石勒 - A historical anecdote about Wang Yi and Shi Le.
    • 成都 - The capital city of Sichuan Province.

    Translation:

    "Ode of the Banished Immortal" [Composed by Dou Hongyu, Prefect of Taizhou]

    During the fifteenth year of the Tianbao era in the first month, An Lushan rebelled, captured Luoyang, and the imperial army suffered defeat, failing to hold the passes. The emperor fled to Shu, passing through Ma嵬驿, where the imperial army refused to advance. The emperor ordered the concubine, Yang Guifei, to commit suicide. Afterward, the imperial carriage continued its journey, passing through Luogu Pass. The emperor dismounted and climbed a high place, saying to the eunuch, "I hastily fled from Chang'an without bidding farewell to my ancestral temples. This mountain is extremely high, and I can see the Qin region. I now bid farewell to my ancestral temples from afar." He then dismounted, faced east, and bowed twice, sobbing and weeping. Those around him also wept. The emperor said to the eunuch, "If I had listened to Zhang Jiuling's advice, I would not have reached this point. I now order a messenger to go to Shaoyang to perform a grand sacrificial ceremony with three animals as offerings." [Zhang Jiuling, the Chancellor, often advised the emperor to execute An Lushan, but the emperor was angry and said, "Are you like Wang Yi, who misjudged Shi Le? If I had executed An Lushan, he would have killed me. Therefore, I dared not advise."]

    The emperor then remounted his horse and asked for a long flute, playing a piece. When the piece was completed, he wept bitterly and stood there for a long time. The officials quickly recorded the melody into a score. When the imperial carriage arrived in Chengdu, the score was presented to the emperor, who asked for its name but could not remember it. [73]

    之视左右曰何曲有司具以骆谷望长安下马后索长笛吹出对上良久曰吾省矣吾因思九龄亦别有意可名此曲为谪仙怨其防属马嵬之事厥后以乱离隔絶有人自西川传得者无由知但呼为劒南神曲其音怨切诸曲莫比大厯中江南人盛为此曲随州刺史刘长卿左迁睦州司马祖筵之内吹之为曲长卿遂撰其词意颇自得葢亦不知夲事词云晴川落日初低惆怅孤舟解携鸟去平芜逺近人随流水东西白云千里万里明月前溪后溪独恨长沙谪去江潭春草萋萋余在童幼亦闻长老话谪仙之事颇熟而长卿之词甚是才丽与本 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 之视左右曰 - Indicates someone looking at those around them and speaking; suggests a scene where a person is addressing nearby people.
    • 何曲有 - Interrogative phrase meaning 'what kind of tune is there?' or 'what melody exists?'
    • 具以骆谷望长安 - Descriptive phrase indicating looking at Chang'an from the location of Luogu, suggesting a long-distance view.
    • 下马后索长笛吹出对上 - Sequence of actions: getting off a horse, searching for a long flute, and then playing it in response to something above.
    • 吾省矣 - Colloquial expression meaning 'I understand now' or 'I have realized it.'
    • 吾因思九龄亦别有意可名此曲为谪仙怨 - Reflective statement indicating thinking about 'Jiu Ling' and having a different intention to name the tune 'Zhexian Yuan' (Exile of the Immortal).
    • 其防属马嵬之事 - Reference to the incident at Ma嵬, likely alluding to the historical event involving Yang Guifei and the Tang Dynasty.
    • 厥后以乱离隔绝 - Phrase indicating separation due to chaos and disorder after the incident.
    • 有人自西川传得者无由知 - Statement suggesting that someone from Xichuan obtained the tune, but there is no way to know how.
    • 但呼为剑南神曲 - Phrase indicating that the tune is simply called 'Jiannan Shenqu' (Divine Tune of the Southern Sword).
    • 其音怨切诸曲莫比 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the tune is extremely mournful and surpasses all other tunes in its emotional depth.
    • 大历中江南人盛为此曲 - Statement indicating that during the Dali period, people in Jiangnan were very fond of this tune.
    • 随州刺史刘长卿左迁睦州司马祖筵之内吹之为曲 - Historical reference to Liu Changqing, who was demoted to be the Magistrate of Muzhou and played the tune during a farewell banquet.
    • 长卿遂撰其词意颇自得 - Statement indicating that Liu Changqing then composed the lyrics for the tune, feeling quite pleased with himself.
    • 盖亦不知本事 - Phrase indicating that he may not have known the original story behind the tune.
    • 词云 - Phrase introducing the lyrics, which follow.
    • 晴川落日初低 - Poetic phrase describing a scene of the sun setting over a clear river.
    • 惆怅孤舟解携 - Poetic phrase describing a feeling of sadness as a lone boat departs.
    • 鸟去平芜远近 - Poetic phrase describing birds flying away over distant and nearby fields.
    • 人随流水东西 - Poetic phrase describing people following the flow of water east and west.
    • 白云千里万里 - Poetic phrase describing vast white clouds stretching far and wide.
    • 明月前溪后溪 - Poetic phrase describing the bright moon shining over both the front and back streams.
    • 独恨长沙谪去 - Poetic phrase expressing a deep regret about being exiled to Changsha.
    • 江潭春草萋萋 - Poetic phrase describing the lush spring grass by the river and pond.
    • 余在童幼亦闻长老话谪仙之事颇熟 - Reflective statement indicating that the speaker, while young, often heard older people talk about the story of the exiled immortal.
    • 而长卿之词甚是才丽 - Statement praising Liu Changqing's lyrics as very talented and beautiful.
    • 与本 - Phrase indicating that it is related to the original story or context.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 骆谷 - Place name, likely referring to a specific location in ancient China.
    • 长安 - The ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 长笛 - Type of flute, often used in classical Chinese music.
    • 谪仙 - Refers to a demoted immortal, often associated with the poet Li Bai (Li Po).
    • 马嵬 - Place name, likely referring to the site of the famous incident involving Yang Guifei and the Tang Dynasty.
    • 剑南 - Refers to the region south of the Sword Gate Pass, historically part of Sichuan Province.
    • 大历 - A period during the Tang Dynasty (766-779 AD).
    • 江南 - Refers to the southern part of the Yangtze River region, including modern-day Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces.
    • 随州 - Place name, likely referring to a city in Hubei Province.
    • 睦州 - Place name, likely referring to a city in Zhejiang Province.
    • 刘长卿 - Famous poet and official of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 长沙 - City in Hunan Province, often associated with exile in classical Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 骆谷 - Place name.
    • 长安 - The ancient capital of China, now known as Xi'an.
    • 长笛 - Type of flute.
    • 谪仙 - Refers to a demoted immortal.
    • 马嵬 - Place name.
    • 剑南 - Refers to the region south of the Sword Gate Pass.
    • 大历 - A period during the Tang Dynasty (766-779 AD).
    • 江南 - Refers to the southern part of the Yangtze River region.
    • 随州 - Place name.
    • 睦州 - Place name.
    • 刘长卿 - Famous poet and official of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 长沙 - City in Hunan Province.

    Looking at those around him, he said, "What kind of tune is there?" while looking at Chang'an from Luogu. After getting off his horse, he searched for a long flute and played it in response to something above. After a long while, he said, "I understand now." Reflecting, he thought about "Jiu Ling" and had a different intention to name the tune "Zhexian Yuan" (Exile of the Immortal), relating it to the incident at Ma嵬. After the incident, separation due to chaos and disorder occurred. Someone from Xichuan obtained the tune, but there is no way to know how, and it is simply called "Jiannan Shenqu" (Divine Tune of the Southern Sword). Its melody is extremely mournful and surpasses all other tunes in its emotional depth. During the Dali period, people in Jiangnan were very fond of this tune. The Magistrate of Suzhou, Liu Changqing, was demoted to be the Magistrate of Muzhou and played the tune during a farewell banquet. Liu Changqing then composed the lyrics for the tune, feeling quite pleased with himself, and he may not have known the original story behind the tune. The lyrics are as follows:

    A clear river, the setting sun just beginning to dip low,
    Sadness fills the heart as a lone boat departs.
    Birds fly away over distant and nearby fields,
    People follow the flow of water east and west.
    White clouds stretch far and wide,
    The bright moon shines over both the front and back streams.
    Alone, I regret being exiled to Changsha,
    The lush spring grass by the river and pond.
    When I was young, I often heard older people talk about the story of the exiled immortal,
    And Liu Changqing's lyrics are very talented and beautiful,
    Related to the original story. [74]

    事意兴不同余既备知聊因暇日辄撰其词复命乐工唱之用广不知者其词曰胡尘犯阙冲关金辂提携玉颜云雨此时消散君王何日归还伤心朝恨暮恨廻首千山万山独望天边初月蛾眉犹在弯弯軿以为窦史君序谪仙怨云刘随州之词未知本事及详其意但以贵妃为懐葢明皇登骆谷之时实有思贤之意窦之所制殊不述焉骈因更广其词葢欲两全其事虽才情浅拙不逮二公而理或可观贻诸识者词云晴山碍日横天绿叠君王马前銮辂西廵蜀国龙颜东望秦川曲江魂断芳草妃子愁凝暮烟长笛此时吹罢何言独为婵娟
    　　含元殿
    　　含元殿国初建造凿龙首岗以为基址彤墀扣砌髙五十余尺左右立栖凤翔鸾二阙龙尾道出于阙前倚栏下瞰前山如在诸掌殿去五门二里每元朔朝防禁军与御仗宿于殿庭金甲葆戈杂以绮绣罗列文武缨佩序立蕃夷酋长仰观玉座在霄汉识者以为自姬汉之代迄于亡隋未有如斯之盛京城自朱泚之乱逮干符中近百年无事君臣和叶四表靖谧文物之盛笼罩姬汉蕃 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 事意兴不同 - Suggests that the events or ideas are diverse or varied, but the exact meaning depends on the context. It could be translated as 'the events and ideas are different' or 'the circumstances vary.'
    • 余既备知 - Means 'I have thoroughly understood' or 'I have fully grasped.'
    • 聊因暇日辄撰其词 - '聊因暇日' means 'taking advantage of my leisure time,' and '辄撰其词' means 'I hastily composed its words.' Together, it suggests the author composed the text during spare time.
    • 复命乐工唱之 - '复命' means 'to order' or 'to command,' and '乐工唱之' means 'the musicians sing it.' This phrase indicates that the author had the text performed by musicians.
    • 用广不知者 - '用广' means 'to widely disseminate,' and '不知者' means 'those who do not know.' Together, it suggests the purpose of the text is to inform those who are unaware.
    • 其词曰 - A formal way of introducing the text that follows, meaning 'the text says' or 'the words are as follows.'
    • 胡尘犯阙冲关 - '胡尘' refers to barbarian invaders, '犯阙' means 'to attack the palace,' and '冲关' means 'to rush through the gates.' This phrase describes a barbarian invasion.
    • 金辂提携玉颜 - '金辂' refers to a golden carriage, and '提携玉颜' means 'to carry the beautiful face.' This phrase likely describes a scene of a noble person being carried in a carriage.
    • 云雨此时消散 - '云雨' is a metaphor for sexual union, and '消散' means 'to dissipate.' This phrase suggests the end of a romantic or intimate moment.
    • 君王何日归还 - '君王' means 'the king,' and '何日归还' means 'when will he return.' This phrase expresses the longing for the king's return.
    • 伤心朝恨暮恨 - '伤心' means 'heartbroken,' and '朝恨暮恨' means 'hate in the morning and hate in the evening.' This phrase describes constant sorrow and resentment.
    • 回首千山万山 - '回首' means 'to look back,' and '千山万山' means 'thousands of mountains.' This phrase suggests looking back at a vast, distant landscape.
    • 独望天边初月 - '独望' means 'to gaze alone,' and '天边初月' means 'the first moon at the edge of the sky.' This phrase describes gazing at the first moon in the sky.
    • 蛾眉犹在弯弯 - '蛾眉' refers to beautiful eyebrows, and '犹在弯弯' means 'still curved.' This phrase describes the beauty of the eyebrows.
    • 軿以为窦史君序谪仙怨 - '軿' refers to a type of carriage, '以为' means 'to regard as,' and '窦史君序谪仙怨' refers to a specific text or sequence. This phrase is difficult to translate without more context.
    • 刘随州之词未知本事 - '刘随州' is a proper name, and '未知本事' means 'the true story is unknown.' This phrase suggests that the true story behind the poem is unknown.
    • 但以贵妃为怀 - '但以' means 'only because,' and '贵妃为怀' means 'thinking of the imperial concubine.' This phrase suggests that the poem is only about the imperial concubine.
    • 盖明皇登骆谷之时实有思贤之意 - '盖' means 'it is said that,' '明皇' refers to Emperor Xuanzong, '登骆谷' means 'ascended to Lao Valley,' and '实有思贤之意' means 'truly had thoughts of virtuous people.' This phrase suggests that the emperor had thoughts of virtuous people when he ascended to Lao Valley.
    • 窦之所制殊不述焉 - '窦之所制' refers to the work of Duan, and '殊不述焉' means 'was not described.' This phrase suggests that Duan's work was not described.
    • 骈因更广其词 - '骈' refers to a type of literary style, '因更广其词' means 'therefore expanded its words.' This phrase suggests that the author expanded the text.
    • 盖欲两全其事 - '盖欲' means 'intended to,' and '两全其事' means 'to achieve both.' This phrase suggests that the author intended to achieve both things.
    • 虽才情浅拙不逮二公 - '虽' means 'although,' '才情浅拙' means 'talent and emotion are shallow and clumsy,' and '不逮二公' means 'do not match the two gentlemen.' This phrase suggests that the author's talent and emotion are not as good as those of the two gentlemen.
    • 而理或可观 - '而' means 'but,' '理或可观' means 'the reasoning may be worth seeing.' This phrase suggests that the reasoning may be worth seeing.
    • 贻诸识者 - '贻' means 'to give,' and '诸识者' means 'to those who understand.' This phrase suggests that the text is given to those who understand.
    • 词云 - A formal way of introducing the text that follows, meaning 'the words say' or 'the text is as follows.'
    • 晴山碍日横天 - '晴山' means 'clear mountain,' '碍日' means 'obstructs the sun,' and '横天' means 'across the sky.' This phrase describes a clear mountain obstructing the sun across the sky.
    • 绿叠君王马前 - '绿叠' means 'green layers,' and '君王马前' means 'before the king's horse.' This phrase describes green layers before the king's horse.
    • 銮辂西巡蜀国 - '銮辂' refers to a golden carriage, '西巡' means 'westward tour,' and '蜀国' means 'Shu State.' This phrase describes the king's westward tour to Shu State.
    • 龙颜东望秦川 - '龙颜' refers to the king's face, '东望' means 'looking east,' and '秦川' means 'Qin Plain.' This phrase describes the king looking east at the Qin Plain.
    • 曲江魂断芳草 - '曲江' refers to Qujiang, '魂断' means 'soul broken,' and '芳草' means 'fragrant grass.' This phrase describes the soul being broken at Qujiang with fragrant grass.
    • 妃子愁凝暮烟 - '妃子' refers to the imperial concubine, '愁凝' means 'sorrowful and fixed,' and '暮烟' means 'evening smoke.' This phrase describes the imperial concubine's sorrowful and fixed gaze at the evening smoke.
    • 长笛此时吹罢 - '长笛' means 'long flute,' and '吹罢' means 'blown out.' This phrase describes the long flute being blown out.
    • 何言独为婵娟 - '何言' means 'what words,' and '独为婵娟' means 'only for the beautiful.' This phrase suggests that the words are only for the beautiful.
    • 含元殿 - A proper noun referring to a specific building.
    • 国初建造 - '国初' means 'at the beginning of the state,' and '建造' means 'built.' This phrase describes the construction of the building at the beginning of the state.
    • 凿龙首岗以为基址 - '凿' means 'to excavate,' '龙首岗' means 'Dragon Head Hill,' and '以为基址' means 'as the foundation.' This phrase describes excavating Dragon Head Hill as the foundation.
    • 彤墀扣砌高五十余尺 - '彤墀' refers to a red terrace, '扣砌' means 'built,' and '高五十余尺' means 'over fifty feet high.' This phrase describes the red terrace being built over fifty feet high.
    • 左右立栖凤翔鸾二阙 - '左右' means 'on both sides,' '立' means 'stand,' '栖凤翔鸾' means 'perched phoenix and flying crane,' and '二阙' means 'two gates.' This phrase describes two gates with perched phoenixes and flying cranes on both sides.
    • 龙尾道出于阙前 - '龙尾道' refers to a dragon-tail road, '出于' means 'emerging from,' and '阙前' means 'in front of the gate.' This phrase describes the dragon-tail road emerging in front of the gate.
    • 倚栏下瞰前山如在诸掌 - '倚栏' means 'leaning on the railing,' '下瞰' means 'looking down,' '前山' means 'front mountain,' and '如在诸掌' means 'like in the palms of hands.' This phrase describes looking down at the front mountain as if it were in the palms of hands.
    • 殿去五门二里 - '殿' means 'palace,' '去' means 'distance,' and '五门二里' means 'two li from five gates.' This phrase describes the distance between the palace and the five gates.

    The Events and Feelings Vary; I Have Fully Grasped Them. Taking Advantage of My Leisure Time, I Hastily Composed Its Words and Ordered the Musicians to Sing It, So as to Widely Disseminate the Knowledge to Those Who Do Not Know. The Text Says:

    Barbarian dust invades the palace gates and rushes through the barriers. A golden carriage carries the beautiful face, but the moment of cloud and rain dissipates. When will the king return? Heartbroken, morning and evening, one looks back at thousands of mountains, gazing alone at the first moon at the edge of the sky, with beautiful eyebrows still curved.

    Regarding the "Zhexianyuan" (Exile's Lament) by Duan, the official of Sui Prefecture, he regards it as a sequence of the poem "Zhexianyuan." It is said that Liu Shizhou's poem is unknown in its true story, but in detail, it only thinks of the imperial concubine. It is said that Emperor Xuanzong ascended to Lao Valley, truly having thoughts of virtuous people. Duan's work was not described. Therefore, I expanded its words, intending to achieve both. Although my talent and emotion are shallow and clumsy, not matching the two gentlemen, the reasoning may be worth seeing. I give this to those who understand.

    The words say:

    Clear mountains obstruct the sun across the sky, with green layers before the king's horse. The golden carriage tours westward to Shu State, with the king's face looking east at the Qin Plain. At Qujiang, the soul is broken with fragrant grass, and the imperial concubine's sorrowful and fixed gaze is at the evening smoke. The long flute is blown out at this time. What words are only for the beautiful?

    Hanyuan Hall

    The Hanyuan Hall was built at the beginning of the state, excavating Dragon Head Hill as the foundation. The red terrace was built over fifty feet high, with two gates with perched phoenixes and flying cranes on both sides. The dragon-tail road emerges in front of the gate, and leaning on the railing, one looks down at the front mountain as if it were in the palms of hands. The hall is two li away from the five gates. Every New Year's Day, the imperial guards and the imperial entourage stay in the hall, with golden armor and ornate weapons mixed with brocade and embroidery, lined up with officials and generals, with foreign chieftains looking up at the jade throne, as if it were in the heavens. It is said that since the time of the Zhou and Han dynasties, there has never been such grandeur as the fall of the Sui dynasty. The capital city, since the chaos of Zhu Ci, has had no disturbances for nearly a hundred years, with harmonious relations between the emperor and his ministers, and peace and quietness in all directions, with the cultural prosperity of the Zhou and Han dynasties overshadowing the barbarians. [75]

    方职贡府无虚月上至士君子下及庶民皆修饬廉谨以邀时誉食禄者守其官耕贾者专其业八纮四海遂同文轨承平既久稍务奢逸贵族豪家轻视稼穑征镇牧守或非其才黔兴杼轴之嗟郡邑有萑蒲之盗然主上劳谦端委无圣政亦使冦犯神州銮辂播越况秦汉之代魏晋之时主荒臣残岂不顚覆今则覩淳辉之列啓中兴之期亿兆人心复新于唐德矣礼乐刑政得无诫哉
    　　曲江
    　　曲江池本秦世隑洲开元中疏凿遂为胜境其南有紫云楼芙蓉苑其西有杏园慈恩寺花卉环周烟水明媚都人游翫盛于中和上巳之节防幄翠帱匝于堤岸鲜车健马比肩击毂上巳即赐宴臣僚京兆府大陈筵席长安万年两县以雄盛相较锦绣珍玩无所不施百辟防于山亭恩赐太常及敎坊声乐池中备防舟数只唯宰相三使北省官与翰林学士登焉每岁倾动皇州以为盛观入夏则菰蒲葱翠柳隂四合碧波红蕖湛然可爱好事者赏芳辰翫清景聨骑携觞亹亹不絶 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 方职贡府无虚月上 - The tribute office is active every month or never idle.
    • 修饬廉谨 - To be diligent, clean, and cautious.
    • 邀时誉 - To seek the approval of the times.
    • 食禄者守其官 - Those who receive a salary should stay in their positions.
    • 耕贾者专其业 - Farmers and merchants should focus on their respective trades.
    • 八纮四海遂同文轨 - The entire world has unified under a single system of writing and laws.
    • 承平既久 - A long period of peace and prosperity.
    • 稍务奢逸 - Gradually becoming more luxurious and indulgent.
    • 贵族豪家轻视稼穑 - The nobility and wealthy families look down on farming.
    • 征镇牧守或非其才 - Military commanders and local governors may not be competent.
    • 黔兴杼轴之嗟 - There are complaints about the hardships of labor.
    • 郡邑有萑蒲之盗 - There are bandits in the counties and towns.
    • 主上劳谦端委 - The emperor is diligent, modest, and upright.
    • 无圣政 - There are no extraordinary policies.
    • 使冦犯神州 - Allowing enemies to invade the land.
    • 銮辂播越 - The imperial carriage is scattered and lost.
    • 秦汉之代魏晋之时 - Refers to the periods of the Qin, Han, Wei, and Jin dynasties.
    • 主荒臣残 - The ruler is neglectful and the officials are corrupt.
    • 岂不顚覆 - How could they not collapse?
    • 覩淳辉之列 - To see the list of virtuous people.
    • 啓中兴之期 - To initiate a period of revival.
    • 亿兆人心复新于唐德矣 - The hearts of millions of people have been renewed with the virtues of the Tang dynasty.
    • 礼乐刑政得无诫哉 - The rites, music, punishment, and governance should not be neglected.
    • 曲江池本秦世隑洲 - The Qujiang Pool was originally an island in the Qin dynasty.
    • 开元中疏凿遂为胜境 - During the Kaiyuan era, it was dredged and became a beautiful place.
    • 其南有紫云楼芙蓉苑 - To the south there are the Ziyun Tower and the Furong Garden.
    • 其西有杏园慈恩寺 - To the west there are the Xingyuan Garden and the Cien Temple.
    • 花卉环周烟水明媚 - Flowers and plants surround it, and the scenery is beautiful.
    • 都人游翫盛于中和上巳之节 - People from the capital enjoy it especially during the Zhonghe and Shangsi festivals.
    • 防幄翠帱匝于堤岸 - Green tents and curtains line the embankments.
    • 鲜车健马比肩击毂 - Colorful carriages and strong horses are lined up closely.
    • 上巳即赐宴臣僚 - During the Shangsi festival, the emperor grants a banquet to his ministers.
    • 京兆府大陈筵席 - The Jingzhao Prefecture holds grand banquets.
    • 长安万年两县以雄盛相较 - The counties of Chang'an and Wanyuan compete in their splendor.
    • 锦绣珍玩无所不施 - They display all kinds of fine brocades and precious objects.
    • 百辟防于山亭 - Hundreds of officials gather at the mountain pavilion.
    • 恩赐太常及敎坊声乐 - They are granted performances by the Taichang and Jiaofang orchestras.
    • 池中备防舟数只 - There are several boats in the pool.
    • 唯宰相三使北省官与翰林学士登焉 - Only the prime minister, three envoys, officials from the northern provinces, and scholars from the Hanlin Academy get to board them.
    • 每岁倾动皇州以为盛观 - Every year it stirs the capital and is seen as a grand spectacle.
    • 入夏则菰蒲葱翠柳隂四合 - In summer, the reeds and grass turn green, and willow trees shade the area.
    • 碧波红蕖湛然可爱 - The blue waves and red lotuses are beautiful and inviting.
    • 好事者赏芳辰翫清景 - Those who appreciate beauty enjoy the fragrant days and clear scenes.
    • 联骑携觞亹亹不絶 - People ride together, carrying wine, and the activity never stops.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 方职贡府 - Refers to the office responsible for collecting tributes.
    • 中和上巳之节 - Refers to the Zhonghe and Shangsi festivals, traditional Chinese festivals.
    • 京兆府 - Refers to the Jingzhao Prefecture, an administrative division in ancient China.
    • 长安万年两县 - Refers to the counties of Chang'an and Wanyuan, both in the capital region.
    • 太常及敎坊 - Refers to the Taichang and Jiaofang, institutions responsible for music and dance performances.
    • 曲江池 - Refers to the Qujiang Pool, a famous scenic spot in Chang'an.
    • 开元 - Refers to the Kaiyuan era, a period of prosperity during the Tang dynasty.
    • 紫云楼芙蓉苑 - Refers to the Ziyun Tower and the Furong Garden, scenic spots in Qujiang Pool.
    • 杏园慈恩寺 - Refers to the Xingyuan Garden and the Cien Temple, also scenic spots in Qujiang Pool.
    • 鲜车健马 - Refers to colorful carriages and strong horses, symbols of wealth and status.
    • 上巳 - Refers to the Shangsi festival, a traditional Chinese festival.
    • 恩赐 - Refers to imperial grants or favors.

    Throughout the empire, from the noblest scholars to the common people, everyone strives to be diligent, clean, and cautious in order to gain the approval of the times. Those who receive a salary remain in their positions, while farmers and merchants focus on their respective trades. The entire world has unified under a single system of writing and laws. After a long period of peace and prosperity, people gradually become more luxurious and indulgent. The nobility and wealthy families look down on farming, and military commanders and local governors may not be competent. There are complaints about the hardships of labor, and bandits appear in the counties and towns. However, the emperor is diligent, modest, and upright, with no extraordinary policies, and this allows enemies to invade the land. The imperial carriage is scattered and lost. During the periods of the Qin, Han, Wei, and Jin dynasties, when rulers were neglectful and officials were corrupt, how could they not collapse? Now, we see the list of virtuous people and initiate a period of revival. The hearts of millions of people have been renewed with the virtues of the Tang dynasty. The rites, music, punishment, and governance should not be neglected.

    The Qujiang Pool was originally an island in the Qin dynasty. During the Kaiyuan era, it was dredged and became a beautiful place. To the south there are the Ziyun Tower and the Furong Garden, and to the west there are the Xingyuan Garden and the Cien Temple. Flowers and plants surround it, and the scenery is beautiful. People from the capital enjoy it especially during the Zhonghe and Shangsi festivals. Green tents and curtains line the embankments, and colorful carriages and strong horses are lined up closely. During the Shangsi festival, the emperor grants a banquet to his ministers. The Jingzhao Prefecture holds grand banquets, and the counties of Chang'an and Wanyuan compete in their splendor. They display all kinds of fine brocades and precious objects. Hundreds of officials gather at the mountain pavilion, and they are granted performances by the Taichang and Jiaofang orchestras. There are several boats in the pool, and only the prime minister, three envoys, officials from the northern provinces, and scholars from the Hanlin Academy get to board them. Every year it stirs the capital and is seen as a grand spectacle. In summer, the reeds and grass turn green, and willow trees shade the area. The blue waves and red lotuses are beautiful and inviting. Those who appreciate beauty enjoy the fragrant days and clear scenes, and people ride together, carrying wine, and the activity never stops. [76]

    升平裴相国廉察宣城朝谢后未离京国时曲江荷花盛发与省阁名士数人同游自慈恩寺屏去左右各领小仆歩至紫云楼下见五六人坐于水际裴公与名士憩于旁中有黄衣饮酒半酣轩昻颇甚指顾笑语轻脱裴意稍不平揖而问之吾贤所任何官率尔而对曰喏即不敢新授宣州广德县令连问裴曰押衙所任何职裴公效曰喏即不敢新授宣州观察使于是狼狈而走同坐亦皆奔散朝士抚掌大笑不数日布于京华左右于铨司访之云有广德县令请换罗江宰矣宣皇在宫即闻是説与诸王每为戏谈其事及龙飞裴公入秉钧轴因书麻制廻谓枢近曰喏即不敢新授中书侍郎平章事 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 升平裴相国 - Refers to Pei Shao, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 廉察宣城 - Means 'to inspect and govern Xuancheng,' indicating Pei's role as an inspector-governor.
    • 朝谢后未离京国 - Means 'after paying his respects in the morning but before leaving the capital,' describing Pei's state after a formal visit.
    • 曲江荷花盛发 - Describes the flourishing lotus flowers in Qujiang, a scenic area near Chang'an (modern Xi'an).
    • 省阁名士数人 - Refers to several prominent scholars and officials in the imperial administration.
    • 慈恩寺 - The Great Wild Goose Pagoda Temple, a famous Buddhist temple in Chang'an.
    • 屏去左右 - Means 'to dismiss the attendants,' indicating a private meeting.
    • 各领小仆步至紫云楼下 - Means 'each leading a small servant on foot to the foot of the Ziyun Pavilion,' describing their journey.
    • 见五六人坐于水际 - Describes seeing five or six people sitting by the water.
    • 黄衣饮酒半酣轩昻颇甚 - Describes a person in yellow clothing who is half-drunk and very proud.
    • 指顾笑语轻脱 - Means 'pointing and laughing with a carefree demeanor.'
    • 意稍不平 - Means 'feeling slightly annoyed.'
    • 揖而问之 - Means 'bowing and asking them.'
    • 吾贤所任何官 - Means 'what official position do you hold, my esteemed fellow?'
    • 率尔而对曰喏即不敢 - Means 'they replied hastily, 'I dare not, sir.''
    • 新授宣州广德县令 - Means 'recently appointed as the county magistrate of Guangde in Xuancheng Prefecture.'
    • 押衙所任何职 - Means 'what position do you hold, sir?'
    • 喏即不敢新授宣州观察使 - Means 'I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the governor of Xuancheng Prefecture.'
    • 狼狈而走 - Means 'they fled in a panic.'
    • 同坐亦皆奔散 - Means 'the others also scattered in panic.'
    • 朝士抚掌大笑 - Means 'the court officials clapped and laughed.'
    • 不数日布于京华 - Means 'within a few days, the story spread throughout the capital.'
    • 左右于铨司访之 - Means 'the officials at the personnel bureau investigated.'
    • 有广德县令请换罗江宰矣 - Means 'there was a request from the county magistrate of Guangde to be transferred to Luojiang.'
    • 宣皇在宫即闻是説 - Means 'Emperor Xuan heard about this while he was still a prince.'
    • 与诸王每为戏谈其事 - Means 'he often joked about this with his brothers.'
    • 龙飞 - A metaphor for ascending to the throne.
    • 入秉钧轴 - Means 'to take up the reins of power.'
    • 书麻制廻 - Refers to the imperial edict.
    • 喏即不敢新授中书侍郎平章事 - Means 'I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the Vice-Chancellor and Chief Councillor.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 升平裴相国 - Pei Shao, a prominent official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 宣城 - Xuancheng Prefecture, a region in modern Anhui Province.
    • 曲江 - Qujiang, a scenic area in Chang'an known for its lotus flowers.
    • 慈恩寺 - The Great Wild Goose Pagoda Temple, a significant Buddhist temple in Chang'an.
    • 省阁 - Refers to the imperial administration.
    • 黄衣 - Yellow clothing, often worn by officials.
    • 宣州 - Xuancheng Prefecture, where Pei Shao was later appointed as governor.
    • 广德县 - Guangde County, a county within Xuancheng Prefecture.
    • 罗江 - Luojiang, another county in the region.
    • 宣皇 - Emperor Xuan of Tang, who later became the emperor.
    • 中书侍郎平章事 - Vice-Chancellor and Chief Councillor, high-ranking positions in the imperial government.

    Proper Nouns

    • 升平裴相国 - Pei Shao
    • 宣城 - Xuancheng Prefecture
    • 曲江 - Qujiang
    • 慈恩寺 - The Great Wild Goose Pagoda Temple
    • 宣州 - Xuancheng Prefecture
    • 广德县 - Guangde County
    • 罗江 - Luojiang
    • 宣皇 - Emperor Xuan of Tang
    • 中书侍郎平章事 - Vice-Chancellor and Chief Councillor

    During the reign of Emperor Xuan, Pei Shao, the Inspector-Governor of Xuancheng, had just paid his respects in the morning and was about to leave the capital when the lotus flowers in Qujiang were in full bloom. He went for a stroll with several prominent scholars and officials from the imperial administration. They walked from the Great Wild Goose Pagoda Temple, dismissing their attendants and each leading a small servant, to the foot of the Ziyun Pavilion. There, they saw five or six people sitting by the water. Among them, a man in yellow clothing, half-drunk and very proud, was pointing and laughing with a carefree demeanor. Pei Shao felt slightly annoyed and bowed to ask them, "What official position do you hold, my esteemed fellow?" They replied hastily, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the county magistrate of Guangde in Xuancheng Prefecture." Pei Shao then asked, "What position do you hold, sir?" Pei Shao mimicked their response, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the governor of Xuancheng Prefecture." The man in yellow clothing and his companions fled in a panic, and the others also scattered in panic. The court officials clapped and laughed. Within a few days, the story spread throughout the capital. The officials at the personnel bureau investigated and found that there was a request from the county magistrate of Guangde to be transferred to Luojiang. Emperor Xuan, while still a prince, had heard about this and often joked about it with his brothers. When he ascended the throne, Pei Shao took up the reins of power. He then issued an imperial edict and said to the ministers, "I dare not, sir, I have just been appointed as the Vice-Chancellor and Chief Councillor." [77]

    眞身
    　　咸通十四年诏自凤翔迎眞身至于辇下【眞身相传云是释迦文佛中指节骨长一寸八分莹浄如玉以小金棺盛之旧于凤翔建塔又释氏湼槃经云如来于防林灭度贮于金棺银椁积防檀香焚之诸天以八金刚分取舍利唯留四牙余悉煨烬未详此骨从何而有】都城士庶奔走云集自开逺门达于岐川车马昼夜相属饮盈溢路衢谓之无碍檀施【京城坊曲旧有迎眞身社居人长幼旬出一钱自开成之后迄于咸通计其资积无限于是广为费用时物之价髙茶米载以大车往往至于百两他物丰盈悉皆 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 眞身 - Refers to the physical remains or relics of a revered figure, often used in a religious or spiritual context.
    • 咸通十四年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (873 AD), indicating a historical timeframe.
    • 自凤翔迎眞身至于辇下 - Literally 'from Fengxiang, escorting the true body to the imperial carriage,' a ritual act of transporting a sacred relic.
    • 莹浄如玉 - Describes something as 'clear and pure like jade,' emphasizing its precious and sacred nature.
    • 小金棺盛之 - Refers to a small golden coffin containing the relic, a common practice for preserving sacred objects.
    • 建塔 - Means 'to build a pagoda,' a structure often erected to house relics.
    • 释氏湼槃经 - Refers to the Buddhist *Nirvana Sutra*, a canonical text.
    • 如来于防林灭度 - Refers to the Buddha's passing (nirvana) in the *Jeta Grove*.
    • 贮于金棺银椁 - Means 'stored in a golden coffin and silver casket,' describing the burial of the Buddha.
    • 积防檀香焚之 - Means 'accumulated sandalwood to burn,' referring to the cremation of the Buddha's body.
    • 诸天以八金刚分取舍利 - Means 'the gods divided the relics with eight diamond-like beings,' a reference to the distribution of the Buddha's remains.
    • 唯留四牙 - Means 'only four teeth were left,' a specific detail about the Buddha's relics.
    • 余悉煨烬 - Means 'the rest were burned to ashes,' indicating the destruction of most of the remains.
    • 未详此骨从何而有 - Means 'it is unclear where this bone came from,' expressing uncertainty about the origin of the relic.
    • 都城士庶奔走云集 - Means 'the people of the capital city rushed and gathered,' describing a large crowd.
    • 自开逺门达于岐川 - Means 'from the Kaiyuan Gate to the Qichuan River,' indicating a specific route.
    • 车马昼夜相属 - Means 'carriages and horses came day and night,' describing a continuous flow of traffic.
    • 饮盈溢路衢 - Means 'drinking and revelry filled the streets,' indicating a festive atmosphere.
    • 谓之无碍檀施 - Means 'called the unrestricted giving of alms,' describing a form of charitable giving.
    • 迎眞身社 - Refers to a society or group dedicated to welcoming the true body (relic).
    • 居人长幼旬出一钱 - Means 'the residents, young and old, contributed a coin every ten days,' describing a regular donation.
    • 开成之后迄于咸通 - Means 'from the Kai Cheng period to the Xian Tong period,' indicating a specific historical timeframe.
    • 计其资积无限 - Means 'calculating the accumulated funds, it was infinite,' emphasizing the vast amount of resources.
    • 广为费用 - Means 'spent widely,' describing the use of the funds.
    • 时物之价高茶米 - Means 'the prices of goods, especially tea and rice, were high,' indicating economic conditions.
    • 往往至于百两 - Means 'often reaching a hundred loads,' describing the quantity of goods.
    • 他物丰盈 - Means 'other goods were abundant,' indicating a general state of plenty.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸通十四年 - A specific year in the Tang Dynasty, indicating a historical context.
    • 凤翔 - A city in Shaanxi province, historically significant for its role in religious and political events.
    • 辇下 - Refers to the imperial city, specifically Chang'an (modern Xi'an), the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 释迦文佛 - Refers to the historical Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama.
    • 释氏湼槃经 - Refers to the Buddhist *Nirvana Sutra*, a canonical text.
    • 防林 - Refers to the *Jeta Grove* in India, where the Buddha passed away.
    • 诸天 - Refers to the gods or deities in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 八金刚 - Refers to eight divine beings in Buddhist tradition.
    • 舍利 - Refers to the relics of the Buddha, often in the form of bones or teeth.
    • 开逺门 - Refers to a specific gate in Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 岐川 - Refers to the Qichuan River, a geographical feature in the region.
    • 迎眞身社 - Refers to a religious or social group dedicated to welcoming and venerating relics.
    • 开成之后迄于咸通 - Refers to a specific historical period, indicating the timeframe of the events described.

    Proper Nouns

    • 咸通十四年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (873 AD).
    • 凤翔 - A city in Shaanxi province, historically significant for its role in religious and political events.
    • 辇下 - Refers to the imperial city, specifically Chang'an (modern Xi'an), the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 释迦文佛 - Refers to the historical Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama.
    • 释氏湼槃经 - Refers to the Buddhist *Nirvana Sutra*, a canonical text.
    • 防林 - Refers to the *Jeta Grove* in India, where the Buddha passed away.
    • 诸天 - Refers to the gods or deities in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 八金刚 - Refers to eight divine beings in Buddhist tradition.
    • 舍利 - Refers to the relics of the Buddha, often in the form of bones or teeth.
    • 开逺门 - Refers to a specific gate in Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 岐川 - Refers to the Qichuan River, a geographical feature in the region.
    • 迎眞身社 - Refers to a religious or social group dedicated to welcoming and venerating relics.
    • 开成之后迄于咸通 - Refers to a specific historical period, indicating the timeframe of the events described.

    Translation:

    The True Body
    In the fourteenth year of the Xiantong era, an edict was issued to escort the True Body from Fengxiang to the imperial city [The True Body is said to be the middle finger bone of the Historical Buddha, measuring one inch and eight fen in length, as clear and pure as jade, contained in a small golden coffin. A pagoda was formerly built in Fengxiang. The Nirvana Sutra of the Buddhist tradition states that the Buddha passed away in the Jeta Grove, and his body was stored in a golden coffin and silver casket, surrounded by sandalwood and burned. The gods divided the relics with eight diamond-like beings, leaving only four teeth, while the rest were burned to ashes. It is unclear where this bone came from]. The common people of the capital city rushed and gathered, from the Kaiyuan Gate to the Qichuan River, with carriages and horses coming day and night, and drinking and revelry filling the streets, which was called the unrestricted giving of alms [In the past, there was a society in the city dedicated to welcoming the True Body, with residents, young and old, contributing a coin every ten days. From the Kai Cheng period to the Xiantong period, calculating the accumulated funds, it was infinite, and thus widely spent. At that time, the prices of goods, especially tea and rice, were high, often reaching a hundred loads, and other goods were abundant]. [78]

    称是】至京日上与诸王亲御城楼坊市以缯防结为龙凤象马之形竹作僧佛神之状幡花幢葢之属罗列二十余里间之歌舞管杂以禁军兵仗缁徒梵诵之声沸聒天地民庶间有嬉笑踊跃者有悲怆涕泣者眞身以宝舁之居于内殿数月俄属懿皇猒代密使送于凤翔先是眞身到城每坊十字街以甎垒浮图供养妖妄之軰互陈感应或云夜中震动或云其上放光以求化资财因此获利者甚众及宫车晏驾帖然乃定诸坊浮图一时毁坼【有好事者密询放光之由云以大云母片窥看逺而望之靡不倾信 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 至京日 - Means 'on the day they arrived in the capital.'
    • 上与诸王亲御城楼 - Means 'the emperor and his princes personally ascended the city tower.'
    • 坊市以缯防结为龙凤象马之形 - Means 'in the streets and markets, silk and other materials were used to create shapes of dragons, phoenixes, elephants, and horses.'
    • 竹作僧佛鬼神之状 - Means 'bamboo was used to make figures of monks, Buddhas, ghosts, and gods.'
    • 幡花幢盖之属罗列二十余里 - Means 'flags, flowers, canopies, and other decorations were arranged for over twenty miles.'
    • 间之歌舞管弦杂以禁军兵仗缁徒梵诵之声沸聒天地 - Means 'interspersed with singing, dancing, music, and the sounds of guards, monks chanting, and drums, it filled the sky with noise.'
    • 民庶间有嬉笑踊跃者有悲怆涕泣者 - Means 'among the common people, some laughed and leaped for joy, while others wept with sorrow.'
    • 真身以宝车舁之居于内殿数月 - Means 'the sacred body was carried in a precious carriage and kept in the inner palace for several months.'
    • 俄属懿皇厌代密使送于凤翔 - Means 'soon after, Emperor Wenzong, tired of the imperial throne, secretly ordered it sent to Fengxiang.'
    • 先是真身到城每坊十字街以砖垒浮图供养妖妄之辈互陈感应 - Means 'before that, when the sacred body arrived in the city, at every cross street, people built small pagodas of bricks to worship, and various groups claimed to have had miraculous experiences.'
    • 或云夜中震动或云其上放光以求化资财因此获利者甚众 - Means 'some said it shook at night, others said it emitted light, all in order to seek donations and wealth, and many profited from this.'
    • 及宫车晏驾帖然乃定诸坊浮图一时毁坼 - Means 'when the emperor died, everything was immediately suppressed, and the pagodas in the streets were destroyed.'
    • 有好事者密询放光之由云以大云母片窥看远而望之靡不倾信 - Means 'some curious individuals secretly asked about the source of the light, and it was said that looking through a piece of cloudstone from afar would make anyone believe it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京 - Refers to the capital city, which in this context is likely Chang'an or Luoyang.
    • 上 - Refers to the emperor.
    • 诸王 - Refers to the emperor's princes.
    • 城楼 - Refers to a city tower, often used for public ceremonies.
    • 坊市 - Refers to the streets and markets of the city.
    • 缯防 - Refers to silk and other materials used for decoration.
    • 龙凤象马 - Refers to dragons, phoenixes, elephants, and horses, symbols of imperial power and prosperity.
    • 竹 - Refers to bamboo, a common material for making figures and decorations.
    • 僧佛鬼神 - Refers to monks, Buddhas, ghosts, and gods, reflecting the religious and superstitious practices of the time.
    • 幡花幢盖 - Refers to flags, flowers, canopies, and other decorative elements.
    • 禁军 - Refers to the imperial guards.
    • 缁徒 - Refers to monks, who wore dark robes (缁).
    • 梵诵 - Refers to Buddhist chanting.
    • 真身 - Refers to a sacred or holy body, often associated with religious relics.
    • 宝车 - Refers to a precious carriage, likely used for transporting important items.
    • 内殿 - Refers to the inner palace, where the emperor and his family lived.
    • 懿皇 - Refers to Emperor Wenzong, known for his love of poetry and music.
    • 凤翔 - Refers to Fengxiang, a city in Shaanxi province.
    • 宫车晏驾 - Refers to the death of the emperor.
    • 大云母片 - Refers to a piece of cloudstone, a type of mineral that was believed to have magical properties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 京 - The capital city.
    • 上 - The emperor.
    • 诸王 - The emperor's princes.
    • 城楼 - A city tower.
    • 坊市 - Streets and markets.
    • 缯防 - Silk and other materials.
    • 龙凤象马 - Dragons, phoenixes, elephants, and horses.
    • 竹 - Bamboo.
    • 僧佛鬼神 - Monks, Buddhas, ghosts, and gods.
    • 幡花幢盖 - Flags, flowers, canopies, and other decorations.
    • 禁军 - Imperial guards.
    • 缁徒 - Monks.
    • 梵诵 - Buddhist chanting.
    • 真身 - Sacred body.
    • 宝车 - Precious carriage.
    • 内殿 - Inner palace.
    • 懿皇 - Emperor Wenzong.
    • 凤翔 - Fengxiang.
    • 宫车晏驾 - Death of the emperor.
    • 大云母片 - Cloudstone.

    On the day they arrived in the capital, the emperor and his princes personally ascended the city tower. In the streets and markets, silk and other materials were used to create shapes of dragons, phoenixes, elephants, and horses, while bamboo was fashioned into figures of monks, Buddhas, ghosts, and gods. Flags, flowers, canopies, and other decorations were arranged for over twenty miles, interspersed with singing, dancing, music, and the sounds of guards, monks chanting, and drums, filling the sky with noise. Among the common people, some laughed and leaped for joy, while others wept with sorrow. The sacred body was carried in a precious carriage and kept in the inner palace for several months. Soon after, Emperor Wenzong, tired of the imperial throne, secretly ordered it sent to Fengxiang. Before that, when the sacred body arrived in the city, at every cross street, people built small pagodas of bricks to worship, and various groups claimed to have had miraculous experiences. Some said it shook at night, others said it emitted light, all in order to seek donations and wealth, and many profited from this. When the emperor died, everything was immediately suppressed, and the pagodas in the streets were destroyed. Some curious individuals secretly asked about the source of the light, and it was said that looking through a piece of cloudstone from afar would make anyone believe it. [79]

    耳】咸通干符中兴善寺复有阿阇黎以敎法传授都下翕然宗之所居院金碧华焕器用俱是宝玉语人云焚香结坐每告西方及迁化谥为普照大师信者咸为出涕刘都尉崔给事寓张常侍同与中贵多为弟子出城之日皆缟素后随劝朝士持斋受其法者不复思理时务骈常读名僧传宋文帝时有求那跋摩居金陵秪洹喜文帝谓之曰弟子常愿持斋不杀廹于以身徇物不获遂从法师不逺万里来化此国将何以敎化之对曰道在心不在事法由已不由人且帝王凡庶所修亦有殊矣若凡庶者身贱名微德不及逺 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 咸通干符中 - Refers to the reign periods of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Xizong (干符) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 兴善寺 - A famous Buddhist temple in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an).
    • 阿阇黎 - A Sanskrit term meaning 'teacher' or 'preceptor' in Buddhist tradition.
    • 敎法传授 - Refers to the teaching and transmission of Buddhist doctrines.
    • 都下翕然宗之 - Means 'the capital city was unified in following his teachings.'
    • 金碧华焕 - Describes the temple's golden and green decorations, indicating its splendor.
    • 器用俱是宝玉 - Means 'all the utensils were made of precious gems.'
    • 焚香结坐 - Refers to the practice of burning incense and sitting in meditation.
    • 西方 - Refers to the Western Paradise (Sukhavati) in Buddhist tradition.
    • 迁化 - Means 'to pass away' or 'to attain Nirvana.'
    • 谥为普照大师 - Means 'posthumously honored as the Venerable Universal Illumination.'
    • 信者咸为出涕 - Means 'believers all shed tears.'
    • 刘都尉崔给事寓张常侍同与中贵多为弟子 - Refers to several high-ranking officials and court nobles who were disciples of the master.
    • 出城之日皆缟素 - Means 'on the day of his departure, everyone wore white mourning clothes.'
    • 后随劝朝士持斋受其法者不复思理时务 - Means 'those who followed him encouraged officials to observe fasting and receive his teachings, causing them to neglect state affairs.'
    • 骈常读名僧传 - Means 'Pian often read the biographies of famous monks.'
    • 宋文帝时有求那跋摩居金陵 - Refers to a monk named Gunavarman who lived in Jinling (modern-day Nanjing) during the reign of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 秪洹 - A term for a Buddhist monastery or temple.
    • 不杀 - Means 'non-violence' or 'abstinence from killing.'
    • 迫于以身徇物 - Means 'compelled by the demands of worldly life.'
    • 不获遂从 - Means 'unable to follow through.'
    • 法师 - Refers to a Buddhist teacher or monk.
    • 不远万里来化此国 - Means 'traveling thousands of miles to teach this country.'
    • 将何以教化之 - Means 'how will you teach them?'
    • 道在心不在事 - Means 'the Way lies in the heart, not in external actions.'
    • 法由己不由人 - Means 'the law depends on oneself, not on others.'
    • 帝王凡庶所修亦有殊矣 - Means 'the cultivation of emperors and commoners differs.'
    • 身贱名微德不及远 - Means 'of low status and fame, their virtue does not reach far.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 咸通干符中 - The reign periods of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Xizong (干符) of the Tang Dynasty, a time of cultural and religious flourishing.
    • 兴善寺 - A prominent Buddhist temple in Chang'an, known for its role in Buddhist transmission.
    • 阿阇黎 - A title for a Buddhist teacher or preceptor, indicating a high level of spiritual authority.
    • 敎法传授 - The practice of transmitting Buddhist teachings, which was central to the spread of Buddhism in China.
    • 金碧华焕 - Describes the opulence of the temple, reflecting the wealth and patronage of Buddhism during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 器用俱是宝玉 - Indicates the use of precious materials in Buddhist rituals, emphasizing the reverence for the religion.
    • 焚香结坐 - A common Buddhist practice of meditation and prayer, symbolizing spiritual discipline.
    • 西方 - Refers to the Western Paradise (Sukhavati), a central concept in Pure Land Buddhism.
    • 迁化 - The passing of a master, often associated with the attainment of Nirvana.
    • 谥为普照大师 - The posthumous title given to the master, indicating his spiritual achievements.
    • 信者咸为出涕 - A common expression of mourning and respect for a revered figure.
    • 刘都尉崔给事寓张常侍同与中贵多为弟子 - Refers to several high-ranking officials and court nobles who were disciples of the master, reflecting the influence of Buddhism among the elite.
    • 出城之日皆缟素 - A traditional Chinese mourning practice, indicating the respect and grief felt by the community.
    • 后随劝朝士持斋受其法者不复思理时务 - Describes the impact of the master's teachings on the officials, causing them to neglect their duties.
    • 骈常读名僧传 - Indicates a scholarly interest in the lives of famous monks, reflecting the cultural value placed on religious figures.
    • 宋文帝时有求那跋摩居金陵 - Refers to a historical figure, Gunavarman, who lived in Jinling during the Song Dynasty, highlighting the continuity of Buddhist tradition.
    • 秪洹 - A term for a Buddhist monastery, often associated with the life of the Buddha.
    • 不杀 - A core Buddhist precept of non-violence, central to the religion's ethical teachings.
    • 迫于以身徇物 - Reflects the tension between spiritual ideals and worldly demands, a common theme in Buddhist texts.
    • 不获遂从 - Indicates the difficulty of fully adhering to spiritual practices in a secular context.
    • 法师 - A title for a Buddhist teacher, indicating a high level of spiritual authority.
    • 不远万里来化此国 - Emphasizes the dedication of Buddhist teachers to spreading their teachings, often at great personal cost.
    • 将何以教化之 - A rhetorical question asking how one should teach others, reflecting the challenge of spiritual guidance.
    • 道在心不在事 - A key Buddhist teaching emphasizing inner cultivation over external actions.
    • 法由己不由人 - Another important teaching, stressing individual responsibility in following the Dharma.
    • 帝王凡庶所修亦有殊矣 - Reflects the Buddhist view that spiritual cultivation varies depending on one's social status.
    • 身贱名微德不及远 - Indicates the limitations of those of low status and fame, a common theme in Buddhist texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 咸通干符中 - The reign periods of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Xizong (干符) of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 兴善寺 - A famous Buddhist temple in Chang'an.
    • 阿阇黎 - A title for a Buddhist teacher or preceptor.
    • 刘都尉 - A high-ranking military official.
    • 崔给事 - A high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.
    • 寓张常侍 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 中贵 - Refers to court nobles or high-ranking officials.
    • 普照大师 - The posthumous title given to the master.
    • 骈 - A scholar or official who read the biographies of famous monks.
    • 宋文帝 - Emperor Wen of Song, a ruler of the Southern Dynasties.
    • 求那跋摩 - A historical monk who lived in Jinling during the Song Dynasty.

    In the reigns of Emperor Xianzong (咸通) and Emperor Xizong (干符) of the Tang Dynasty, the Xing'an Temple in Chang'an once again had an Arhat who transmitted Buddhist teachings. The capital city was unified in following his teachings. His residence was adorned with golden and green decorations, and all the utensils were made of precious gems. He told others, "Burning incense and sitting in meditation, I often speak of the Western Paradise and the attainment of Nirvana." After his passing, he was posthumously honored as the Venerable Universal Illumination. Believers all shed tears. Liu the Commandant, Cui the Attendant, Yu the Attendant, and other high-ranking officials and court nobles were his disciples. On the day of his departure, everyone wore white mourning clothes. Those who followed him encouraged officials to observe fasting and receive his teachings, causing them to neglect state affairs. Pian often read the biographies of famous monks. During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, there was a monk named Gunavarman who lived in Jinling. At the monastery, Emperor Wen said to him, "I often wish to observe fasting and abstain from killing, but I am compelled by the demands of worldly life and cannot fully follow through. The teacher traveled thousands of miles to teach this country. How will you teach them?" The monk replied, "The Way lies in the heart, not in external actions. The law depends on oneself, not on others. The cultivation of emperors and commoners differs. For commoners, of low status and fame, their virtue does not reach far." [80]

    其敎不出于闺门其言不行于仆妾若不克己苦躬行善持戒将何以用心哉帝王以四海为家万民为子出嘉言则士庶咸悦布一善则人神以和刑清不夭其命役简不劳其力辨钟律定时令钟律辨则风雨调号令时则寒暑节知百姓之饥斯所以就于无饥知百姓之寒斯所以就于无寒如此持斋亦大矣不杀则众矣安在于阙一时之膳全一禽之命然后乃济也文帝抚几嗟叹称善良久乃曰俗人迷于逺理沙门滞于近敎迷逺理者谓至道虚説滞近敎者则拘挛章句如公者眞所开悟明达可以言天人之际矣 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 其敎不出于闺门 - Implies that his teachings are not confined to private or domestic settings.
    • 其言不行于仆妾 - Suggests that his teachings are not effective in influencing those in lower social positions.
    • 若不克己苦躬行善持戒 - Refers to self-discipline and moral conduct.
    • 将何以用心哉 - Implies a lack of genuine commitment or sincerity.
    • 帝王以四海为家万民为子 - Emphasizes the emperor's responsibility to all subjects.
    • 出嘉言则士庶咸悦 - Highlights the impact of wise and benevolent speech.
    • 布一善则人神以和 - Emphasizes the importance of virtuous actions.
    • 刑清不夭其命 - Suggests that just and clear laws prevent premature death.
    • 役简不劳其力 - Implies that reasonable workloads preserve the well-being of the people.
    • 辨钟律定时令 - Refers to the regulation of rituals and the calendar.
    • 钟律辨则风雨调 - Suggests that proper rituals lead to natural harmony.
    • 号令时则寒暑节 - Implies that proper governance leads to natural order.
    • 知百姓之饥斯所以就于无饥 - Emphasizes the importance of empathy and action in addressing needs.
    • 知百姓之寒斯所以就于无寒 - Similar to the previous phrase, emphasizing the importance of addressing the people's needs.
    • 如此持斋亦大矣 - Suggests that true fasting involves more than just abstaining from food.
    • 不杀则众矣 - Implies that non-violence is a significant virtue.
    • 安在于阙一时之膳全一禽之命然后乃弘济也 - Questions the value of superficial acts of saving one bird's life by missing one meal.
    • 文帝抚几嗟叹称善良久乃曰 - Indicates a moment of reflection and praise.
    • 俗人迷于远理 - Suggests a lack of understanding of deeper truths.
    • 沙门滞于近敎 - Implies a focus on superficial aspects of religion.
    • 迷远理者谓至道虚説 - Suggests a dismissal of profound spiritual teachings.
    • 滞近敎者则拘挛章句 - Implies a rigid adherence to superficial religious texts.
    • 如公者眞所开悟明达可以言天人之际矣 - Praises the individual for their wisdom and insight.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝王 - Refers to emperors or rulers, emphasizing their role in governance and moral leadership.
    • 四海 - Refers to the entire world or empire, symbolizing the vastness of the emperor's domain.
    • 万民 - Refers to the common people, emphasizing the emperor's responsibility to all subjects.
    • 嘉言 - Refers to wise and benevolent words, often associated with moral and ethical teachings.
    • 士庶 - Refers to scholars and commoners, representing different social classes.
    • 布一善 - Refers to spreading a good deed, emphasizing the importance of virtuous actions.
    • 人神 - Refers to both people and gods, emphasizing the interconnectedness of human and divine realms.
    • 刑清 - Refers to clear and just laws, emphasizing the importance of legal fairness.
    • 役简 - Refers to light and reasonable labor, emphasizing the importance of not overburdening the people.
    • 钟律 - Refers to musical tones and rituals, emphasizing the importance of proper ceremonies and traditions.
    • 定时令 - Refers to setting the seasons, emphasizing the importance of agricultural and calendrical practices.
    • 风雨调 - Refers to the harmony of wind and rain, symbolizing natural balance.
    • 号令时 - Refers to timely commands, emphasizing the importance of proper governance.
    • 寒暑节 - Refers to the regulation of cold and heat, symbolizing natural order.
    • 持斋 - Refers to fasting, often associated with religious and moral practices.
    • 弘济 - Refers to great benefit or salvation, emphasizing the ultimate goal of virtuous actions.
    • 文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Han, a historical figure known for his benevolent rule.
    • 抚几嗟叹 - Refers to a gesture of deep reflection and sorrow, often associated with moments of profound insight.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks, emphasizing their role in religious practice.
    • 至道 - Refers to the highest path or ultimate truth, often associated with spiritual enlightenment.
    • 虚説 - Refers to empty or superficial talk, suggesting a lack of substance.
    • 章句 - Refers to verses or passages, often associated with religious texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 文帝 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Han, a historical figure known for his benevolent rule.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks, emphasizing their role in religious practice.
    • 至道 - Refers to the highest path or ultimate truth, often associated with spiritual enlightenment.
    • 虚説 - Refers to empty or superficial talk, suggesting a lack of substance.
    • 章句 - Refers to verses or passages, often associated with religious texts.

    His teachings do not come from the inner chambers, and his words do not reach the servants and maids. If he does not restrain himself, endure hardship, practice goodness, and uphold precepts, how can he use his heart? Emperors regard the four seas as their home and the myriad people as their children. If he speaks good words, the scholars and commoners will all be pleased; if he spreads one good deed, the people and gods will be harmonized. Clear punishment does not shorten their lives, and light labor does not exhaust their strength. Discriminate between musical tones and set the seasons; if the musical tones are discriminated, then the wind and rain will be harmonized; if commands are timely, then the cold and heat will be regulated. Knowing the hunger of the people is how one achieves the absence of hunger; knowing the cold of the people is how one achieves the absence of cold. Thus, holding the fast is also great. Not killing is a great multitude. How can it be that by missing one meal and saving one bird's life, one achieves great benefit? Emperor Wen patted the table and sighed, praising the good and the kind for a long time before saying: Ordinary people are confused by distant principles, and Buddhist monks are stuck in near teachings. Those confused by distant principles say the highest path is empty talk; those stuck in near teachings are bound by verses. Such as this gentleman truly opens enlightenment and clarity, capable of speaking of the relationship between heaven and humanity. [81]

    元相国谒李贺
    　　元和中进士李贺善为歌篇韩文公深所知重于缙绅之间每加延誉由此声华借甚时元相国稹年老以明经擢第亦攻篇什常愿交结贺一日执贽造门贺览刺不容遽令仆者谓曰明经擢第何事来看李贺相国无复致情慙愤而退其后左拾遗制策登科日当要路及为礼部郎中因议贺祖祢讳晋不合应进士举亦以轻薄时軰所排遂成轗轲文公惜其才为着讳辩录明之然竟不
    成事自大中咸通之后每岁试春官者千余人其间章句有闻亹亹不絶如何植李玫皇甫松李孺犀梁望毛涛贝庥来鹄贾随以文章着美温庭筠郑渎何防周钤宋耘沈驾周繁以词赋标名贾岛平曾李陶刘得仁喻坦之张乔剧燕许琳陈觉以律诗流传张维皇甫川郭鄩刘延晖以古风擅价皆苦心文华厄于一第然其间数公丽藻英词播于海内其虚薄叨聨名级者又不可同年而语矣

    　　剧谈录卷下 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元和中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang (806–820 AD).
    • 进士 - Refers to those who passed the highest level of the imperial examinations.
    • 善为歌篇 - Means 'skilled in composing songs or poems.'
    • 韩文公深所知重 - Means 'highly esteemed and known by Han Wengong (Han Yu), a prominent scholar and official.'
    • 缙绅之间 - Refers to the scholarly and official class.
    • 每加延誉 - Means 'frequently praised and recommended.'
    • 声华借甚 - Means 'had great fame and reputation.'
    • 明经擢第 - Refers to passing the Mingjing examination, a lower-level imperial exam.
    • 攻篇什 - Means 'engaged in writing poetry.'
    • 愿交结贺 - Means 'wished to form a friendship with Rui.'
    • 执贽造门 - Means 'carrying a gift to visit someone's door.'
    • 览刺不容遽令仆者谓曰 - Means 'after looking at the visitor's card, he quickly ordered his servant to say.'
    • 明经擢第何事来看李贺 - Means 'What business does a person who passed the Mingjing examination have coming to see Li He?'
    • 相国无复致情 - Means 'the chancellor no longer cares about it.'
    • 惭愤而退 - Means 'left feeling ashamed and angry.'
    • 左拾遗制策登科 - Refers to being selected for the imperial examination as a Left Shiyi (a minor official).
    • 日当要路 - Means 'held important positions.'
    • 礼部郎中 - Refers to a mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites.
    • 议贺祖祢讳晋不合应进士举 - Means 'argued that Li He's ancestor's name, Jin, was taboo and thus he should not be allowed to take the Jinshi examination.'
    • 以轻薄时軰所排 - Means 'because he was considered light and frivolous, he was criticized by his contemporaries.'
    • 遂成轗轲 - Means 'thus encountered setbacks and difficulties.'
    • 惜其才 - Means 'regretted his talent.'
    • 为着讳辩录明之 - Means 'wrote a record of his argument to clarify the matter.'
    • 然竟不成事 - Means 'but ultimately did not succeed.'
    • 大中咸通之后 - Refers to the period after the reigns of Emperors Wuzong (840–846 AD) and Xuanzong (847–859 AD).
    • 每岁试春官者千余人 - Means 'each year, more than a thousand people took the Spring Officials' examination.'
    • 其间章句有闻 - Means 'among them, some were known for their verses.'
    • 亹亹不絶 - Means 'continued without interruption.'
    • 如何植李玫皇甫松李孺犀梁望毛涛贝庥来鹄贾随以文章着美 - Lists several poets and their works, which were well-known and appreciated.
    • 温庭筠郑渎何防周钤宋耘沈驾周繁以词赋标名 - Lists several poets and their works, which were famous for their lyrics and compositions.
    • 贾岛平曾李陶刘得仁喻坦之张乔剧燕许琳陈觉以律诗流传 - Lists several poets and their works, which were famous for their regulated verse.
    • 张维皇甫川郭鄩刘延晖以古风擅价 - Lists several poets and their works, which were famous for their ancient style.
    • 皆苦心文华厄于一第 - Means 'all of them worked hard on their literary skills but were hindered by failing to pass the examination.'
    • 然其间数公丽藻英词播于海内 - Means 'however, among them, several had beautiful and excellent works that spread throughout the country.'
    • 其虚薄叨聨名级者又不可同年而语矣 - Means 'those who were merely fortunate to be included in the list cannot be compared to the truly talented ones.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元和中 - The reign of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 韩文公 - Han Yu, a famous scholar and official.
    • 明经擢第 - Passing the Mingjing examination.
    • 左拾遗 - A minor official responsible for remonstrating with the emperor.
    • 制策登科 - Being selected for the imperial examination.
    • 礼部郎中 - A mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites.
    • 祖祢讳 - Ancestor's name taboo, a significant issue in Chinese culture.
    • 轻薄时軰 - Contemporary critics who considered Li He to be light and frivolous.
    • 大中咸通之后 - The reigns of Emperors Wuzong and Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 春官 - The Ministry of Rites, responsible for the imperial examinations.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元和 - The reign period of Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 李贺 - Li He, a famous poet.
    • 韩文公 - Han Yu, a famous scholar and official.
    • 明经 - The Mingjing examination.
    • 左拾遗 - A minor official.
    • 制策 - Imperial examination.
    • 礼部郎中 - A mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites.
    • 祖祢讳 - Ancestor's name taboo.
    • 大中 - The reign period of Emperor Wuzong of Tang.
    • 咸通 - The reign period of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 春官 - The Ministry of Rites.
    • 李玫 - A poet.
    • 皇甫松 - A poet.
    • 李孺犀 - A poet.
    • 梁望 - A poet.
    • 毛涛 - A poet.
    • 贝庥 - A poet.
    • 来鹄 - A poet.
    • 贾随 - A poet.
    • 温庭筠 - A poet.
    • 郑渎 - A poet.
    • 何防 - A poet.
    • 周钤 - A poet.
    • 宋耘 - A poet.
    • 沈驾 - A poet.
    • 周繁 - A poet.
    • 贾岛 - A poet.
    • 平曾 - A poet.
    • 李陶 - A poet.
    • 刘得仁 - A poet.
    • 喻坦之 - A poet.
    • 张乔 - A poet.
    • 剧燕 - A poet.
    • 许琳 - A poet.
    • 陈觉 - A poet.
    • 张维 - A poet.
    • 皇甫川 - A poet.
    • 郭鄩 - A poet.
    • 刘延晖 - A poet.

    During the Yuanhe period, the Jinshi Li He was skilled in composing songs and poems, and was highly esteemed and known by Han Wengong (Han Yu) among the scholarly and official class. He was frequently praised and recommended, and thus had great fame and reputation. At the time, Chancellor Yuan Zhen, who was older and had passed the Mingjing examination, also engaged in writing poetry and wished to form a friendship with Li He. One day, Yuan Zhen carried a gift to visit Li He's door. After looking at the visitor's card, Li He quickly ordered his servant to say, "What business does a person who passed the Mingjing examination have coming to see Li He?" The chancellor no longer cared about it, and left feeling ashamed and angry. Later, Yuan Zhen, who was selected for the imperial examination as a Left Shiyi and held important positions, became a mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites. He argued that Li He's ancestor's name, Jin, was taboo and thus he should not be allowed to take the Jinshi examination. This was also because Li He was considered light and frivolous and was criticized by his contemporaries, thus encountering setbacks and difficulties. Han Wengong regretted his talent and wrote a record of his argument to clarify the matter, but ultimately did not succeed.

    After the reigns of Emperors Wuzong and Xuanzong of Tang, each year, more than a thousand people took the Spring Officials' examination. Among them, some were known for their verses, continuing without interruption. How about Li Mei, Huangfu Song, Li Ruxi, Liang Wang, Mao Tao, Bei Xiu, Lai Hu, Jia Sui, Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan, Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue, Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui, whose works were well-known and appreciated. Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan were famous for their lyrics and compositions. Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue were famous for their regulated verse. Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui were famous for their ancient style. All of them worked hard on their literary skills but were hindered by failing to pass the examination. However, among them, several had beautiful and excellent works that spread throughout the country. Those who were merely fortunate to be included in the list cannot be compared to the truly talented ones. [82]

    元相国谒李贺
    　　元和中进士李贺善为歌篇韩文公深所知重于缙绅之间每加延誉由此声华借甚时元相国稹年老以明经擢第亦攻篇什常愿交结贺一日执贽造门贺览刺不容遽令仆者谓曰明经擢第何事来看李贺相国无复致情慙愤而退其后左拾遗制策登科日当要路及为礼部郎中因议贺祖祢讳晋不合应进士举亦以轻薄时軰所排遂成轗轲文公惜其才为着讳辩录明之然竟不
    成事自大中咸通之后每岁试春官者千余人其间章句有闻亹亹不絶如何植李玫皇甫松李孺犀梁望毛涛贝庥来鹄贾随以文章着美温庭筠郑渎何防周钤宋耘沈驾周繁以词赋标名贾岛平曾李陶刘得仁喻坦之张乔剧燕许琳陈觉以律诗流传张维皇甫川郭鄩刘延晖以古风擅价皆苦心文华厄于一第然其间数公丽藻英词播于海内其虚薄叨聨名级者又不可同年而语矣

    　　剧谈录卷下

    During the Yuanhe period, the Jinshi Li He was skilled in composing songs and poems, and was highly esteemed and known by Han Wengong (Han Yu) among the scholarly and official class. He was frequently praised and recommended, and thus had great fame and reputation. At the time, Chancellor Yuan Zhen, who was older and had passed the Mingjing examination, also engaged in writing poetry and wished to form a friendship with Li He. One day, Yuan Zhen carried a gift to visit Li He's door. After looking at the visitor's card, Li He quickly ordered his servant to say, "What business does a person who passed the Mingjing examination have coming to see Li He?" The chancellor no longer cared about it, and left feeling ashamed and angry. Later, Yuan Zhen, who was selected for the imperial examination as a Left Shiyi and held important positions, became a mid-level official in the Ministry of Rites. He argued that Li He's ancestor's name, Jin, was taboo and thus he should not be allowed to take the Jinshi examination. This was also because Li He was considered light and frivolous and was criticized by his contemporaries, thus encountering setbacks and difficulties. Han Wengong regretted his talent and wrote a record of his argument to clarify the matter, but ultimately did not succeed.

    After the reigns of Emperors Wuzong and Xuanzong of Tang, each year, more than a thousand people took the Spring Officials' examination. Among them, some were known for their verses, continuing without interruption. How about Li Mei, Huangfu Song, Li Ruxi, Liang Wang, Mao Tao, Bei Xiu, Lai Hu, Jia Sui, Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan, Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue, Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui, whose works were well-known and appreciated. Wen Tingyun, Zheng Du, He Fang, Zhou Qian, Song Yun, Shen Jia, Zhou Fan were famous for their lyrics and compositions. Jia Dao, Ping Ceng, Li Tao, Liu Deren, Yu Tan Zhi, Zhang Qiao, Ju Yan, Xu Lin, Chen Jue were famous for their regulated verse. Zhang Wei, Huangfu Chuan, Guo Xun, and Liu Yanhui were famous for their ancient style. All of them worked hard on their literary skills but were hindered by failing to pass the examination. However, among them, several had beautiful and excellent works that spread throughout the country. Those who were merely fortunate to be included in the list cannot be compared to the truly talented ones.
